title: EXPOSÉ IX NÉRON MODELS AND MONODROMY

pages.title: EXPOSÉ IX NÉRON MODELS AND MONODROMY

blocks.html:
EXPOSÉ IX
NÉRON MODELS AND MONODROMYby A. Grothendieck

(with an appendix by M. Raynaud)
CONTENTS
0. Introduction

1. Néron model of abelian schemes: notations; the canonical
pairing \(\Phi_o\times\Phi'_o\longrightarrow(\mathbb Q/\mathbb Z)_k\), and the canonical biextension \(W^o\)
of \((A^o,A'{}^o)\)

2. Fixed part and toric part of \(T_\ell(A_K)\). Criteria for good reduction.
Orthogonality theorem for \(\ell\ne p\)
3. Case of semi-stable reduction. The semi-stable reduction theorem
4. Application to a conjecture of Serre–Tate and to the conductor

5. The orthogonality theorem in the case \(\ell=p\) (semi-stable case)
Duality of the abelian schemes \(B_o\) and \(B'_o\). Characterization of the
fixed part. Criteria for good reduction, for essential good reduction
and for semi-stable reduction

6. Remarks on the construction of the toric part and the fixed
part of \(T_p(A)\) in the case of non-semi-stable reduction

7. The Raynaud extension \(G^{\natural}\) over \(S\) attached to the abelian scheme \(A_K\) with
semi-stable reduction. Duality of the abelian schemes \(B,B'\) over \(S\)

8. The Raynaud extension \(A_K^{\natural o}\) in the semi-stable case, and the
ind-group \(A_K^{\natural}\)
9. Definition of the monodromy pairing \(M_\ell\otimes M'_\ell\longrightarrow\mathbb Z_{\ell S}\)

10. Properties of the monodromy pairing. Integrality
and positivity theorem

11. Connected components of the Néron model: duality relation,
asymptotic behavior
12. Néron model of a jacobian and Picard–Lefschetz formula
13. Links with the transcendental theory: complex analytic case

13.1. Reminders on analytic groups over \(S\)
13.2. Formal completion along a fiber
13.3. The analytic RAYNAUD extension
13.4. Interpretation in terms of the "mixed Hodge structures"
of DELIGNE
13.5. Case of a relatively algebraic situation

14. Links with the transcendental theory: rigid-analytic case
The canonical homomorphism \(M_K\longrightarrow A_K^{\natural o}\).
0. Introduction

0.1. In the present exposé, we apply the results of the preceding exposés to the study of the Néron model (1.1) \(A\) of an abelian scheme \(A_K\) defined over the function field \(K\) of the henselian trait \(S\). We will see in fact that the most important properties of the Néron model can be interpreted in terms of the action of the local monodromy group \(I\) on the Tate module \(T_\ell(A_K(\overline K))\) (where \(\ell\) is a prime number \(\ne p\)). Since the dual of the latter is interpreted as the \(\ell\)-adic cohomology group \(H^1(A_{\overline K},\mathbb Z_\ell)\), and since for every projective and smooth scheme \(X\) over \(K\), \(H^1(X_{\overline K},\mathbb Z_\ell)\) is identified with \(H^1(A_{\overline K},\mathbb Z_\ell)\), where \(A_{\overline K}\) is the Albanese variety of \(X_{\overline K}\), we see therefore that the present exposé can also be considered as a detailed study of the phenomena of local monodromy for the \(H^1\) \(\ell\)-adic (or better still, for the "motivic" \(H^1\)) of projective and smooth varieties over \(K\). From this point of view, it seems clear that the principal results of the present exposé are destined to be subsumed in a "Néron theory" for motives of arbitrary weight, i.e. for \(H^i\) (\(\ell\)-adic, or De Rham, or Hodge etc) with \(i\) arbitrary, which one is only beginning to glimpse at the present time. (Cf. on this subject [9], and more particularly the conjectures of DELIGNE 9.8 to 9.13 of the cited report.)

0.2. The most important result of the present exposé is the "semi-stable reduction theorem" 3.6, asserting that after a finite separable extension \(K'\) of \(K\), the Néron model of \(A_{K'}\) has a special fiber whose identity component is an extension of an abelian scheme by a torus. This result had been conjectured by J.P. SERRE in 1964, and the principle of the proof we give of it here (*) goes back to the same year. It appears (3.5) that this property of "semi-stable reduction" is equivalent to the fact that the action of the monodromy group \(I\) on \(T_\ell(A_K(\overline K))\) is "essentially unipotent of level 2" (\(\ell\) being a fixed prime number distinct from the residue characteristic \(p\) of \(S\)), and in this form was proved in terms of \(H^1\) \(\ell\)-adic in exposé III as a particular case of the "monodromy theorem", using the resolution of singularities of excellent schemes of dimension 2 (due to S. ABHYANKAR). To establish the equivalence of the two properties, we will need on the other hand an "orthogonality theorem" 2.4, due to IGUSA [11] in the particular case where \(A_K\) is the jacobian of a smooth curve \(X_K\) over \(K\), generic fiber of a regular and connected projective scheme \(X\) over \(S\) whose geometric special fiber has as its only singular points ordinary quadratic singular points. This is moreover a case of semi-stable reduction, which would not suffice for our purpose; it is above all to establish the orthogonality theorem in its general form that we use the convenient tool furnished by the theory of biextensions, developed in the two preceding exposés. Let us point out moreover that a very different proof of the semi-stable reduction theorem, not using resolution of singularities, has been found by D. MUMFORD, using his theory of \(\theta\) functions (unpublished); unfortunately, he is obliged to restrict himself to the case of a residue characteristic \(\ne2\).

0.3. The semi-stable reduction theorem is the principal tool used in the later paragraphs for the study of Néron models, which we undertake here from the "algebraic" point of view. It seems equally indispensable in the study of abelian varieties over \(K\) from the "rigid-analytic" point of view or that of "\(\theta\) functions" (these terms being synonymous, it seems, when it is a question of abelian varieties over complete valued fields ...), as it has been developed recently by D. MUMFORD and M. RAYNAUD (following works of TATE and MORIKAWA). (We will say a few words in § 13, 14 on the links between the algebraic and transcendental points of view.) Finally, this same theorem is a key ingredient of the proof of the "semi-stable reduction theorem for algebraic curves" of ARTIN–DELIGNE–MUMFORD [3] (*), of which we have already had to make use in the study of the monodromy action on the fundamental group ("essentially tame action theorem" V ), and which is used again in the proof of the integrality and positivity theorem 10.4 in the present exposé (**).

0.4. The present exposé is distinguished from the other exposés of the Seminar by the fact that in the study of the TATE module \(T_\ell(A_K)\), we have systematically taken into account also the case where \(\ell\) is equal to the residue characteristic \(p\). It turns out that the language of Barsotti–Tate groups [33] (*) makes it possible to extend to this case all the most important phenomena established for the case \(\ell\ne p\), subject however, at times, to having available the fundamental theorem of TATE [33 th. 4], established for the moment only in the case where \(K\) is of characteristic zero. Of course, in the latter case the pro-\(p\)-group of Barsotti–Tate \(T_p(A_K)\) is again interpreted in terms of the action of the monodromy group \(I\) on the \(p\)-adic module \(T_p(A_K(\overline K))\), but one should beware that most of the statements that this superficial analogy with the case \(\ell\ne p\) might suggest are grossly false. (Thus, it is no longer true that the action of the monodromy group is essentially unipotent for \(\ell=p\).) But using more judicious translations (such as for example 5.13 below), it is clear already that the study of the pro-\(p\)-groups of Barsotti–Tate defined by geometric means over the function field of a trait with residue characteristic \(p>0\), whether or not it is of unequal characteristics, must be considered as forming part of the theory of "local monodromy", where these groups play the role of "\(p\)-adic local systems" over \(\operatorname{Spec}(K)\). In fact, it is essentially the "local systems" which arise in the study, from the "\(p\)-adic" point of view, of the \(H^1\) of projective and smooth varieties over \(K\). For the study of the higher \(H^i\) from the \(p\)-adic point of view, which remains to be done, it appears that it will be necessary to suitably enlarge the category of pro-\(p\)-groups of Barsotti–Tate over \(K\) resp. over \(S\), drawing inspiration from the points of view furnished by "crystalline cohomology".

0.5. The exposé given here, very close to the oral exposés up to § 4, is distinctly more detailed and complete from the following paragraphs on, which had been only partially sketched. It will not be used in the later exposés. On the other hand, certain results of the present exposé (10.4 and 12.5) use the Picard–Lefschetz formula, which will be established only later.
LEITFADEN
\[ \xymatrix@R=1.2em@C=2.2em{ &1\ar[d]&\\ &2\ar[d]&\\ 4\ar[ur]&3\ar[d]&\\ 6\ar[ur]&5\ar[d]\ar[dr]\ar[rr]&&9\ar[d]\\ &8\ar[d]\ar[ur]&7\ar[dl]\ar[d]\ar[dll]&10\ar[dl]\ar[d]\ar[dr]\\ &13&14&11&12 } \]

footnotes.html: It is developed in a letter from the lecturer to J.P. SERRE of 30.10.64.

The proof of this theorem had been given by P. DELIGNE in
the oral Seminar, but it was not judged useful to reproduce it
in the texts of the Seminar.

There exists another proof of this last theorem, via the
rigid-analytic theory, not using the semi-stable reduction
theorem.
or "\(p\)-divisible groups" in the terminology of TATE.

pages.title: 1. Néron model of abelian schemes: notations; the canonical pairing \(\Phi_o\times\Phi'_o\longrightarrow(\mathbb Q/\mathbb Z)_k\), and the canonical biextension \(W^o\) of \((A^o,A'{}^o)\).

blocks.html:
1. Néron model of abelian schemes: notations; the canonical
pairing \(\Phi_o\times\Phi'_o\longrightarrow(\mathbb Q/\mathbb Z)_k\), and the canonical biextension \(W^o\) of \((A^o,A'{}^o)\).

1.0. In all the sequel of the present exposé, we suppose fixed a trait \(S\), for which we use our usual notations \(s,\eta,k,K\) etc. of I 1. We suppose given an abelian scheme \(A_K\) over \(K\), whose dual abelian scheme [5] will be denoted \(A'_K\). The duality between \(A_K\) and \(A'_K\) is given by a divisorial correspondence \(W_K\) on \((A_K,A'_K)\), i.e. an invertible Module on \(A_K\times A'_K\), birigidified relatively to the unit sections of \(A_K,A'_K\), which is determined up to unique isomorphism by its class (cf. VIII 3.1)
(1.0.1) \[w_K\in\operatorname{Biext}^1(A_K,A'_K;G_{mK}).\]
An important role will be played by the corresponding pairing (VIII 2.2)
(1.0.2) \[\varphi:T_\ell(A_K)\times T_\ell(A'_K)\longrightarrow \mathbb Z_\ell(1)_K \mathrel{\mathop=^{\mathrm{dfn}}}T_\ell(G_{mK}),\]
where \(\ell\) is an arbitrary prime number. When \(\ell\ne p=\operatorname{car}.k\), the datum of \(\varphi\) is equivalent to that of a pairing of free \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-modules of finite type
(1.0.3) \[\varphi:T_\ell(A_K(\overline K))\times T_\ell(A'_K(\overline K))\longrightarrow T_\ell(\overline K^*),\]

compatible with the action of \[\pi=\operatorname{Gal}(\overline K/K)\] on the three modules in play, \(\overline K\) being a separable closure of \(K\). It is known (VIII 3.2) that the pairing in question is a perfect duality, i.e. that it identifies each of the modules \(T_\ell(A_K(\overline K)),T_\ell(A'_K(\overline K))\) with the "dual" of the other with values in the invertible module \(\mathbb Z_\ell(1)=T_\ell(\overline K^*)\).
We will sometimes suppose chosen a polarization of \(A_K\), which is thus an element
(1.0.4) \[\xi\in\operatorname{NS}(A_K/K)=\operatorname{NS}_{A_K/K}(K),\]
where \[\operatorname{NS}_{A_K/K}\mathrel{\mathop=^{\mathrm{dfn}}} \operatorname{Pic}_{A_K/K}/(\operatorname{Pic}^o_{A_K/K}=A'_K).\] It defines in the usual way a homomorphism
(1.0.5) \[\psi_\xi:A_K\longrightarrow A'_K,\]
whence a homomorphism
(1.0.6) \[T_\ell(\psi_\xi)\text{ or }\psi_\xi:T_\ell(A_K)\longrightarrow T_\ell(A'_K),\]
and a bilinear form \(\varphi_\xi\)
(1.0.7) \[\varphi_\xi(x,y)=\varphi(x,\psi_\xi(y)):T_\ell(A_K)\times T_\ell(A_K)\longrightarrow\mathbb Z_\ell(1)_K,\]
which can also be interpreted for \(\ell\ne p\) as a pairing of free \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-modules of finite type with operators:
(1.0.8) \[\varphi_\xi:T_\ell(A_K(\overline K))\times T_\ell(A_K(\overline K))\longrightarrow T_\ell(\overline K^*).\]

These constructions make sense for every element of the Néron–Severi group of \(A_K\), and furnish an alternating form \(\varphi_\xi\) (1.0.7) or (1.0.8) (VIII 2.2.11). The fact that \(\xi\) is a polarization implies that \(\psi_\xi\) in (1.0.5) is an isogeny, or what amounts to the same since \(\dim A_K=\dim A'_K\), an epimorphism of Groups, or again that \(T_\ell(\psi_\xi)\) in (1.0.6) is injective (or again, has finite cokernel), or finally that the form \(\varphi_\xi\) of (1.0.7) is separating.
1.1. Recall that there exists over \(S\) a smooth scheme \(A\), called the Néron model of \(A_K\), which represents the functor
(1.1.1) \[S'\longmapsto\operatorname{Hom}(S'_\eta,A_K)\]
on the category of smooth \(S\)-schemes. We thus have by definition an isomorphism of functors in \(S'\), a relative scheme smooth over \(S\):
(1.1.2) \[\operatorname{Hom}_S(S',A)\xrightarrow{\sim}\operatorname{Hom}_K(S'_\eta,A_K).\]

The construction of \(A\) is given by NERON [19] when the residue field \(k\) of \(S\) is perfect, and was extended by RAYNAUD to the general case in the course of an unwritten seminar which he gave at the IHES in 1966/67 (cf. [21] for the statement of the principal results obtained by RAYNAUD, completing the results of NERON of loc. cit.). We will admit here this existence result, as well as the fact that \(A\) is a quasi-projective scheme over \(S\). Applying (1.1.2) for \(S'\) smooth over \(\eta\), we see moreover that the following canonical morphism is an isomorphism
(1.1.3) \[A\times_S\operatorname{Spec}(K)\xrightarrow{\sim}A_K,\]

which justifies the pair of notations \(A,A_K\). We will also write, should the occasion arise, \(A_\eta\) instead of \(A_K\). Using (1.1.3) as an identification isomorphism, the canonical bijection (1.1.2) is none other than the map of restriction to the generic fibers.

Since the functor (1.1.1) evidently has a structure of commutative Group, it follows that the Néron model \(A\) which represents it has itself a structure of commutative group scheme smooth over \(S\). The sequel of this exposé is devoted to the study of this group scheme.
An important invariant, deduced from the consideration of the Néron model, is evidently the commutative group scheme, smooth and of finite type over \(k\):
(1.1.4) \[A_o=A\times_S s=A_k.\]
Following the general notations, we denote by \(A_o^o\) its identity component, and we also consider the group scheme
of connected components of \(A_o\), which is a group scheme finite and étale over \(k\). In terms of the separable closure \(\overline k\) of \(k\), it is thus determined by the finite ordinary group
(1.1.6) \[\Phi_o(\overline k),\]
with the natural operations of \[\pi_o=\operatorname{Gal}(\overline k/k)\] on this group.
At the same time as the Néron model \(A\) of \(A_K\), we will study
(1.1.7) \[A'=\text{Néron model of }A'_K,\]
which is also a commutative group scheme, smooth and of finite type over \(S\). We will denote by
the group of connected components of its special fiber \(A'_o\).
1.2. Let us apply VIII 7.1 b) to the pair \((A,A')\) and to the biextension \(W_K\) of (1.0.1) \((A_K,A'_K)\) by \(G_{mK}\). We thus find a canonical pairing
(1.2.1) \[\Phi_o\times\Phi'_o\longrightarrow(\mathbb Q/\mathbb Z)_k,\]
i.e. a pairing of \(\pi_o\)-modules
(1.2.2) \[\Phi_o(\overline k)\times\Phi'_o(\overline k)\longrightarrow\mathbb Q/\mathbb Z.\]

This pairing appears here as the obstruction to the prolongation of \(W_K\) to a biextension \(W\) of \((A,A')\) by \(G_{mS}\), and the significance of this point of view is made explicit in VIII 7.3.2. Let us point out right away, concerning this pairing, the

blocks.title: Conjecture 1.3.
blocks.html:
Conjecture 1.3.

The pairing (1.2.1) is a perfect duality, i.e. identifies each of the groups of connected components \(\Phi_o\) and \(\Phi'_o\) with the dual with values in \((\mathbb Q/\mathbb Z)_k\) of the other.

1.3.1. Let us point out that it follows from the commutation of the formation of (1.2.1) with formally unramified base changes (VIII 7.3.5.3), and from the analogous property for the formation of Néron models, that it would suffice to prove 1.3 in the case where \(S\) is complete with separably closed residue field. The conjecture has been proved by ARTIN and MAZUR in the case where \(A_K\) is the jacobian of a proper and smooth curve over \(K\), by using their general theory (unpublished) of the autoduality of the relative jacobian (in the sense of derived categories) of a proper scheme in curves over \(S\); they proved the same conjecture 1.3 in the case where the residue field is finite, using the duality theory of TATE [32]. We will see also in 11.4 and 11.5 (modulo some compatibility verifications) that 1.3 is true for the \(\ell\)-primary components for \(\ell\ne p\), and that 1.3 is true in the case of "semi-stable reduction". These results make 1.3 extremely plausible. Moreover 1.3 can also be considered as a by-product of a general duality theory for group schemes over the fraction fields of complete discrete valuation rings, formally analogous to that of SGA 5 I, which remains heuristic at the present time, and which would also contain local class field theory in the form given to it by SERRE [27].

1.3.2. The validity of 1.3 for a given abelian scheme \(A_K\) amounts to saying that the pairing (1.2.1) is separating on the left and on the right. Moreover the fact that it is separating on the left means, by virtue of VIII 7.3.2, that the only open subgroup scheme \(V\) of \(A\) such that \(W_K\) can be prolonged to a biextension of \((V,A')\) by \(G_{mS}\) is the identity component \(A^o\) of \(A\). One has a symmetric interpretation for the condition that (1.2.1) be separating on the right.
1.4. In a general way, if
(1.4.1) \[U\subset A\quad\hbox{et}\quad U'\subset A'\]
are open subgroup schemes such that \(W_K\) prolongs to a biextension of \((U,U')\) by \(G_{mS}\) (which, by virtue of VIII 7.3.2, is made explicit by the condition that the subgroups
(1.4.2) \[\Psi_o=U_o/U_o^o\subset\Phi_o,\qquad \Psi'_o=U'_o/U'{}^o_o\subset\Phi'_o\]

of \(\Phi_o\) and of \(\Phi'_o\) defined by \(U\) and \(U'\) are orthogonal to one another for the pairing (1.2.1)), we denote by \(W\) this prolongation (unique up to unique isomorphism) of \(W_K\). It is determined up to unique isomorphism by its class
(1.4.3) \[w\in\operatorname{Biext}^1(U,U';G_{mS}),\]
itself characterized by the condition that its image in \(\operatorname{Biext}^1(A_K,A'_K;G_{mK})\) be \(w_K\) of (1.0.1).

One can take for example for \(U\) and \(U'\) the identity components \(A^o\) and \(A'{}^o\) of \(A\) and \(A'\). One thus finds a canonical biextension \(W^o\) or \(W\) of \((A^o,A'{}^o)\) by \(G_{mS}\), giving rise to a class
(1.4.4) \[w=w^o\in\operatorname{Biext}^1(A^o,A'{}^o;G_{mS}),\]
with which we will principally work. Evidently the classes \(w\) (1.4.3) relative to various pairs \((U,U')\) all have \(w^o\) (1.4.4) as restriction to \((A^o,A'{}^o)\).

blocks.title: Proposition 1.5.
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.5.

Consider the special fiber \(W_s\) of the canonical biextension (1.4.4) \(W\) of \((A^o,A'{}^o)\) by \(G_{mS}\), so that \(W_s\) is a biextension of \((A_o^o,A'{}^o_o)\) by \(G_{mk}\). The latter is separating (VIII 4.11).
Let us indeed choose an ample invertible Module \(L\) on \(A^o\). Then \(L_\eta\) defines a homomorphism (cf. 1.0.5)
\[A_K\longrightarrow A'_K,\]
which prolongs to a homomorphism \(A\longrightarrow A'\), whence
\[\psi:A^o\longrightarrow A'{}^o,\]

and we can consider the biextension \(W_L\) of \((A^o,A^o)\) by \(G_{mS}\), the inverse image of \(W\) by \((\operatorname{id}_{A^o},\psi)\). The restriction of \(W_L\) to the generic fibers is then none other than the biextension \(\delta(L_\eta)\) of (VIII 4.12), hence by virtue of VIII 7.4, \(W_L\) is associated to the birigidified Module \(\delta(L)\), and consequently \((W_L)_s\) is defined by \(\delta(L_s)\). On the other hand, it can also be described as the inverse image of the biextension \(W_s\) by the morphisms
\[\operatorname{id}_{A_o^o}:A_o^o\longrightarrow A_o^o,\qquad \psi_o:A_o^o\longrightarrow A'{}^o_o.\]

Since \(L\) and hence \(L_s\) is ample, it follows from VIII 4.13 that \((W_L)_s\) is separating, and in particular separating on the left, and a fortiori \(W_s\) is therefore separating on the left. By symmetry, \(W_s\) is also separating on the right, which proves 1.5.
Let us note that we will considerably sharpen 1.5 in the case where \(A_o^o\) is of unipotent rank zero (5.4).

pages.title: 2. Fixed part and toric part of \(T_\ell(A_K)\). Criteria for good reduction. Orthogonality theorem for \(\ell\ne p\)

blocks.html:
2. Fixed part and toric part of \(T_\ell(A_K)\). Criteria for good reduction.
Orthogonality theorem for \(\ell\ne p\)
2.1. Like every commutative algebraic group over \(k\), \(A_o^o\) admits a largest subtorus, whose formation commutes with any extension of the base field [SGA 3 XVII 7.2.1, XII 1.12]:
(2.1.1) \[T_o\subset A_o^o.\]

When \(k\) is perfect, \(T_o\) is characterized by the condition that \(A_o^o/T_o\) be an extension of an abelian scheme \(B_o\) over \(k\) by a unipotent group (smooth and connected) [25], which necessarily admits a composition series with quotients all isomorphic to \(G_{ak}\) (SGA 3 XVII 4.1.3)
(2.1.2) \[0\longrightarrow U_o\longrightarrow A_o^o/T_o\longrightarrow B_o\longrightarrow0.\]
In any case, \(k\) perfect or not, if \(A_o^o\) is an extension of an abelian scheme \(B_o\) by an affine smooth and connected scheme \(L_o\):
(2.1.3) \[0\longrightarrow L_o\longrightarrow A_o^o\longrightarrow B_o\longrightarrow0,\]
we know that this extension structure is unique [25], and that \(T_o\) is then \(\subset L_o\), hence \(T_o\) is the largest subtorus of \(L_o\), so that we have an exact sequence (2.1.2), with

2.1.5. Let us point out that \(A_o^o\) is of unipotent rank zero (i.e. that, over an algebraic closure of \(k\), it admits no subgroup isomorphic to \(G_a\)) if and only if \(A_o^o/T_o\) is an abelian scheme \(B_o\), so that we will then be under the preceding conditions, with \(U_o=0\).
If \(\ell\) is a prime number distinct from the characteristic of \(k\), so that \(A_o^o\) is \(\ell\)-divisible, the inclusion (2.1.1) gives rise to an exact sequence of pro-group schemes:
(2.1.6) \[ \xymatrix{ 0\ar[r]&T_\ell(T_o)\ar[r]&T_\ell(A_o^o)\ar[r]&T_\ell(A_o^o/T_o)\ar[r]&0\\ &&T_\ell(A_o)\ar[u]^{?}&T_\ell(A_o/T_o)\ar[u]_{?} } \]

whose terms can also be interpreted as constant twisted free \(\ell\)-adic sheaves; when one has available an exact sequence (2.1.2), for example \(k\) perfect or \(A_o^o\) of unipotent rank zero, we will have
(2.1.7) \[T_\ell(A_o^o/T_o)\xrightarrow{\sim}T_\ell(B_o),\]
so that the exact sequence (2.1.5) is then written
(2.1.8) \[0\longrightarrow T_\ell(T_o)\longrightarrow T_\ell(A_o^o)\longrightarrow T_\ell(B_o)\longrightarrow0.\]
Let us set
(2.1.9) \[n=\dim A_K=\dim A_o,\quad \mu=\dim T_o,\quad \lambda=\dim U_o,\quad \alpha=\dim B_o,\]
so that we have
(2.1.10) \[n=\mu+\lambda+\alpha,\]

(2.1.11) \[ \left\{ \begin{aligned} \operatorname{rang}T_\ell(T_o)&=\mu,\quad \operatorname{rang}T_\ell(A_o^o/T_o)=\operatorname{rang}T_\ell(B_o)=2\alpha,\\ \operatorname{rang}T_\ell(A_o^o)&=\mu+2\alpha, \end{aligned} \right. \]
The second relation being a well-known theorem of Weil [5] [15].

2.1.12. The integers \(n\), \(\mu\), \(\lambda\), \(\alpha\) are defined manifestly without even supposing that one has available an exact sequence (2.1.2), by passing to an algebraic closure of \(k\); they are known respectively under the name of dimension of \(A_o\), reductive rank of \(A_o\) = dimension of \(T_o\), unipotent rank of \(A_o\), abelian rank of \(A_o\). They are still related by the relations (2.1.10) and (2.1.11) (omitting in the latter the term rank \(T_\ell(B_o)\),...).

2.1.13. When \(k\) is of characteristic \(p>0\), and \(A_o^o\) is \(p\)-divisible, or what amounts to the same, of unipotent rank zero (cf. 2.1.5), then it is appropriate to consider also the pro-group \(T_p(A_o^o)\), which will be a Barsotti–Tate group (or \(p\)-divisible group in the terminology of [33]). Then the exact sequence (2.1.8) remains valid for \(\ell=p\) and it is an exact sequence of Barsotti–Tate groups. Here we will have \(\lambda=0\), hence
and the formulas (2.1.11) remain valid, interpreting in them the rank as the rank (or "height") of the Barsotti–Tate groups in question.
2.1.14. One also introduces for \(A'{}^o_o\) invariants
\[T'_o\subset A'{}^o_o,\qquad U'_o,\qquad B'_o,\]

where \(T'_o\) is the toric part of \(A'{}^o_o\), \(B'_o\) its abelian part (defined notably if \(k\) is perfect or if \(A'{}^o_o\) is of unipotent rank zero), \(U'_o\) its unipotent part (defined for example if \(k\) is perfect, or, trivially, if \(A'{}^o_o\) is of unipotent rank zero). One finds an exact sequence corresponding to (2.1.6) or (2.1.8), which we will not write out again. One could introduce for \(A'{}^o_o\) integers \(\mu'\), \(\lambda'\), \(\alpha'\) as in (2.1.9), but it will be seen below (2.2.7) that they are the same as for \(A_o^o\), which spares us from introducing a new notation for them.
2.2. We propose to make precise the relations between \(T_\ell(A_K)\) and \(T_\ell(A_o)=T_\ell(A_o^o)\), bringing in \(T_\ell(A^o)\). Let us first recall the

blocks.title: Lemma 2.2.1.
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.2.1.

Let \(m>0\) be an integer. Then the following conditions are equivalent:

  1. (i) \(m\,\operatorname{id}_{A^o}\) is flat.
  2. (ii) \(m\,\operatorname{id}_{A^o}\) is surjective.
  3. (iii) \(m\,\operatorname{id}_{A^o}\) is quasi-finite.
  4. (iv) \(m\) is prime to the characteristic of \(k\), or \(A_o^o\) is of unipotent rank zero (cf. 1.5.5).

Moreover, if the conditions in question are verified for \(m\), they are so for \(m^\nu\) for every \(\nu>0\), the groups

\[{}_{m^\nu}A^o\mathrel{\mathop=^{\mathrm{dfn}}}\operatorname{Ker}(m^\nu\operatorname{id}_{A^o})\]

are quasi-finite, separated and flat over \(S\), and they form for variable \(\nu\) a projective system of group schemes, with faithfully flat transition morphisms \(x\longmapsto m^{\nu'-\nu}x:{}_{m^{\nu'}}A^o\longrightarrow{}_{m^\nu}A^o\).

Indeed, the criterion of flatness by fibers (EGA IV 11.3.5) shows us that (i) just like (ii), (iii) are conditions on the fibers, and they are equivalent by (SGA 3 VIA 5.6), because the fibers in question are smooth and connected. Moreover one sees from the form (ii) and by a classical theorem of Weil [5] [15] that they are verified on the generic fiber, which is an abelian scheme; it therefore suffices to express it on the special fiber. The equivalence with (iv) is then also well known, thanks to the structure theory. Under any of these forms, the stability of the condition in question under \(m\mapsto m^\nu\) is trivial, whence the fact that these conditions imply that the \({}_{m^\nu}A^o\) are flat quasi-finite group schemes over \(S\), evidently separated over \(S\) since \(A^o\) is. The last assertion on the faithful flatness of the transition morphisms in question, which amounts to saying that they are epimorphisms for the fppf topology, follows formally from the fact that \(m\,\operatorname{id}_{A^o}\) is faithfully flat, hence an epimorphism for the topology in question.
2.2.2. Accordingly, we will take for \(m\) a prime number \(\ell\) satisfying the conditions of 2.2.1. We then denote by

the projective system described in 2.2.1. It will be noted that by the two base changes \(\eta\longrightarrow S\) and \(s\longrightarrow S\), it gives back the projective systems \(T_\ell(A_K)\) and \(T_\ell(A_o^o)=T_\ell(A_o)\) considered in 1.0 and 2.1 respectively.
2.2.3. Suppose now \(S\) henselian. It is then known that every scheme \(X\) quasi-finite and separated over \(S\) decomposes canonically into a sum

where \(X^f\) is finite over \(S\), and where \(X'_o=\varnothing\) i.e. \(X'\) is reduced to its generic fiber \(X'_\eta\); hence \(X^f\) has the same special fiber as \(X\). This decomposition is evidently functorial in \(X\). One concludes in particular that if \(X\) is a group scheme over \(S\), then \(X^f\) is a subgroup scheme of it. If \(X\) is flat resp. étale over \(S\), the same is evidently true of \(X^f\).
We will apply this to the components \({}_{\ell^\nu}A^{o,f}\) of \(T_\ell(A^o)\), whence a projective subsystem of groups
(2.2.3.2) \[(({}_{\ell^\nu}A^o)^f)_{\nu>0}\mathrel{\mathop=^{\mathrm{dfn}}}T_\ell(A^o)^f\subset T_\ell(A^o),\]
which will be called the fixed part of the Tate module \(T_\ell(A^o)\). We have evidently, by construction, canonical isomorphisms, functorial in \(A\):
(2.2.3.3) \[T_\ell(A^o)^f\times_S s\xrightarrow{\sim}T_\ell(A^o)\times_S s\xrightarrow{\sim}T_\ell(A_o^o).\]
On the other hand, the generic fiber of \(T_\ell(A^o)^f\) defines a canonical sub-pro-group of \(T_\ell(A_K)\), again called the "fixed part" of this Tate module, namely
(2.2.3.4) \[T_\ell(A_K)^f\subset T_\ell(A_K).\]

2.2.4. When \(\ell\ne p=\operatorname{car}.k\), then \(T_\ell(A^o)^f\) is interpreted as a constant twisted \(\ell\)-adic sheaf on \(S\) (the \({}_{\ell^\nu}A^o\) being finite étale over \(S\)). Since the functor
\[X\longmapsto X_o\mathbin{\mathop\times_{s}}S\]

from the category of finite étale schemes over \(S\) to that of finite étale schemes over \(k\) is an equivalence of categories, \(S\) being henselian, and since we thus have an analogous equivalence for the categories of constant twisted \(\ell\)-adic sheaves on \(S\) resp. on \(k\), the relation (2.2.3.3) giving the special fiber of \(T_\ell(A^o)^f\) therefore characterizes this fixed part up to unique isomorphism. On the other hand, as a twisted constant \(\ell\)-adic subsheaf of \(T_\ell(A^o)\), it is evidently characterized by its generic fiber (2.2.3.4), or what amounts to the same, by the corresponding sub-\(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-module, stable under \(\pi=\operatorname{Gal}(\overline K/K)\):
(2.2.4.1) \[T_\ell(A_K)^f(\overline K)\mathrel{\mathop=^{\mathrm{dfn}}}T_\ell(A(\overline K))^f\subset T_\ell(A(\overline K)).\]
In fact, we have the following result:

blocks.title: Proposition 2.2.5.
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.2.5.

Suppose as above \(\ell\ne p\), and \(S\) henselian. Then the submodule (2.2.4.1) of \(T_\ell(A(\overline K))=T_\ell(A_K)(\overline K)\) corresponding to the fixed part \(T_\ell(A_K)^f\) (2.2.3.4) of \(T_\ell(A_K)\) is the submodule \(T_\ell(A(\overline K))^I\) of invariants under the inertia group \(I\subset\pi\).
This will follow, by passage to the projective limit, from the analogous relation, valid for every integer \(m>0\) prime to \(p=\operatorname{car}.k\):
(2.2.5.1) \[({}_mA)^f(\overline K)=({}_mA(\overline K))^I.\]

To prove this relation, let us introduce the strict localization \(\widetilde S\) of \(S\), with fraction field \(\widetilde K\) the maximal unramified subextension of \(\overline K\) over \(K\). Since \(({}_mA)^f\) is finite étale over \(S\), its points with values in \(\overline K\) are already with values in \(\widetilde K\), hence the first member of (2.2.5.1) is none other than \(\operatorname{Hom}_S(\widetilde S,({}_mA)^f)\), while the second is, by definition of \(\widetilde K\), equal to \({}_mA(\widetilde K)\). Now the first member, by definition of \(({}_mA)^f\), taking into account that \(\widetilde S\) is a projective limit of schemes \(S_i\) étale finite over \(S\), is also equal to \(\operatorname{Hom}_S(\widetilde S,{}_mA)= {}_m\operatorname{Hom}_S(\widetilde S,A)\), and by definition of the Néron model we have \(\operatorname{Hom}_S(\widetilde S,A)=A(\widetilde K)\), which indeed gives the announced equality (6.2.5.1).
To deduce 2.2.5 from this, one notes that one deduces from (2.2.5.1) that
\[(*)\qquad T_\ell(A(\overline K))^I=\varprojlim_ u({}_{\ell^ u}A)^f(\overline K) \supset\varprojlim_ u({}_{\ell^ u}A^o)^f(\overline K)\ ;\]

but if \((x_\nu)\) is an element of the second member, then for every integer \(\nu\geq0\), \(x_\nu\) is a section of \(A\) which is infinitely divisible by \(\ell\), hence its value at \(s\) is an element of \(A_o(k)\) infinitely divisible by \(\ell\), hence it is in \(A_o^o(k)\), which proves the equality of the last two members of (*) and completes the proof of 2.2.5.

blocks.title: Proposition 2.2.6.
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.2.6.

Let \(C_K\) be a second abelian scheme over \(K\), with Néron model \(C\), and consider a homomorphism \(u_K:C_K\longrightarrow A_K\), which therefore prolongs to a homomorphism \(u:C\longrightarrow A\). Suppose that \(u_K\) is an isogeny. Then for every prime number \(\ell\), \(T_\ell(u_K):T_\ell(C_K)\longrightarrow T_\ell(A_K)\) induces an isogeny of the fixed parts

(2.2.6.1) \[T_\ell(C_K)^f\longrightarrow T_\ell(A_K)^f,\]

and likewise the morphism induced by \(u_o:C_o\longrightarrow A_o\)

(2.2.6.2) \[T_\ell(u_o^o):T_\ell(C_o^o)\longrightarrow T_\ell(A_o^o)\]

is an isogeny. Finally, \(u_o:C_o\longrightarrow A_o\) induces an isogeny of the largest subtorus of \(C_o\) into the largest subtorus \(T_o\) of \(A_o\), and above an extension \(k'\) of \(k\) over which the "abelian parts" of \(C_o\) and of \(A_o\) are defined (for example over the perfect closure of \(k\)), \(u_o\) induces an isogeny between the abelian parts of \(C_{ok'}\) and of \(A_{ok'}\).

This statement is a formal consequence of the fact that \(T_\ell(A_K)^f\), \(T_\ell(A_o^o)\), the largest subtorus of \(A_o\), and finally the "abelian part" of \(A_{ok'}\), are for variable \(A_K\) additive functors in \(A_K\), and that the fact that \(u_K\) is an isogeny is expressed by the condition that there exists a morphism \(v_K:A_K\longrightarrow C_K\) such that we have \(u_Kv_K=n.\operatorname{id}\), \(v_Ku_K=n.\operatorname{id}\), with \(n\) an integer \(>0\).
Let us note in particular:

blocks.title: Corollary 2.2.7.
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.2.7.

If \(A_K\) and \(C_K\) are isogenous abelian schemes over \(K\), then the corresponding invariants \(\mu,\lambda,\alpha\) (2.1.11) are the same for \(A_K\) and for \(C_K\). In particular, they are the same for \(A_K\) and its dual \(A'_K\).

Remarks 2.2.8. In the two preceding statements 2.2.6 and 2.2.7, we have supposed \(S\) henselian. It is on the other hand immediate that 2.2.7, as well as the assertions of 2.2.6 concerning the properties of the induced homomorphism \(u_o:C_o\longrightarrow A_o\), are valid without this hypothesis. One reduces indeed to the case treated by the base change \(S^h\longrightarrow S\), where \(S^h\) is the henselization of \(S\), taking into account the fact (immediate by virtue of the definitions) that the formation of Néron models commutes with this base change.

blocks.title: Corollary 2.2.9. (Case of good reduction.)
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.2.9. (Case of good reduction.)

Let \(S\) be a trait, \(A_K\) an abelian scheme over the fraction field \(K\) of \(S\), \(A\) the Néron model of \(A_K\), \(\ell\) a prime number \(\ne\operatorname{car}.k\). The following conditions are equivalent:

  1. (i) There exists an abelian scheme over \(S\) whose generic fiber is isomorphic to \(A_K\).
  2. (ii) \(A\) is an abelian scheme.
  3. (iio) \(A^o\) is an abelian scheme.
  4. (iii) \(A_o\) is an abelian scheme.
  5. (iiio) \(A_o^o\) is an abelian scheme, i.e. (with the notations of (2.1.11) we have \(\lambda=\mu=0\).
  6. (iv) \(A\) is proper over \(S\).
  7. (ivo) \(A^o\) is proper over \(S\).
  8. \(v\) \(T_\ell(A_K)\) is "unramified over \(S\)" i.e. prolongs to a constant twisted \(\ell\)-adic sheaf on \(S\), i.e. the inertia group \(I\) operates trivially on \(T_\ell(A_K(\overline K))\).
  9. (v bis) For every integer \(\nu>0\), \({}_{\ell^\nu}A_K\) is "unramified over \(S\)" i.e. prolongs to an étale covering over \(S\), i.e. the inertia group \(I\) operates trivially on \({}_{\ell^\nu}A_K(\overline K)\).

Finally, an abelian scheme over \(S\) as in (i) is a Néron model of \(A_K\), and in particular is determined up to unique isomorphism.

2.2.9.1. One says that \(A_K\) has good reduction over \(S\) if it satisfies condition (i) above. The criterion \(v\) (or (v bis)) is known under the name of the NERON–OGG–SHAFAREVICH criterion of good reduction.

Let us prove 2.2.9. The fact that an abelian scheme over \(S\) satisfies the universal property of a Néron model, which reduces to the fact that a rational section of an abelian scheme over a regular base is everywhere defined, is well known (cf. e.g. [5]). This proves the equivalence (i) \(\Longleftrightarrow\) (ii) \(\Longleftrightarrow\) (iio), as well as the last assertion of 2.2.9. The conditions in question evidently imply (iii), (iv) (ivo), which each imply (iiio); let us prove that (iiio) \(\Longrightarrow\) (iio) (which will prove the equivalence of conditions (i) to (ivo)). Now this follows from the fact that a group scheme smooth over a scheme \(S\), whose fibers are abelian schemes, is proper over \(S\) (hence is an abelian scheme) by virtue of (EGA IV 15.7.10). On the other hand, taking into account 2.2.5 and the fact that \(T_\ell(A_K(\overline K))/T_\ell(A_K(\overline K))^I\) is manifestly a torsion-free \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-module, the condition \(v\) is expressed by the equality of the ranks of \(T_\ell(A_K(\overline K))\) and of its "fixed part", i.e. (by (2.1.11) and (2.2.3.3)) by the condition
\[\mu+2\alpha=2(\lambda+\mu+\alpha),\]
i.e. \(2\lambda+\mu=0\), i.e. \(\lambda=\mu=0\), which is none other than (iiio). Finally the equivalence of \(v\) and (v bis) is clear by definition. This completes the proof of 2.2.9.
2.3. We again suppose \(S\) henselian, and \(\ell\ne p=\operatorname{car}.k\). Let us now use the inclusion
(2.3.1) \[T_\ell(T_o)\lhook\joinrel\longrightarrow T_\ell(A_o^o)\]
of (2.1.6), and the equivalence of categories recalled in 2.2.4. One finds a canonical constant twisted \(\ell\)-adic subsheaf of \(T_\ell(A^o)^f\),
called the toric part of \(T_\ell(A^o)\),
(2.3.2) \[T_\ell(A^o)^t\lhook\joinrel\longrightarrow T_\ell(A^o)^f\lhook\joinrel\longrightarrow T_\ell(A^o),\]
characterized by the condition that its special fiber be given by the inclusion (2.3.1):
(2.3.3) \[T_\ell(A^o)^t\mathbin{\times_S}s=T_\ell(T_o)\lhook\joinrel\longrightarrow T_\ell(A_o^o).\]

We will moreover give further on an interpretation of the toric part of \(T_\ell(A^o)\) as the Tate module \(T_\ell(T)\) of a torus \(T\) over \(S\) which lifts the torus \(T_o\) over \(k\), an interpretation which will make it possible to extend the definition of the toric part to the case \(\ell=p\). For the moment, and to fix ideas, we restrict ourselves to examining the case \(\ell\ne p\), in which case the toric part of \(T_\ell(A^o)\) can therefore be described by the corresponding sub-\(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-module of \(T_\ell(A(\overline K))\):
(2.3.4) \[T_\ell(A(\overline K))^t\lhook\joinrel\longrightarrow T_\ell(A(\overline K))^f\lhook\joinrel\longrightarrow T_\ell(A(\overline K)),\]

stable under the action of \(\pi\), where the first term is defined as \(T_\ell(A^o)^t(\overline K)\), and is again called, as is only proper, the toric submodule of the Tate module \(T_\ell(A(\overline K))\). The latter thus appears as equipped with a canonical three-step filtration (2.3.4). Applying these reflections to the dual abelian scheme \(A'_K\), one finds likewise a three-step filtration
(2.3.5) \[T_\ell(A'(\overline K))^t\lhook\joinrel\longrightarrow T_\ell(A'(\overline K))^f\lhook\joinrel\longrightarrow T_\ell(A'(\overline K)).\]

The middle terms in (2.3.4) and (2.3.5) ("fixed parts") being described in terms of the \(\pi\)-module structures of the Tate modules of \(A(\overline K)\), \(A'(\overline K)\) as the modules of invariants under the inertia group \(I\) (1.6.5), the toric parts are completely described in terms of the fixed parts and of the pairing \(\varphi\) of (1.0.2), thanks to the following theorem (of which a particular case is already found in [11]).

Theorem 2.4. (Orthogonality theorem). Let \(S\) be a henselian trait, \(A_K\) an abelian scheme over its fraction field \(K\), \(A'_K\) the dual abelian scheme, \(\ell\) a prime number distinct from the residue characteristic of \(S\), whence on the Tate modules \(T_\ell(A(\overline K))\) and \(T_\ell(A'(\overline K))\) the canonical filtration (2.3.4) resp. (2.3.5) by the "fixed part" and by the "toric part". Then the toric part \(T_\ell(A(\overline K))^t\) is the intersection of the fixed part \(T_\ell(A(\overline K))^f\) with the orthogonal of the fixed part \(T_\ell(A'(\overline K))^f=T_\ell(A'(\overline K))^I\) for the canonical pairing \(\varphi\) (1.0.3); hence symmetrically, the toric part \(T_\ell(A'(\overline K))^t\) is the intersection of \(T_\ell(A'(\overline K))^f\) with the orthogonal of the fixed part \(T_\ell(A(\overline K))^f=T_\ell(A(\overline K))^I\). (NB the exponent I denotes the submodule of invariants under the action of the inertia group I.)

Proof a) Let us prove that \(T_\ell(A(\overline K))^t\) is orthogonal to \(T_\ell(A'(\overline K))^f\). For this, we are going to interpret the restriction of the form \(\varphi\) to the fixed parts of the Tate modules, with the help of the canonical biextension \(W^o=W\) of (1.4.4), the canonical prolongation of the biextension \(W_K\) of \((A_K,A'_K)\) by \(G_{mK}\) to a biextension of \((A^o,A'{}^o)\) by \(G_{mS}\). By virtue of VIII 2.2, to \(W\) is associated a pairing
(2.4.1) \[\varphi_W:T_\ell(A^o)\times T_\ell(A'{}^o)\longrightarrow T_\ell(G_{mS}),\]

from which the pairing \(\varphi\) of (1.0.3) is deduced by passing to the points with values in \(\overline K\). It follows that the restriction of \(\varphi\) to the fixed parts considered in 2.4 is obtained from the pairing
(2.4.2) \[T_\ell(A^o)^f\times T_\ell(A'{}^o)^f\longrightarrow T_\ell(G_{mS})\]

the restriction of (2.4.1), by passing to the points with values in \(\overline K\). Since the two terms which appear in the first member of (2.4.2) are now constant twisted \(\ell\)-adic sheaves, to study this last pairing, we can restrict ourselves to studying the pairing induced on the special fibers. Now the fibers of the two terms in question are none other than the Tate modules \(T_\ell(A_o^o),T_\ell(A'{}_o^o)\) (2.2.3.3), and the pairing deduced from (2.4.2) by passage to the special fibers is none other than the pairing
(2.4.3) \[T_\ell(A_o^o)\times T_\ell(A'{}_o^o)\longrightarrow T_\ell(G_{mk})\]
deduced from the biextension \(W_o\) of \((A_o^o,A'{}_o^o)\) by \(G_{mk}\), the restriction of \(W\) to the special fibers.

Let us now recall (2.3) that the toric part of \(T_\ell(A(\overline K))\) is obtained by passing to the points with values in \(\overline K\) in the constant twisted \(\ell\)-adic subsheaf \(T_\ell(A^o)^t\) of \(T_\ell(A^o)^f\); hence the orthogonality relation to be proved is expressed by saying that the pairing induced by (2.4.2)
(2.4.4) \[T_\ell(A^o)^t\times T_\ell(A'{}^o)^f\longrightarrow T_\ell(G_{mS})\]
is identically zero. Now it suffices to see this for the corresponding pairing on the special fibers, which by the definition of the toric part is none other than the pairing
(2.4.5) \[T_\ell(T_o)\times T_\ell(A_o^o)\longrightarrow T_\ell(G_{mk})\]
induced by (2.4.3). Now by virtue of VIII 4.10 this pairing is necessarily zero, which proves the announced orthogonality.
b) Let us prove that the inclusion
(2.4.6) \[T_\ell(A(\overline K))^t\subset T_\ell(A(\overline K))^f\cap \bigl(T_\ell(A'(\overline K))^f\bigr)^\perp\]

is an equality, where the sign \(\perp\) denotes the orthogonal. It amounts to the same to say that in the pairing (2.4.2), the annihilator of the second factor, which contains the toric part \(T_\ell(A^o)^t\) as we have just seen, is equal to the latter. Since it is again a question of relations between constant twisted \(\ell\)-adic sheaves, it suffices to verify this assertion on the special fibers, i.e. that the inclusion already obtained (1.10)
(2.4.7) \[T_\ell(T_o)\subset T_\ell(A_o'{}^o)^\perp\]

is an equality; in other words, it must be verified that the biextension \(W_o\) of \((A_o^o,A_o'{}^o)\) is non-degenerate on the left (VIII 4.11). Now this is what we have already noted in 1.5, as a consequence of a result of the thesis of RAYNAUD.
2.5. One can give an equivalent form to the orthogonality theorem, by using a polarization \(\xi\) of \(A_K\), and the alternating bilinear form (1.0.8), compatible with the operations of \(\pi\),
(2.5.1) \[\varphi_\xi:T_\ell(A(\overline K))\times T_\ell(A(\overline K)) \longrightarrow T_\ell(\overline K^*),\]
deduced from the form \(\varphi\) (1.0.3) by transport of structure on the second argument with the help of the isogeny of \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-modules
\[T_\ell(A(\overline K))\longrightarrow T_\ell(A'(\overline K))\]
induced by the isogeny \(\psi_\xi:A_K\longrightarrow A'_K\) defined by \(\xi\). The equality in (2.4.6) then takes the simple form
(2.5.2) \[W=V\cap V^\perp,\]
where we have set
(2.5.3) \[\left\{\begin{aligned} U&=T_\ell(A(\overline K)),\qquad V=U^I=\text{« fixed part » of }T_\ell(A(\overline K)),\\ W&=\text{toric part of }T_\ell(A(\overline K)), \end{aligned}\right.\]
and where \(\perp\) denotes the orthogonal with respect to the polarization form (2.5.1). The preceding modules then give rise to the diagram of inclusions
(2.5.4) \[\xymatrix@C=3.8em@R=2.2em{ &&V\ar[dr]^{2\alpha}&&\\ U&W^\perp=V+V^\perp\ar[l]_{\mu}\ar[ur]^{2\lambda}\ar[dr]_{2\alpha} &&W=V\cap V^\perp\ar[r]^{\mu}&0\\ &&V^\perp\ar[ur]_{2\lambda}&& }\]

where the integers \(2\alpha,2\lambda,\mu\) denote the ranks of the quotients of two consecutive modules in the diagram, the notations being those of (2.1.9). (The fact that \(W\) is of rank \(\mu\) is the definition of \(\mu\); that \(V/W\) is of rank \(2\alpha\) is the second relation (2.1.11) together with the exact sequence (2.1.6), whence by orthogonality rank \(U/W^\perp=\mu\) and rank \(W^\perp/V^\perp=2\alpha\); finally the formulas
(2.5.5) \[\operatorname{rang} V/V\cap V^\perp=\operatorname{rang} V/W=2\lambda,\]
and the dual formula, follow from the preceding ones, taking into account (2.1.10) and rank \(U=n\).

Remarks 2.6. a) We have obtained here the orthogonality theorem 2.4 as an easy consequence of the theory of biextensions developed in the preceding sections. One can prove the inclusion (2.4.6) without using the theory of biextensions, by an arithmetic argument in the style of the proof of the "monodromy theorem" given in I, by reducing to a situation where \(S\) has finite residue field, and where the orthogonality result follows from a simple consideration of weights for the operation of Frobenius (using the result of A. WEIL on the eigenvalues of the said Frobenius). (It is unnecessary to invoke here resolution of singularities, by stating the desired result for group schemes \(A\) over schemes \(S\), under sufficiently general conditions.) Unfortunately it does not seem that one can obtain by this method the equality in the preceding inclusion, which will be used, in an essential way it seems, in the proof of the "semi-stable reduction theorem" in the next section.

b) The orthogonality theorem 2.4 makes it possible to make explicit the most important properties of the Néron model of an abelian variety \(A_K\) in terms of the representation of the inertia group \(I\) on the Tate module \(T_\ell(A(\overline K))\), \(\ell\) being a fixed prime number distinct from the residue characteristic. (The case \(\ell=p\) will be examined separately further on (no. 5)). As a first statement of this type, but not using the orthogonality theorem, let us recall the criterion of good reduction 2.2.9. We will give numerous other examples in the sequel, beginning in the next section (3.5).

pages.title: 3. Case of semi-stable reduction. The semi-stable reduction theorem.

blocks.html: 3. Case of semi-stable reduction. The semi-stable reduction theorem.
Let us first give an auxiliary result in the style of SGA 3:

Proposition 3.1.

Let \(S\) be a scheme, \(G\) a commutative \(S\)-group scheme, flat, separated, of finite presentation over \(S\), \(H\) a group scheme quasi-separated and locally of finite type over \(S\), \(u:G\longrightarrow H\) a homomorphism of \(S\)-group schemes, \(K=\operatorname{Ker}u\). For every \(s\in S\), denoting by \(\bar s\) the spectrum of an algebraic closure of \(k(s)\), we denote by \(\mu(s)\) (resp. \(\alpha(s)\)) the reductive rank (resp. the abelian rank) of \(K_s\), i.e. the dimension of the largest subtorus (resp. of the largest abelian variety quotient) of \((K_{\bar s})^o_{\mathrm{red}}\). Let \(s\in S\), \(t\in S\) a generization of \(s\), \(p\) the characteristic exponent of \(k(s)\).

a) For every integer \(n>0\) prime to \(p\), \({}_nK\) is étale of finite type and separated over \(S\) in a neighborhood of \(s\), and we have the divisibility:

\(3.1.1\) \[\operatorname{rang}({}_nK_s)\mid \operatorname{rang}({}_nK_t).\]

b) We have

(3.1.2) \[\mu(s)+2\alpha(s)\leq\mu(t)+2\alpha(t).\]

c) If \((K_{\bar t})^o_{\mathrm{red}}\) is unipotent, the same is true of \((K_{\bar s})^o_{\mathrm{red}}\).

d) Suppose \(G_s\) of unipotent rank zero, \(H\) smooth over \(S\), and \(u_t\) an isogeny. Then \(u\) is flat and quasi-finite above an open neighborhood \(U\) of \(s\), a fortiori \(K|U\) is flat and quasi-finite.

e) Under the conditions of d), if \(u_t\) is moreover a monomorphism (hence an isomorphism of \(G_t\) onto an open subgroup of \(H_t\)), then \(u_s\) is an isomorphism of \(G_s\) onto an open subgroup of \(H_s\), and if moreover \(S\) is irreducible with generic point \(t\), then there exists an open neighborhood \(U\) of \(s\) such that \(u\) induces an isomorphism of \(G|U\) onto an open subgroup of \(H|U\).
Proof.

a) We may suppose that \(n\) is invertible on \(S\), and then \(n.\operatorname{id}_G\) is étale fiber by fiber (SGA 3 XV 1.3), hence, \(G\) being flat of finite presentation over \(S\), it follows from the fiberwise criterion of étaleness EGA IV 17.8.2 that \(n.\operatorname{id}_G\) is étale, hence its kernel \({}_nG\) is étale; it is moreover clear that it is separated and of finite presentation over \(S\), since \(G\) is. One sees likewise that \({}_nH\) is locally of finite type, quasi-separated and formally unramified over \(S\) (*) hence its unit section is an open immersion (EGA IV 17.4.2 b′)), hence the kernel of \({}_nu:{}_nG\longrightarrow{}_nH\) is an open subscheme of \({}_nG\), hence étale over \(S\), which is retrocompact in \({}_nG\) hence of finite presentation over \(S\), and separated over \(S\) since \(G\) is. Now this kernel is evidently none other than \({}_nK\).

The formula \(3.1.1\) follows from the preceding properties of \({}_nK\), as one sees by reducing by base change to the case where \(S\) is local henselian with closed point \(s\), and then using the decomposition of \({}_nK\) into \({}_nK^f\) and a scheme with empty special fiber (cf. 2.2.3). In fact, this argument shows, more precisely, that there exists a monomorphism for the geometric fibers
\[{}_nK(\bar s)\lhook\joinrel\longrightarrow{}_nK(\bar t).\]

b) Let \(n>0\) be prime to the characteristic exponent of \(k(s)\) and to the ranks of the quotients of the geometric fibers of \(K\) at \(s\) and \(t\) by their reduced identity components; then the two members of \(3.1.1\) are obtained by raising \(n\) respectively to the power with exponent the one and the other member of (3.1.2), as follows from the known structure of commutative algebraic groups. Hence b) follows from a).
c) Since the hypothesis \((K_{\bar t})^o_{\mathrm{red}}\) unipotent is equivalent to the relations \(\mu(t)=\alpha(t)=0\), and likewise for \(K_s\), assertion c) follows formally from b).

d) The hypothesis that \(u_t\) is an isogeny implies that \(K_t\) is finite, hence we are under the conditions of c), and consequently \((K_{\bar s})^o_{\mathrm{red}}\) is unipotent. Since \(G_s\) is of unipotent rank zero, one deduces that \(K_s\) is finite. Moreover the fact that \(u_t\) is an isogeny implies \(\dim G_t=\dim H_t\), and the fact that \(G\) and \(H\) are flat locally of finite presentation implies \(\dim G_s=\dim G_t\), \(\dim H_s=\dim H_t\) (SGA 3 VIB 4.3), whence \(\dim G_s=\dim H_s\). Since \(\dim\operatorname{Ker}u_s=0\), one concludes that \(\dim H_s=\dim u_s(G_s)\) (SGA 3 VIB 1.2), which is equivalent to \(u_s\) flat thanks to the hypothesis \(H_s\) smooth (SGA 3 VIA 5.6). One concludes that there exists an open neighborhood \(U\) of \(s\) such that \(u\) is flat and quasi-finite at the points of the unit section over \(U\) (SGA 3 VIB 2.2 (i) and 2.5 (ii)). It follows, as for \(u_s\), that \(u_{s'}\) is flat for every \(s'\in U\), hence \(u|_{G_U}\) is flat and quasi-finite, being so on each fiber.

e) For the first assertion, one reduces at once by base change to the case where \(S\) is integral with generic point \(t\), hence to proving the second assertion of e). The latter follows at once from d) and from the

Lemma 3.1.3.

Let \(S\) be a scheme, \(K\) an \(S\)-scheme quasi-finite, of finite presentation, separated and flat over \(S\), \(s\in S\), \(t\in T\) a generization of \(s\). Then we have

\[\operatorname{rang}K_s\leq\operatorname{rang}K_t.\]

If \(\operatorname{rang}K_\eta\leq1\) for every maximal point of \(S\), then \(K\longrightarrow S\) is an open immersion (hence an isomorphism if it admits a section).

For the first assertion, one reduces by strict localization to the case where \(S\) is strictly local. Decomposing then \(K\) into the sum of a finite scheme \(K^f\) and a scheme with empty special fiber, we will have
\[\operatorname{rang}K_s=\operatorname{rang}K_s^f=\operatorname{rang}K_t^f\leq\operatorname{rang}K_t,\]
the second equality coming from the fact that \(K^f\) is flat, finite and of finite presentation over \(S\), hence locally free over \(S\).

For the second assertion, the preceding already shows that for every \(s\in S\) we have \(\operatorname{rang}K_s\leq1\), hence \(K_s\) is empty or isomorphic to \(\operatorname{Spec}k(s)\). Consequently \(K\longrightarrow S\) is a monomorphism (SGA 3 VIB 2.11), and being flat and of finite presentation it is an open immersion (EGA IV 17.9.1), which completes the proof.

Remark 3.1.4. In 3.1 a) and the rest of proposition 3.1, no information is given about \({}_nK_s\) when \(n\) is a power of \(p\) (when \(p>1\)). When \(G\) is smooth (and \(S\) locally noetherian or \(H\) of finite presentation over \(S\)) at least, one can give such information, by essentially the same method as in the proof of a) (reducing to the case \(S\) local noetherian complete, and using SGA 3 XV 1.6 c), X 3.2, IX 3.6, IX 5.2), namely:
\(3.1.4.1\) \[\operatorname{rang\,mult}({}_nK_s)\mid\operatorname{rang\,mult}({}_nK_t),\]

where rank mult denotes the rank of the largest subgroup of multiplicative type of the commutative algebraic group in question (SGA 3 XVII 7.2.1); it will be noted that an algebraic group of multiplicative type which is torsion is finite. The formula \(3.1.4.1\) is valid for every integer \(n\geq1\). One concludes, thanks to SGA XVII 4.6.1 (vi), that in c), if \(K_t\) is unipotent (SGA 3 VII 1.1), the same is true of \(K_s\).

Proposition 3.2.

Let \(S\) be a locally noetherian regular integral scheme of dimension 1, \(K\) its field of rational functions, \(A_K\) an abelian scheme over \(K\), \(A\) its Néron model over \(S\), which is a group scheme smooth and separated over \(S\) (and quasi-projective over \(S\) if \(S\) is noetherian [23, VIII 2, 3°]). The following conditions are equivalent:

(i) For every \(s\in S\), \(A_s^o\) is of unipotent rank zero, i.e. (2.1.5) is an extension of an abelian scheme by a torus.

(ii) There exists a group scheme \(G\) smooth, separated of finite type over \(S\), prolonging \(A_K\), and whose fibers are of unipotent rank zero.

Moreover, if \(G\) is as in (ii), then the unique morphism \(G\longrightarrow A\) inducing the given isomorphism on the generic fibers is an isomorphism of \(G\) onto an open subgroup of \(A\), hence induces an isomorphism of the identity components \(G^o\xrightarrow{\sim}A^o\).

3.2.1. Let us note right away that the definition of the Néron model \(A\) of \(A_K\) is the same as in the local case, recalled in 1.1. The existence of this Néron model, and the fact that it is separated of finite type over \(S\), is well known, and in fact reduces immediately to the local case. Of course, for every closed point \(s\in S\), \(S'=\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal O_{S,s})\) is a trait, and \(A\times_SS'\) is none other than the Néron model of \(A_K\) over \(S'\), in the sense considered in 1.1.

Let us prove 3.2. It is trivial that (i) implies (ii), for it suffices to take \(G=A\). It thus remains to prove that if \(G\) is as in (ii), then \(G\longrightarrow A\) is an open immersion. Now this is indeed a particular case of 3.1 e).

Corollary 3.3. With the notations of 3.2, suppose the identity components of the fibers of the Néron model are of unipotent rank zero. Let \(S'\) be a scheme satisfying the same hypotheses as \(S\), \(K'\) its field of rational functions, \(f:S'\longrightarrow S\) a dominant morphism, \(A_{K'}=A_K\otimes_KK'=\) the generic fiber of \(A\times_SS'\), \(A'\) the Néron model of \(A_{K'}\), and consider the unique morphism \[u:A\times_SS'\longrightarrow A'\] inducing the identity on the generic fibers. Then \(u\) is an open immersion, and hence induces an isomorphism on the identity components \[(3.3.1)\qquad A^o\times_SS'\xrightarrow{\sim}A'{}^o.\]
It suffices indeed to apply 3.2 in the case of \(S'\), taking \(G=A\times_SS'\).

3.3.2. One sees therefore that the equivalent properties of 3.2 imply a partial "stability" property for Néron models; one should beware that when \(A_K\) does not have good reduction, i.e. when \(A\) is not an abelian scheme, it is not true that for every \(S'\) as in 3.3 (and even for \(S'\) finite over \(S\), and \(K'/K\) separable ...), the morphism \(A\times_SS'\longrightarrow A'\) itself is an isomorphism, i.e. induces isomorphisms on the groups of connected components of the fibers (cf. 11.10 below for the behavior of these groups of connected components under extension of the base). These observations, reinforced by 3.9 below, justify the following terminology, introduced by MUMFORD (and which harmonizes also, it seems, with the notions of the same name studied in his book [17]):

Definition 3.4. The notations being those of 3.2, one says that the abelian scheme \(A_K\) over \(K\) has semi-stable reduction over \(S\) if the equivalent conditions (i) and (ii) of 3.2 are verified.

3.4.0. It will be noted that in the form (ii), this condition makes no appeal to the notion of Néron model. On the other hand, it reduces immediately by localization to the case where \(S\) is a trait, as follows for example from 3.2.1. Moreover, since the formation of the Néron model commutes with the base change from \(S\) to its henselization, or its strict henselization, the notion even reduces to the case where \(S\) is a strictly local trait.

Proposition 3.5. (Galois criterion of semi-stable reduction.) We suppose \(S\) a trait. The following conditions on the abelian scheme \(A_K\) are equivalent (the notations being those of (2.5.3), applied to the situation deduced from \((A_K,S)\) by passage to the henselization of \(S\)):
  1. (i) \(A_K\) has semi-stable reduction over \(S\) (3.4).
  2. (ii) We have, with the notations (2.5.3), (2.5.4) \[V\supset V^\perp.\]
  3. (iii) We have for the toric part \(W\) of the Tate module: \[W=V^\perp.\]
  4. (iv) The action of the inertia group \(I\) on \(U=T_\ell(A(\overline K))\) is unipotent of level 2 (i.e. there exists a submodule \(U'\) of \(U\) stable under \(I\) such that \(I\) operates trivially on \(U'\) and on \(U/U'\)).
We may suppose \(S\) henselian (3.4.0). With the notations of 2.1.9, (i) means that we have
\[\lambda=0\]

which by virtue of (2.5.5) is equivalent to \(V=V\cap V^\perp\) i.e. to (ii). Since \(W=V\cap V^\perp\) by the orthogonality theorem 2.4, we have (ii) \(\Longleftrightarrow\) (iii). Moreover (iv) means also that \(I\) operates trivially on \(U^I=V\), which by duality means that it operates trivially on \(V^\perp\) i.e. that \(V^\perp\subset U^I=V\), which is (ii). This completes the proof.

Remarks 3.5.1. The conditions (ii) and (iv) visibly depend only on the action of \(I\) on the \(\mathbb Q_\ell\)-vector space \(U\otimes_{\mathbb Z_\ell}\mathbb Q_\ell\), and could have been formulated in terms of this action. Let us also point out that these conditions are manifestly stable under any base change by a morphism of traits \(S'\longrightarrow S\). Finally, if (iv) is satisfied for \(A_K\), it is also manifestly satisfied for every abelian subscheme and every abelian scheme quotient of \(A_K\), and in particular for every abelian scheme isogenous to \(A_K\) (for example for \(A'_K\)).

Corollary 3.5.2. Let \(g\) be a topological generator of \(\mathbb Z_\ell(1)=T_\ell(\overline K^*)\). The equivalent conditions of 3.5 are also equivalent to the following condition:

\(v\) The action of the inertia group \(I\) on \(U=T_\ell(A(\overline K))\) is carried out through the largest pro-\(\ell\)-finite quotient \(I(\ell)\) of \(I\), which is canonically isomorphic to \(\mathbb Z_\ell(1)\), and the operation \(g_U\) of the generator \(g\) on \(U\) satisfies the condition

\[(3.5.3)\qquad (1-g_U)^2=0.\]

Indeed, for any continuous unipotent action of a profinite group \(I\) on an \(\ell\)-adic vector space, we see at once that the image of \(I\) is a pro-\(\ell\)-group. It follows that \(v\) is equivalent to (iv).
We can now prove the central result of the present exposé:

Theorem 3.6. (semi-stable reduction theorem). Let \(S\) be a noetherian regular connected scheme of dimension 1, \(K\) the field of rational functions of \(S\), \(A_K\) an abelian scheme over \(K\). Then there exists a finite Galois extension \(K'\) of \(K\), such that the abelian scheme \(A_{K'}=A_K\otimes_KK'\) has semi-stable reduction (3.4) over the normalization \(S'\) of \(S\) in \(K'\).

We observe that \(S'\) is noetherian regular connected of dimension 1, which gives a meaning to the stated conclusion. To prove 3.6, let us choose a separable closure \(\overline K\) of \(K\), and write the extension \(\overline K\) as an inductive limit of its finite Galois subextensions \(K_i\). Let \(S_i\) be the normalization of \(S\) in \(K_i\), and let \(U\) be the largest open subset of \(S\) over which \(A_K\) has good reduction. Since \(S\) is noetherian connected of dimension 1, and \(U\ne\varnothing\), we see that \(T=S-U\) is a finite set of closed points of \(S\), and that for every \(t\in T\), \(\mathcal O_{S,t}\) is a discrete valuation ring. It suffices to find, for every \(t\in T\), an index \(i=i(t)\) such that \(A_{K_i}\) has semi-stable reduction at the points of \(S_i\) lying above \(t\); indeed, it will then suffice to take for \(K'\) the compositum of the extensions \(K_{i(t)}\) for \(t\in T\). This therefore reduces us to the case where \(S\) is a trait. On the other hand, since the group \(\operatorname{Gal}(K_i/K)\) permutes among themselves the points of \(S_i\) lying above the closed point \(s\) of \(S\), it suffices to find \(K'\) in such a way that \(A_{K'}\) has semi-stable reduction at one point of the normalization \(S'\); moreover it is unnecessary here that \(K'\) be Galois over \(K\), it suffices to find \(K'\) a finite étale extension of \(K\) satisfying the preceding condition, since the Galois closure of this extension will satisfy the same condition. Using the criterion 3.5 (iv) we thus see that 3.6 amounts to the following assertion:

Corollary 3.7. We again suppose that \(S\) is a trait. Then there exists an open subgroup \(\pi''\) of \(\pi\) such that the action \(I'=I\cap\pi''\) on \(U=T_\ell(A(\overline K))\) is unipotent of level 2, or again: there exists an open subgroup \(I'\) of \(I\) whose action on \(U\) is unipotent of level 2.

We have a canonical isomorphism (compatible with the operations of \(\pi\)): \[(*)\qquad H^1(A_{\overline K},\mathbb Z_\ell)\simeq \operatorname{Hom}(T_\ell(A(\overline K)),\mathbb Z_\ell),\] which shows that assertion 3.7 is equivalent to the same assertion for the operations of \(I\) on the left-hand side of (*). Now this assertion was proved in III (using resolution of singularities for excellent schemes of dimension 2).

Corollary 3.8. The equivalent conditions (i) to (iv) of 3.5 are also equivalent to the following condition:

(vi) The action of \(I\) on \(U=T_\ell(A(\overline K))\) is unipotent.

Indeed, to prove that \(v\) implies (vi), we note that if a group \(I\) acting in a unipotent way on a vector space \(E\) of finite dimension over a field of characteristic zero (in this case \(\mathbb Q_\ell\)) admits a subgroup of finite index \(I'\) acting with level \(m\) (in this case \(m=2\)), then \(I\) itself acts with level \(m\). Indeed, \(I'\) being of finite index in \(I\), the algebraic group \(G'\subset GL(E)\) which it defines is of finite index in the one, \(G\), defined by \(I\); now \(G\) being unipotent is connected (characteristic zero!), hence \(G=G'\), hence \(I\) and \(I'\) have the same "level of unipotence".
We also obtain a converse to 3.3:

Corollary 3.9. The notations are those of 3.2. Conditions (i) and (ii) of 3.2 are also equivalent to the following condition:

(iii) For every étale extension \(K'\) of \(K\), denoting by \(S'\) the normalization of \(S\) in \(K'\), by \(A'\) the Néron model of \(A_{K'}=A\otimes_SK'=A_K\otimes_KK'\), the canonical morphism \[A\times_SS'\longrightarrow A'\] induces an isomorphism on the identity components.

The necessity of the condition was indeed seen, in a stronger form, in 3.3. To see the sufficiency, we choose \(K'\) as in 3.6. Since the fibers of \(A'{}^0\) have unipotent rank zero, the same is true of those of \((A\times_SS')^0=A^0\times_SS'\), hence also of those of \(A^0\), from which the preceding ones are deduced by extension of the base field.

footnotes.html:
At least if \(H\) is commutative; in the general case, it will remain
true that \({}_nH\) is unramified over \(S\) along its unit section, which suffices
for our purposes.

pages.title: 4. Application to a conjecture of SERRE-TATE and to the conductor

blocks.html: 4. Application to a conjecture of SERRE-TATE and to the conductor

4.1. We suppose \(S\) henselian, and keep the notations of (2.5.3), with \(\ell\ne p\) (*). To study the action of \(\pi=\operatorname{Gal}(\overline K/K)\) and of its inertia subgroup \(I\) on \(U=T_\ell(A(\overline K))\), we shall use the fact that there exists an open subgroup \(I'\) of \(I\), which we shall even take to be invariant in \(\pi\), such that the action of \(I'\) on \(U\) is unipotent of level 2, i.e. such that we have \[U^{I'}\supset (U^{I'})^\perp.\] It is clear that \(U^{I'}\) does not then depend on the particular choice of \(I'\); we could call it the essentially fixed submodule of \(U\), and we shall denote it by \(U^{ef}\), its orthogonal complement then taking the name of essentially toric submodule of \(U\), which will be denoted \(U^{et}\). We thus have a three-step filtration \[(4.1.1)\qquad U\supset U^{ef}\supset U^{et}\supset0,\] which is evidently invariant under the action of \(\pi\). Let us note at the same time that there exists a largest subgroup of \(I\) which acts in a unipotent way (or, what amounts to the same thing, unipotent of level two) on \(U\): it is the subgroup \(I'\) of the elements which act in a unipotent way, which is also the kernel of the representation of \(I\) in the graded module associated with the filtration (4.1.1) of \(U\), or, what amounts to the same thing here, the kernel of the induced representation of \(I\) on \(U^{ef}\). By virtue of the Galois criterion of semi-stable reduction (3.5), this subgroup of \(I'\) corresponds to the smallest Galois extension \(\widetilde K'\) of \(K\) (the maximal unramified extension of \(K\)) such that \(A_{\widetilde K'}\) has semi-stable reduction. This description shows at the same time that this subgroup \(I'\) of \(I\) does not depend on the choice of the prime number \(\ell\ne p\).

Note that the representation of \(\pi\) on \(U/U^{ef}\) is known up to isogeny once we know the representation on \(U^{ef}\), hence on \(U^{et}\), thanks to the isogeny induced by the polarization pairing (2.5.1): \[(4.1.2)\qquad U/U^{ef}\longrightarrow (U^{et})^\vee(1)\quad\text{an isogeny},\] where the (1) denotes the Tate twist by \(\mathbb Z_\ell(1)=T_\ell(\overline K^*)\); the preceding homomorphism is indeed, evidently, compatible with the operations of \(\pi\). (NB. We have a canonical isomorphism \(U/U^{ef}\simeq(U'{}^{et})^\vee(1)\), where \(U'=T_\ell(A'(\overline K))\)). It is therefore natural to study more closely the action of \(\pi\) on \(U^{ef}\).

Now by construction, the latter is trivial on the invariant subgroup \(I'\) of \(I\); it therefore factors through a representation of the quotient group \[(4.1.3)\qquad \pi'=\pi/I',\] an action of which we shall now give a description "independent of \(\ell\)".

4.2. Let then \(K'\) be a Galois extension of \(K\), corresponding to an open invariant subgroup \(V\) of \(\pi\) such that \(V\cap I=I'\), the Galois group \(G\) of \(K'/K\) thus appearing as an extension \[(4.2.1)\qquad1\longrightarrow\Gamma\longrightarrow G\longrightarrow G_0\longrightarrow1,\] where \((4.2.2)\ \Gamma=I/I'\) is the inertia subgroup, and \(G_0\) the Galois group of the largest unramified subextension \(K''\) of \(K'/K\). The exact sequence (4.2.1) is thus the image of the analogous exact sequence \[(4.2.3)\qquad1\longrightarrow\Gamma\longrightarrow\pi'\longrightarrow\pi_0\longrightarrow1,\] where \(\pi'\) is the group (4.1.3) acting on \(U^{ef}\). We thus have a diagram of inclusions of fields \[(4.2.4)\qquad K\longrightarrow K''=\widetilde K\cap K'\longrightarrow\widetilde K, \qquad K'\longrightarrow\widetilde K'\longrightarrow\overline K.\] Let \(S'\) be the normalization of \(S\) in \(K'\), \(A^*\) the Néron model of \(A_{K'}\). Then by the Galois criterion of semi-stable reduction (4.5), \(A_{K'}\) has semi-stable reduction over \(S'\), i.e. \(A^{*0}\) is an extension of an abelian scheme by a torus; on the other hand, by transport of structure, \(G\) acts on \(A^*\) hence on \(A^{*0}\) by automorphisms in a way compatible with the given action of \(G\) on the base scheme \(S'\) of \(A^*\). It is then clear that the action of \(\pi'\) on \(U^{ef}=T_\ell(A^{*0})_f(\overline K)\) is deduced by transport of structure from the action of \(\pi'\) on \((A^{*0}/K',\widetilde K')\), \(\pi'\) being equivalent over \(A^*\) via \(G\). Taking into account the canonical isomorphism \[(4.2.6)\qquad U^{ef}\simeq T_\ell(A^{*0})_f(\overline K)\simeq T_\ell(A_0^{*0})(\overline k)\] (valid for every twisted constant \(\ell\)-adic sheaf on \(S'\), cf. I), we then find the following description of the action of \(\pi'\) on \(U^{ef}\):

We consider the smooth connected group scheme \(A_0^{*0}\) over the residue field \(k'\subset\overline k\) of \(S'\), which is a quasi-Galois extension of \(k\) with Galois group \(G_0\). The quotient group \(G\) of \(\pi'\) acts on \(A_0^{*0}\) in a way compatible with its operation on \(k'\) via \(G_0\). Moreover \(\pi'\) acts on \(\overline k\) via its quotient \(\pi_0\), hence it acts on the situation \((A_0^{*0}/k',\overline k)\). Therefore by transport of structure \(\pi'\) acts on \[U^{ef}=T_\ell(A_0^{*0})(\overline k),\] and this is the desired operation of \(\pi'\). In particular, the subgroup \(\Gamma\) of \(G\) which acts trivially on \(k'\) acts on \(A_0^{*0}\) by \(k'\)-automorphisms, and the corresponding representation of \(\Gamma=I/I'\) on \(T_\ell(A_0^{*0})(\overline k)\) is the representation deduced from the natural representation of the inertia group \(I\) on \(U^{ef}\).

On the other hand, the essentially toric part \(U^{et}\) of \(U\) corresponds simply to the submodule \[(4.2.7)\qquad U^{et}\simeq T_\ell(T_0^*)(\overline k)\subset U^{ef}\simeq T_\ell(A_0^{*0})(\overline k),\]
where \(T_0^*\) is the largest subtorus of \(A_0^{*o}\). Setting likewise

for the abelian part of \(A_0^{*o}\), the operation of \(G\) on \(A_0^{*o}\) induces separately operations of \(G\) on \(T_0^*\) and on \(B_0^*\), and the operation of \(\pi'\) on \(U^{et}\) and on \(U^{ef}/U^{et}\) is deduced from the preceding operations by means of the isomorphism (4.2.7) and the corresponding isomorphism
\[(4.2.9)\qquad U^{ef}/U^{et}\simeq T_\ell(B_0^*)(\overline k).\]
From this, and from the isogeny (4.1.2), we deduce, for the character \(g\longmapsto\operatorname{Tr}g_U\) of the action of \(\pi'\) (or of \(\pi\)) on \(U\), the formula
\[(4.2.10)\qquad \operatorname{Tr}g_{U_\ell}=\operatorname{Tr}g_{B_0^*,\ell} +\operatorname{Tr}g_{T_0^*,\ell}+\chi_\ell(g)\operatorname{Tr}g_{T_0^*,\ell}^{-1},\]

where on the left-hand side we have written \(U_\ell\) for \(U\), to recall the chosen prime number \(\ell\ne p\), where \(g_{B_0^*,\ell}\) resp. \(g_{T_0^*,\ell}\) denotes the action of \(g\) on the Tate module \(T_\ell(B_0^*)(\overline k)\) resp. \(T_\ell(T_0^*)(\overline k)\), deduced from its action on \(B_0^*\) resp. \(T_0^*\) which we have just made explicit, and where
\[(4.2.11)\qquad \chi_\ell:\pi'\longrightarrow\pi_0\longrightarrow\mathbb Z_\ell^*\]
is the character of \(\pi'\) (or of \(\pi_0\)) deduced from the action on \(\mathbb Z_\ell(1)=T_\ell(\overline k^*)\), — a character which is trivial on \(\Gamma^o\).
We are now in a position to prove a particular case of a conjecture of SERRE-TATE [29, Appendice] (*).

Theorem 4.3. Let \(S\) be a henselian trait with residue field \(k\), field of functions \(K\), \(A_K\) an abelian scheme over \(K\); consider the representation of \(\pi=\operatorname{Gal}(\overline K/K)\) on the Tate module \(U_\ell=T_\ell(A(\overline K))\), where \(\ell\) is a prime number distinct from the residue characteristic.

a) There exists an open subgroup \(I'\) of the inertia group which acts in a unipotent way on \(U_\ell\), so that the character of the representation of \(\pi\) on \(U_\ell\) is in fact a function on \(\pi/I'\). Moreover, on \(\Gamma=I/I'\) this function takes integer values, and is independent of \(\ell\). More generally, for every \(g\in I\), the coefficients of the characteristic polynomial of \(g_{U_\ell}\) are rational integers independent of \(\ell\).

b) Suppose that \(k\) is a finite field with \(q\) elements, so that \(\pi_0=\pi/I\simeq\operatorname{Gal}(\overline k/k)\) is identified with the completion \(\widehat{\mathbb Z}\) of \(\mathbb Z\), the generator \(1\) of \(\mathbb Z\) corresponding to the frobenius automorphism \(f_q:x\longmapsto x^q\) of \(\overline k/k\). Then for every \(g\in\pi\) whose image in \(\pi_0\) lies in \(\mathbb Z\), the trace (and more generally every coefficient of the characteristic polynomial) of \(g_{U_\ell}\) is a rational number independent of \(\ell\), which is even an integer if the image of \(g\) in \(\pi_0\) is of the form \(f_q^n\), with \(n\geq0\).

Let us prove, for example, the assertions about the traces. The assertions about the coefficients of the characteristic polynomial would be proved in the same way, but we may also note that they follow formally from the assertions made about the traces, thanks to the computation of these coefficients in terms of the traces of the iterates (formulas which, however, introduce denominators, hence do not give the announced integrality), and to a lemma of DWORK-FATOU (of which a pretty proof is given by KLEIMAN in [14 2.d]).
a) We use formula (4.2.10), which here becomes
\[(4.3.0)\qquad \operatorname{Tr}g_{U_\ell}=\operatorname{Tr}g_{B_0^*,\ell} +\operatorname{Tr}g_{T_0^*,\ell}+\operatorname{Tr}g_{T_0^*,\ell}^{-1},\]

where now \(g_{B_0^*}\) and \(g_{T_0^*}\) are \(k'\)-automorphisms of \(B_0^*\) resp. of \(T_0^*\). It therefore suffices to show that the two corresponding traces are rational integers independent of \(\ell\), which is a general "geometric" statement about algebraic groups. For \(g_{B_0^*}\) this is a well-known result of WEIL from the theory of abelian varieties [5 ][15 ]; for \(g_{T_0^*}\) it is practically trivial, by making explicit the representation of \(\Gamma\) on \(T=T_0^*\) by means of the corresponding representation on the dual group \(M'\), isomorphic to \(\mathbb Z^d\): the desired trace is contragredient to that of this latter representation, thanks to the canonical isomorphism
\[T_\ell(T)\simeq \check M_\ell(1)\mathrel{\overset{\mathrm{dfn}}=}\check M'\otimes_{\mathbb Z}\mathbb Z_\ell(1).\]
b) If the image of \(g\) in \(\pi_0\) is \(f_q^n\), formula (4.2.10) now becomes
\[(4.3.1)\qquad \operatorname{Tr}g_{U,\ell}=\operatorname{Tr}g_{B_0^*,\ell} +\operatorname{Tr}g_{T_0^*,\ell}+q^n\operatorname{Tr}g_{T_0^*,\ell}^{-1}.\]
We are thus reduced to proving the following lemma (in which we shall take \(k\longmapsto\overline k\), \(A\longmapsto A_0^*\otimes_{k'}\overline k\)):

Lemma 4.3.2. Let \(k\) be the algebraic closure of a finite field, \(A\) a commutative group scheme of finite type over \(k\), \(g\) an automorphism of this group scheme, compatible with the automorphism \(f\) of \(\operatorname{Spec}(k)\) deduced by transport of structure from an iterated frobenius automorphism \(x\longmapsto x^q\) (where \(q=p^r\) is a power of the characteristic, with \(r\ne0\)), — so that we have for the action of \(g\) on functions \(\varphi\):
\[g^*(\lambda\varphi)=f^*(\lambda)g^*(\varphi)=\lambda^{q^{-1}}g^*(\varphi) =\lambda^{p^{-r}}g^*(\varphi)\qquad(\lambda\in k).\]

Consider the automorphism \(g_\ell\) induced by \(g\) on \(T_\ell(A(k))\) by "transport of structure", where \(\ell\) is a prime number \(\ne p\). Then the trace of \(g_\ell\) (more generally, every coefficient of its characteristic polynomial) is a rational integer, independent of \(\ell\), and its eigenvalues are algebraic integers of absolute value equal to \(q^{1/2}\) or \(q\). If \(A\) is an abelian scheme, the absolute values are \(q^{1/2}\); if \(A\) is affine, the eigenvalues are of the form \(q\varepsilon_i\), where the \(\varepsilon_i\) are roots of unity.

Indeed, let us consider the endomorphism \(f_q^A\), "raising to the \(q\)-th power", of the absolute scheme underlying \(A\), which is defined as the identity on points and raising to the \(q\)-th power on the structure sheaf. It is an endomorphism of the group scheme \(A\) compatible with the endomorphism \(f_q^{\operatorname{Spec}(k)}=f^{-1}\) on the base \(\operatorname{Spec}(k)\), hence the composite

is an endomorphism of the group scheme \(A\), compatible with the identity on \(\operatorname{Spec}(k)\), i.e. it is a \(k\)-endomorphism of the group scheme \(A\). I claim that the automorphism \(\Gamma_g\) of \(A(k)\) defined by \(g\) by transport of structure, made explicit by the formula
\[(4.3.4)\qquad \Gamma_g(u)=g\circ u\circ f^{-1}\qquad\text{for }u\in A(k),\]
can also be described by
\[(4.3.5)\qquad \Gamma_g(u)=F(u)\qquad\text{for }u\in A(k).\]

To verify this, it suffices to see that the set-theoretic maps underlying the morphisms \(\operatorname{Spec}(k)\longrightarrow A\) represented by the two sides are the same, which reduces us to proving the equality deduced from (4.3.5) by composing the first member there on the left with \(f_q^A\), and the second member on the right with \(f^{-1}\) (since \(f_q^A\) and \(f^{-1}\) induce the identity on the sets underlying the source schemes).
