But the formula in question is then an immediate consequence of (4.3.3) and (4.3.4).

Applying (4.3.5) to the \(u\in{}_{\ell^\nu}A(k)\), and passing to the limit over \(\nu\), we find that \(g_\ell\) can also be interpreted as the endomorphism \(T_\ell(F)\) of \(T_\ell(A(k))\) associated with the endomorphism \(F\) of \(A\). We know on the other hand that \(T_\ell(F)\) has a characteristic polynomial whose coefficients are integers independent of \(\ell\), by reducing by dévissage to the case where \(A\) is either an abelian scheme or a torus, where the result in question was already used in part a) of our proof. Moreover, this proof also establishes the assertions made about the eigenvalues, taking into account, in the case of an abelian scheme, the classical results of WEIL [15].
This proves 4.3.2 and completes the proof of 4.3, and proves at the same time:

Corollary 4.4. Let us place ourselves under the conditions of 4.3 b). Then for every element \(g\) in \(\pi'\) whose image in \(\pi_0\) is of the form \(f_q^n\) with \(n\geq0\), the eigenvalues of the induced automorphisms of \(U^{et}\), \(U^{ef}/U^{et}\), \(U/U^{ef}\) are algebraic integers whose absolute values are respectively \(q^n\), \(q^{n/2}\) and \(1\). As regards the two extreme pieces, we can say, more precisely, that the corresponding eigenvalues are respectively \((q^n\varepsilon_i)_{1\leq i\leq\mu}\) and \((\varepsilon_i)_{1\leq i\leq\mu}\), where the \(\varepsilon_i\ (1\leq i\leq\mu)\) are roots of unity.

In fact, formula (4.1.2) gives for the last piece the eigenvalues \(q^n/(q^n\varepsilon_i)=\varepsilon_i^{-1}\), but since \(\varepsilon_i\) is a root of unity, we have \(\varepsilon_i^{-1}=\overline{\varepsilon_i}\) (complex conjugate), and since the family of the \(\varepsilon_i\) is stable under complex conjugation (being the family of solutions of an equation with integer coefficients), the family of the \(\varepsilon_i^{-1}\) is also that of the \(\varepsilon_i\), which proves the last assertion of 4.4.

4.5. We shall give an application of 4.3 a) to the theory of the local conductor of abelian schemes. Suppose \(k\) algebraically closed (*). Recall (SGA 5 X 6.2 or [20]) that for every étale commutative \(\ell\)-group scheme \(F_K\) over \(K\), \((\ell\ne p)\), we define an integer
\[(4.5.1)\qquad \alpha_s(F_K)=\alpha_s(i_!F_K)\]

(\(i:\eta\longrightarrow S\) denoting the inclusion), in the following way: we choose a finite Galois extension \(K'\) of \(K\), with group \(\Gamma\), which splits \(F_K\), so that \(F_K\) is defined by a finite \(\ell\)-group \(M\) on which \(\Gamma\) acts. On the other hand, we associate with the extension \(K'\) of \(K\) a certain projective \(\mathbb Z_\ell[\Gamma]\)-module (defined up to isomorphism)
\[(4.5.2)\qquad \operatorname{Sw}=\operatorname{Sw}(K'/K,\mathbb Z_\ell),\]
and we set
\[(4.5.3)\qquad \alpha_s(F_K)=\operatorname{long}\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathbb Z_\ell(\Gamma)}(\operatorname{Sw},M).\]

One verifies (loc. cit.) that this expression does not depend on the choice of the extension \(K'/K\). Moreover, the fact that \(\operatorname{Sw}\) is a projective \(\mathbb Z_\ell[\Gamma]\)-module implies that \(\alpha_s(F_K)\) behaves additively with respect to exact sequences.
We shall apply this definition to the case of the sheaf \({}_{\ell}A_K\), where \(A_K\) is our abelian scheme over \(K\). We thus have, in this case, an integer

\[(4.5.4)\qquad \alpha_s(A_K;\ell)=\operatorname{long}\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathbb Z_\ell(\Gamma)} (\operatorname{Sw},U_\ell\otimes_{\mathbb Z_\ell}\mathbb F_\ell),\qquad U_\ell=T_\ell(A(\overline K)),\]

\(\Gamma\) being chosen as was said above (a choice depending a priori on \(\ell\)). The integer (4.5.4) is called the non-tame conductor exponent (or "wild" conductor exponent) (*) of the abelian scheme \(A_K\) with respect to the discrete valuation ring \(V\subset K\); by passage to the henselization, it is of course defined without assuming \(V\) henselian, and one reduces at once to the case where \(S\) is strictly local. We propose to show the
Corollary 4.6. The non-tame conductor exponent (4.5.4) does not depend on the choice of the prime number \(\ell\ne p\).

We may suppose \(S\) strictly local. We shall give the computation of (4.5.4), replacing \(\operatorname{Sw}\) by any other projective \(\mathbb Z_\ell(\Gamma)\)-module \(L\). Note that the filtration (4.1.1) of \(U_\ell\) is by sub-\(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-modules which are manifestly direct factors, hence this filtration induces an analogous filtration of \(U(\ell)=U_\ell\otimes_{\mathbb Z_\ell}\mathbb F_\ell\), whose successive factors are deduced from the factors \(V_i\ (i=1,2,3)\)
\[U^{et},\qquad U^{ef}/U^{et},\qquad U/U^{ef}\]

occurring in the filtration (4.1.1) by tensoring with \(\mathbb F_\ell\). Of course, this filtration is stable under \(\Gamma\), and gives by additivity a decomposition of \(\operatorname{long}\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathbb Z_\ell(\Gamma)}(L,U(\ell))\) into a sum of three terms, the lengths of the groups

\[(*)\qquad \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathbb Z_\ell(\Gamma)}(L,V_i\otimes_{\mathbb Z_\ell}\mathbb F_\ell) \simeq (\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathbb Z_\ell(\Gamma)}(L,V_i))\otimes_{\mathbb Z_\ell}\mathbb F_\ell,\]

the isomorphism written in (*) resulting again from the projectivity hypothesis made on \(L\); we only suppose \(K'\) chosen large enough so that \(\Gamma\) acts on the \(V_i\). Now \(\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathbb Z_\ell(\Gamma)}(L,V_i)\) is evidently a torsion-free \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-module, hence its rank is equal to the right-hand side of (*). If \(\varphi_i\) is the character of the representation of \(\Gamma\) in \(V_i\), and \(\psi\) the character of the representation of \(\Gamma\) in \(L\), we thus find

\[(4.6.1)\qquad \operatorname{long}\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathbb Z_\ell(\Gamma)}(L,U(\ell)) =\sum_{1\leq i\leq3}N^{-1}\sum_{g\in\Gamma}\psi(g)\varphi_i(g) =N^{-1}\sum_{g\in\Gamma}\psi(g)\varphi(g),\quad\text{with }N=\operatorname{card}\Gamma,\]

where \(\varphi(g)=\sum\varphi_i(g)\) is also the function on \(\Gamma\) deduced by passage to the quotient from the function on the inertia group \(I\) which is the character of the monodromy representation of \(I\) on \(U\). Now by virtue of 4.3 a) this character is a function with rational integer values independent of \(\ell\). Taking \(L=\operatorname{Sw}\), the same holds for the character \(\psi(g)\) of \(L\), which is equal to \(a-N\delta+1\), where \(\delta\) is the "Dirac function" of \(\Gamma\), and \(a\) the "Artin conductor" of \(K'/K\) [26]. This therefore proves 4.6, while bringing out at the same time the following expression, independent of \(\ell\), for the non-tame conductor exponent:
\[(4.6.2)\qquad \alpha_s(A_K)=N^{-1}\sum_{g\in\Gamma}(a(g)-N\delta(g)+1)\,\varphi(g),\]

where \(\Gamma\) is the Galois group of a finite Galois extension \(K'\) of \(K\) which splits \(U^{ef}\) (for example the smallest such extension, cf. 4.1), \(a(g)\) the Artin conductor on \(\Gamma\), and \(\varphi(g)\) the trace function on \(\Gamma\) made explicit in (4.3.0). In the case of semi-stable reduction, we can take \(K'=K\), hence \(\Gamma=e\), and we find
\[(4.6.3)\qquad \alpha_s(A_K)=0\qquad\text{(case of semi-stable reduction)}.\]
To conclude, let us make explicit a criterion for semi-stable reduction which follows from the general reflections of 4.2:

Proposition 4.7. (Criterion of M. RAYNAUD for semi-stable reduction). Let \(S\) be a trait, \(A_K\) an abelian scheme defined over its field of functions \(K\), \(n\) an integer \(>1\) prime to the residue characteristic \(p\) (*), and such that \({}_nA_K\) is unramified with respect to \(S\), i.e. such that the inertia group acts trivially on \({}_nA(\overline K)\). Then either \(A_K\) has semi-stable reduction over \(S\), or \(n=2\), and if \(S\) is strictly local, \(A_K\) acquires semi-stable reduction over a finite Galois extension \(K'\) of \(K\) with Galois group isomorphic to \((\mathbb Z/2\mathbb Z)^r\).

We may evidently suppose \(S\) strictly local. With the notations of 4.1 and 4.2, let us take \(K'\) as small as possible, i.e. suppose that \(\Gamma\) acts faithfully on \(A_0^{*o}\). By hypothesis, \(\Gamma\) acts trivially on \({}_nA(\overline K)\), and a fortiori on \({}_nA(\overline K)^{ef}\simeq{}_nA_0^{*o}(\overline k)\supset{}_nA_0^{*o}(k)\). We then conclude by the two following lemmas:

Lemma 4.7.1. (J. P. SERRE). Let \(G\) be a commutative group scheme over a field \(k\), an extension of an abelian scheme by a torus, \(n\) an integer \(\geq2\). Then every automorphism of finite order of \(G\) inducing the identity on \({}_nG\) is reduced to the identity if \(n\ne2\), and is the identity or of order two if \(n=2\).
Lemma 4.7.2. A finite group of which every element \(\ne1\) is of order 2 is isomorphic to \((\mathbb Z/2\mathbb Z)^r\), for a suitable \(r\geq0\).

4.7.3. Let us recall the proof of 4.7.1, which is well known. We note that thanks to the fact that \(n\,\operatorname{id}_G\) is an epimorphism, the hypothesis that \(u\) induces the identity on \({}_nG\), i.e. that \(1-u\) induces 0 on \({}_nG\), is expressed by the condition that we have \(1-u=nv\) in the ring of endomorphisms of \(G\). To prove the announced conclusion, we are reduced (by decomposing the finite cyclic group generated by \(u\) into its \(p\)-primary components, for the various prime numbers \(p\) dividing the order of \(u\)) to the case where \(u\) is of order \(p^N\), a power of a prime number. Let \(E\) be the subring of \(\operatorname{End}(G)\) generated by \(v\), which is a quotient of \(\mathbb Z[t]/(t^{p^N}-1)\), hence of finite type over \(\mathbb Z\), and which moreover is torsion-free (this is a formal consequence of the fact that \(G\) is divisible). It therefore embeds in \(E_{\mathbb Q}=E\otimes_{\mathbb Z}\mathbb Q\), which appears as a quotient of \(\mathbb Q[T]/(T^{p^N}-1)\), which is a direct composite of fields that are finite extensions of \(\mathbb Q\). The same therefore holds for \(E_{\mathbb Q}\). Considering the images of \(E,u,v\) in the component fields \(K_i\) of \(E_{\mathbb Q}\), we are thus reduced to seeing that if \(K=\mathbb Q(u)\) is a field generated by a primitive \(p^n\)-th root of unity \(u\), and if \(n\) is an integer \(\geq2\) such that \(u-1=0\) mod \(n\) (in the ring of integers of \(K\)), then we have \(u=1\), or \(u=-1\) and \(n=2\). Now this follows at once from the well-known fact that if \(u\ne1\) i.e. \(m\geq1\), the ideal \((u-1)\) is prime and the ideal \((p)\) is a power of it, whose exponent is \(>1\) except if \(p=2,m=1\) i.e. if \(u=-1\). From the fact that \((u-1)\) is prime and divides \(p\) it follows indeed that \(n\geq2\) can divide \(u-1\) only if \(n=p\), and \(p\) divides \(u-1\) only if \((u-1)=(p)\) i.e. \(u=-1\), hence \(n=2\).

4.7.4. Let us prove 4.7.2. It suffices to prove that \(\Gamma\) is commutative. Now if \(x,y\in\Gamma\), we shall have \((xy)(xy)=1\), which can also be written, since \(x^2=1\), \(y^2=1\), \(xy=(xy)^{-1}=y^{-1}x^{-1}=yx\), qed.

Remark 4.7.5. The proof given in 4.7.3, together with 4.7.2, shows us that if \(G\) is a divisible commutative Group of a Topos, \(n\geq2\) an integer, and \(\Gamma\) a finite group acting faithfully on \(G\) and inducing the identity on \({}_nG\), then \(\Gamma\) is reduced to the identity if \(n\geq3\), and is isomorphic to a group \((\mathbb Z/2\mathbb Z)^r\) if \(n=2\).

pages.title: 5. The orthogonality theorem in the case \(\ell=p\) (semi-stable case). Duality of the abelian schemes \(B_o\) and \(B'_o\). Characterization of the fixed part. Criteria for good reduction, essential good reduction and semi-stable reduction.

blocks.html: 5. The orthogonality theorem in the case \(\ell=p\) (semi-stable case). Duality of the abelian schemes \(B_o\) and \(B'_o\). Characterization of the fixed part. Criteria for good reduction, essential good reduction and semi-stable reduction.
5.1. Let us place ourselves in the case 2.2.3 where \(S\) is henselian and \(A^o\) is \(\ell\)-divisible, so that the fixed part
\[T_\ell(A^o)^f\lhook\joinrel\longrightarrow T_\ell(A)\]

is defined as the Barsotti-Tate pro-\(\ell\)-group formed by the \(({}_{\ell^\nu}A^o)^f\). We shall now give a definition of the toric part (2.3.2) of \(T_\ell(A^o)\), independent of the hypothesis \(\ell\ne p\) made in 2.3, but assuming on the other hand that \(S\) is complete (*). For this, let us introduce for every integer \(n\geq0\) the local artinian scheme
\[(5.1.1)\qquad S_n=\operatorname{Spec}(V/\mathfrak m^{n+1}),\]

the \(n\)-th infinitesimal neighborhood of \(s\) in \(S\), and for every \(S\)-scheme \(X\) let \(X_n=X\times_S S_n\). Since \((A^o)_n=(A_n)^o\) is smooth over \(S_n\), it is known (SGA 3 IX 3.6 bis) that \(T_o\) is the special fiber of a unique subtorus
\[(5.1.2)\qquad T_n\subset A_n^o.\]

By the uniqueness of the \(T_n\), these are deduced from one another by the base changes \(S_{n'}\longrightarrow S_n\). In other words, they define a formal subtorus \(\widehat T\) of the group \(\widehat A^o\) (in the category of formal schemes over \(S\)), the formal completion of \(A^o\) along the special fiber:
\[(5.1.3)\qquad \widehat T\subset\widehat A^o.\]

Since the functor \(X\longmapsto\widehat X\) (formal completion along the special fiber) induces an equivalence between the category of finite schemes over \(S\) and that of finite formal schemes over \(S\) (EGA III 4.8), we shall simply identify a finite scheme over \(S\) with the corresponding formal scheme. With this identification, the definition of the "fixed parts" gives us at once, for every \(\nu\geq0\):
\[(5.1.4)\qquad ({}_{\ell^\nu}A^o)^f={}_{\ell^\nu}(\widehat A^o),\]
which can also be written as an identity of projective systems
\[(5.1.5)\qquad T_\ell(A^o)^f=T_\ell(\widehat A^o).\]

We thus obtain a Barsotti-Tate sub-pro-group of the fixed part of \(T_\ell(A^o)\) by taking \(T_\ell(\widehat T)\subset T_\ell(\widehat A^o)\). We shall then define the toric part of \(T_\ell(A^o)\) as
\[(5.1.6)\qquad T_\ell(A^o)^t\mathrel{\mathop=\limits^{\mathrm{dfn}}}T_\ell(\widehat T) \subset T_\ell(\widehat A^o)=T_\ell(A^o)^f.\]
Hence by definition, we shall have
\[(5.1.7)\qquad T_\ell(A^o)^t=({}_{\ell^\nu}\widehat T)_{\nu\geq0},\]
the finite flat \(S\)-group \({}_{\ell^\nu}\widehat T\) being itself described as the inductive limit of the finite flat \(S_n\)-groups
\[(5.1.8)\qquad {}_{\ell^\nu}(T_n),\quad n\text{ variable},\]
i.e. its affine algebra is described as the projective limit of the affine algebras of the groups (5.1.8). Of course, we shall have

which, for \(n=0\), is none other than the formula (2.3.3) by which we had characterized the toric part (as a pro-finite-étale group) in the case \(\ell\ne p\). This shows that the definition (5.1.6) is compatible with the one given in 2.3 when \(\ell\ne p\).
Passing to the generic fibers, the fixed and toric parts of \(T_\ell(A^o)\) define for us fixed and toric parts in \(T_\ell(A_K)\):
\[(5.1.10)\qquad T_\ell(A_K)^t\subset T_\ell(A_K)^f\subset T_\ell(A_K).\]
These are inclusions of Barsotti-Tate pro-\(\ell\)-groups. Let us consider the analogous filtration, corresponding to the dual abelian scheme \(A'_K\):
\[(5.1.11)\qquad T_\ell(A'_K)^t\subset T_\ell(A'_K)^f\subset T_\ell(A'_K).\]
We then have the following result, which generalizes 2.4 (at least in the case where \(S\) is complete, to which we could easily reduce ourselves in 2.4):

Theorem 5.2. Suppose that \(S\) is a complete trait, and let \(A_K\) be an abelian scheme over \(K\), \(A'_K\) the dual abelian scheme, \(\ell\) a prime number such that the connected Néron model \(A^o\) is \(\ell\)-divisible (cf. 2.2.1), so that we then have at our disposal the filtrations (5.1.10) and (5.1.11) of the pro-groups \(T_\ell(A_K)\) and \(T_\ell(A'_K)\). This granted, the toric parts are recovered in terms of the fixed parts by the formula
\[(5.2.1)\qquad T_\ell(A_K)^t=T_\ell(A_K)^f\cap\bigl(T_\ell(A'_K)^f\bigr)^\perp\]
and the analogous formula obtained by exchanging the roles of \(A_K\) and \(A'_K\), where the sign \(\perp\) denotes the orthogonal complement with respect to the canonical pairing (1.0.2).
Finally, when \(A_K\) has semi-stable reduction over \(S\) (which follows from the other hypotheses in the case \(\ell=p\)), then we even have the formula
\[(5.2.2)\qquad T_\ell(A_K)^t=\bigl(T_\ell(A'_K)^f\bigr)^\perp.\]

5.2.3. The meaning of formula (5.2.1) being quite clear if \(\ell\) is prime to the characteristic of \(K\) (where it can be interpreted by passing to the ordinary \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-modules, with action of \(\pi=\operatorname{Gal}(\overline K/K)\), formed by the \(\overline K\)-points of the pro-groups in play, as in 2.4), let us make it explicit in the general case, using the notion of the dual
\[(5.2.3.1)\qquad D(G)=\operatorname{Hom}(G,T_\ell(\mathbb G_m))\]

of a Barsotti-Tate pro-\(\ell\)-group \(G=(G(\nu))\), which by definition is the projective system of the Cartier duals \(D(G(\nu))\) (SGA 3 VII 3.3.1 and VIII 1), where the transition morphisms are defined in the evident way (by transposition from the inclusion morphisms between the \(G(\nu)\), for example). The duality theory of abelian varieties teaches us that the pairing mentioned in 5.2 in fact defines an isomorphism
\[(5.2.3.2)\qquad T_\ell(A'_K)\xrightarrow{\sim}D(T_\ell(A_K)),\]
whence by composition a morphism
\[(5.2.3.3)\qquad T_\ell(A_K)^f\longrightarrow D(T_\ell(A'_K)^f),\]

and formula (5.2.1) asserts that the kernel of the preceding homomorphism (which a priori is a sub-pro-group of the first term) is none other than the toric part \(T_\ell(A_K)^t\). We note that this is an isomorphism of pro-objects, and not of projective systems: we do not assert (and it would be false in general) that for every integer \(\nu\), \(T_\ell(A_K)^t(\nu)\) is equal to the kernel of \(T_\ell(A_K)^f(\nu)\longrightarrow D(T_\ell(A'_K)^f)(\nu)\); the latter may a priori be larger. (We shall see however below that it is not if \(A_K\) has semi-stable reduction, which is the case in particular, by the hypotheses made in 5.2, when \(\ell=p\)).
5.2.4. The proof of (5.2.1) merely repeats, practically without change, that of 2.4. We note that the induced pairing
\[(5.2.4.1)\qquad T_\ell(A_K)^f\times T_\ell(A'_K)^f\longrightarrow T_\ell(\mathbb G_{mK})\]
giving rise to (5.2.3.3) is induced by a pairing
\[(5.2.4.2)\qquad T_\ell(A^o)^f\times T_\ell(A'^o)^f\longrightarrow T_\ell(\mathbb G_{mS})\]
of Barsotti-Tate pro-groups over \(S\), itself induced by the pairing of pro-groups
\[(5.2.4.3)\qquad T_\ell(A^o)\times T_\ell(A'^o)\longrightarrow T_\ell(\mathbb G_{mS})\]
deduced from the biextension \(W\) of \((A^o,A'^o)\) by \(\mathbb G_{mS}\). Consequently, the pairing
\[(5.2.4.4)\qquad T_\ell(A_o^o)\times T_\ell(A_o'^o)\longrightarrow T_\ell(\mathbb G_{mk})\]

deduced from (5.2.4.2) by passage to the special fibers, is none other than the one deduced from the biextension \(W_o\) of \((A_o^o,A_o'^o)\) by \(\mathbb G_{mk}\). By virtue of VIII 4.11 and of 1.5, the left annihilator of the pairing (5.2.4.4) is therefore equal to \(T_\ell(T_o)\), which is none other than the special fiber of the toric part \(T_\ell(A^o)^t\). We therefore conclude, by returning to the definition of the left annihilators made explicit in 5.2.3, with the aid of the

Lemma 5.2.5. Let \(u:G\longrightarrow H\) be a homomorphism of Barsotti-Tate pro-\(\ell\)-groups over \(S\). If \(u_o:G_o\longrightarrow H_o\) is zero, then \(u\) is zero. If \(u_o:G_o\longrightarrow H_o\) is an isogeny (resp. an isomorphism), \(u\) is an isogeny (resp. an isomorphism).
Indeed, we shall apply the first assertion of 5.2.5 to the composite morphism
\[T_\ell(\widehat A^o)^t\longrightarrow T_\ell(\widehat A^o)^f\longrightarrow D(T_\ell(\widehat {A'^o})^f),\]

where the second morphism is the one deduced from the pairing (5.2.4.2); we find that this composite is zero, hence that the pairing under consideration vanishes on the toric part on the left. By symmetry, it therefore vanishes on the toric part on the right, hence it comes from a pairing on the quotients, or "abelian pieces"
\[T_\ell(\widehat A^o)^{ab}\times T_\ell(\widehat {A'^o})^{ab}\longrightarrow T_\ell(\mathbb G_m),\]
which quotients are again Barsotti-Tate pro-groups, as is easily verified. It remains to prove that the morphism corresponding to this pairing
\[T_\ell(\widehat A^o)^{ab}\longrightarrow D(T_\ell(\widehat {A'^o})^{ab})\]
is an isogeny, which again follows from 5.2.5.

We shall leave to the reader the (easy) proof of 5.2.5, which is a result valid over any connected noetherian base. We shall moreover find below (7.4.7), by a somewhat more precise analysis of the biextensions \(W_n\) of the \((A_n^o,A_n'^o)\), a rather evident direct proof of the two particular cases in which we have had to use 5.2.5.

5.2.6. Suppose now that \(A_K\) has semi-stable reduction over \(S\), so that we may take for \(\ell\) any prime number (distinct or not from \(p\)). Moreover, by virtue of the Galois criterion of semi-stable reduction 3.5, we have for \(\ell\ne p\)
\[(5.2.7)\qquad (T_\ell(A'_K)^f)^\perp\;(=T_\ell(A_K)^t)\subset T_\ell(A_K)^f.\]
It remains to prove, in order to finish the proof of 5.2, that this inclusion is valid without restriction on \(\ell\).

Indeed, the left-hand side is canonically isomorphic to \(D(T_\ell(A'_K)/T_\ell(A'_K)^f)\), and is therefore of "rank" (or "height") equal to \(2n-2\alpha-\mu=\mu\) (notations of (2.1.9), where by hypothesis \(\lambda=0\)), which is also that of \(T_\ell(A_K)^t\). Since the inclusion
\[(*)\qquad T_\ell(A_K)^t\subset T_\ell(A_K)\]

is strict, in the sense that it already induces a monomorphism for all the terms of the projective systems under consideration, it follows easily that the first member of (*) is maximal among the Barsotti-Tate sub-pro-groups of \(T_\ell(A_K)\) having the same rank as itself, so that we indeed have the equality of (5.2.7), which is equivalent to the inclusion between the extreme terms of 5.2.7 by the orthogonality theorem.

5.3. Using the correspondence by orthogonality between strict Barsotti-Tate sub-pro-groups (in the sense made precise above) of the dual pro-groups \(T_\ell(A_K)\) and \(T_\ell(A'_K)\), we deduce formally from (5.2.2) that the pairing (1.0.2) between these pro-groups induces a pairing
\[(5.3.2)\qquad T_\ell(A_K)^{ab}\times T_\ell(A'_K)^{ab}\longrightarrow T_\ell(\mathbb G_{mK})\]

which is a perfect duality, i.e. identifying each of the two factors of the first member with the dual of the other (in the sense of 5.2.2). (NB the exponents ab denote the "abelian parts", the quotient of the fixed part by the toric part). It is clear on the other hand that (5.3.2) is induced by the pairing
\[(5.3.3)\qquad T_\ell(A^o)^{ab}\times T_\ell(A'^o)^{ab}\longrightarrow T_\ell(\mathbb G_{mS})\]
deduced by passage to the quotients from the pairing (5.2.4.2). The latter being a perfect duality on the generic fibers, i.e. the corresponding homomorphism
\[(5.3.4)\qquad T_\ell(A^o)^{ab}\longrightarrow D(T_\ell(A'^o)^{ab})\]

being an isomorphism on the generic fibers, we evidently wish to deduce from this that the same holds over all of \(S\), or what amounts to the same thing, on the special fibers. This deduction is valid, trivially, if \(\ell\ne p\); it is likewise valid, at least in the case of unequal characteristics, if \(\ell=p\), thanks to a deep theorem of TATE [33, th.4]. We shall however be able to establish it directly, without the aid of Tate's theorem, below (7.4.6).
To make precise the geometric meaning of the result we wish to establish, note that the pairing deduced from (5.3.3) by passage to the special fibers is none other than the pairing
\[(5.3.5)\qquad T_\ell(B_o)\times T_\ell(B'_o)\longrightarrow T_\ell(\mathbb G_{mk})\]

deduced from the biextension \(W_o\) of \((A_o^o,A_o'^o)\) by passage to the quotient, \(B_o\) and \(B'_o\) denoting respectively the abelian parts of \(A_o^o\) and \(A_o'^o\). Since, by virtue of VIII 4.8, the biextension \(W_o\) comes from a biextension \(W_o^{ab}\) of \((B_o,B'_o)\) by \(\mathbb G_{mk}\), determined up to unique isomorphism, the pairing (5.3.5) can also be interpreted as deduced from the divisorial correspondence \(W_o^{ab}\), and the corresponding homomorphism
\[(5.3.6)\qquad T_\ell(B_o)\longrightarrow D(T_\ell(B'_o))\xrightarrow{\sim}T_\ell(D(B'_o))\]
(where in the last term, the letter \(D\) denotes the dual abelian scheme) is none other than the one deduced by functoriality of \(T_\ell\) from the homomorphism
\[(5.3.7)\qquad B_o\longrightarrow D(B'_o)\]

defined by \(W_o^{ab}\). Hence the fact that (5.3.5) is a perfect duality also means, by the well-known dictionary (cf. [5]), that the kernel of (5.3.7), which is a finite group over \(k\) by virtue of 1.5, has zero \(\ell\)-primary component. To say that this is so for every \(\ell\) therefore means that the isogeny (5.3.7) is an isomorphism, i.e. that the divisorial correspondence \(W_o^{ab}\) is a perfect duality between \(B_o\) and \(B'_o\). The preceding considerations show only that the kernel of (5.3.7) is a \(p\)-group. Let us therefore state the more complete result:

Theorem 5.4. Let \(S\) be a trait, and \(A_K\) an abelian scheme over \(S\) having semi-stable reduction over \(S\). Let us denote by \(B_o,B'_o\) the abelian parts of the Néron models of \(A_K\), and of the dual abelian scheme \(A'_K\), respectively. Then the canonical divisorial correspondence \(W_o^{ab}\) on \((B_o,B'_o)\), deduced by VIII 4.8 from the canonical biextension \(W_o\) of \((A_o^o,A_o'^o)\) (the special fiber of the biextension \(W\) of 1.4), is a perfect duality between \(B_o\) and \(B'_o\).
i.e. defines an isomorphism of each of these abelian schemes with the dual of the other.

The proof of 5.4 in the general case will be given in no. 7, as a consequence of a most natural and important construction due to RAYNAUD. Let us only note here that to prove 5.4, we may suppose \(S\) complete, taking 3.3 into account.
5.5. To express conveniently the extreme factors in the filtrations (5.1.10) and (5.1.11), in the case of semi-stable reduction, let us introduce the twisted constant group schemes over \(S\)
\[(5.5.1)\qquad \underline{M}'_o=D(T_o),\qquad \underline{M}_o=D(T'_o),\]

duals of the maximal tori \(T_o,T'_o\) of the special fibers of \(A,A'\) (SGA 3 X 5.7). Since \(S\) is local and complete, these group schemes can be considered as the special fibers of twisted constant schemes
\[(5.5.2)\qquad \underline{M}',\ \underline{M}\text{ over }S\ ;\]
thus, if the torus \(T_o\) is split (for example if \(k\) is separably closed), i.e. if \(\underline{M}'_o\) is the constant group defined by the ordinary group
\[(5.5.3)\qquad M'=\operatorname{Hom}_{k\text{-gr}}(T_o,\mathbb G_{mk}),\]
then \(\underline{M}'\) is the constant group defined by \(M'\):
\[\underline{M}'=M'_S.\]
We may moreover note that, since \(T_o\) and \(T'_o\) are isogenous (2.2.6), \(T'_o\) is split if \(T_o\) is, so that it is appropriate to introduce also in this case
\[(5.5.4)\qquad M=\operatorname{Hom}_{k\text{-gr}}(T'_o,\mathbb G_{mk}),\]
and we shall then have
\[\underline{M}=M_S.\]
This granted, we shall evidently have, for every integer \(n\), isomorphisms
\[(5.5.5)\qquad \underline{M}'_n\xrightarrow{\sim}D(T_n),\qquad \underline{M}_n\xrightarrow{\sim}D(T'_n),\]
where \(T'_n\) is defined in terms of \(A'\) as \(T_n\) is in terms of \(A\) (5.1.2), from which we deduce canonical isomorphisms (where we set \(\mathbb Z_\ell(1)=T_\ell(\mathbb G_{mS})\)):
\[(5.5.6)\qquad T_\ell(T_n)\xrightarrow{\sim}\underline{M}'_n\otimes_{\mathbb Z}\mathbb Z_\ell(1),\qquad T_\ell(T'_n)\xrightarrow{\sim}\underline{M}_n\otimes_{\mathbb Z}\mathbb Z_\ell(1),\]
and by passage to the limit over \(n\) canonical isomorphisms:

\[(5.5.7)\qquad \left\{\begin{aligned} T_\ell(A^o)^t=T_\ell(\widehat T)&\xrightarrow{\sim}\underline{M}'\otimes\mathbb Z_\ell(1),\\ T_\ell(A'^o)^t=T_\ell(\widehat T')&\xrightarrow{\sim}\underline{M}\otimes\mathbb Z_\ell(1). \end{aligned}\right.\]
In the case of semi-stable reduction, we conclude from this by duality, using the orthogonality theorem 5.2 and the relation (5.2.2), canonical isomorphisms:

\[(5.5.8)\qquad \left\{\begin{aligned} T_\ell(A_K)/T_\ell(A_K)^f&\xrightarrow{\sim}\underline{M}_K\otimes_{\mathbb Z}\mathbb Z_\ell,\\ T_\ell(A'_K)/T_\ell(A'_K)^f&\xrightarrow{\sim}\underline{M}'_K\otimes_{\mathbb Z}\mathbb Z_\ell. \end{aligned}\right.\]
We find likewise, thanks to the same formula (5.2.2), canonical isomorphisms:
\[(5.5.9)\qquad \left\{\begin{aligned} T_\ell(A_K)/T_\ell(A_K)^t&\xrightarrow{\sim}D(T_\ell(A'_K)^f),\\ T_\ell(A'_K)/T_\ell(A'_K)^t&\xrightarrow{\sim}D(T_\ell(A_K)^f), \end{aligned}\right.\]

where we shall recall that the groups \(T_\ell(A_K)^f\) and \(T_\ell(A'_K)^f\) which appear in the second member are the restrictions to \(\eta\) of Barsotti-Tate pro-\(\ell\)-groups defined over \(S\), \(T_\ell(A^o)^f\) and \(T_\ell(A'^o)^f\) respectively; the letter \(D\) denotes here the duals in the sense of Barsotti-Tate pro-groups, namely \(\operatorname{Hom}(-,\mathbb Z_\ell(1))\). Thus:

Proposition 5.6. Suppose \(S\) complete and that \(A_K\) has semi-stable reduction over \(S\). Then for every prime number \(\ell\) (which may be equal to the residue characteristic of \(S\)), the Barsotti-Tate pro-\(\ell\)-group \(T_\ell(A_K)^t\) is the generic fiber of a Barsotti-Tate pro-group over \(S\), "of toric type", given by (5.5.7), while \(T_\ell(A_K)/T_\ell(A_K)^f\) is the generic fiber of a Barsotti-Tate pro-group over \(S\) of pro-étale type, given by (5.5.8). Finally the groups \(T_\ell(A_K)^f\) and \(T_\ell(A_K)/T_\ell(A_K)^t\) are the generic fibers of Barsotti-Tate pro-groups defined over \(S\), equal respectively to \(T_\ell(A^o)^f\) and to \(D(T_\ell(A'^o)^f)=\operatorname{Hom}(T_\ell(A'^o)^f,\mathbb Z_\ell(1))\).

5.6.1. We note that for the étale covering \(S'\) it amounts to the same to split \(T_\ell(A_K)^t\) or to split \(T_\ell(A_K)/T_\ell(A_K)^f\), for this means respectively that \(S'\) splits the twisted constant groups \(\underline M\) and \(\underline M'\); now these are isogenous, since \(T_o\) and \(T'_o\) are. We see at the same time that there exists a smallest étale extension \(S'\) of \(S\) which does the job.
Let us also point out the following easy consequence of the definitions of 2.2.3:

Proposition 5.7. Suppose \(A^o\) \(\ell\)-divisible, \(S\) henselian, let \(F_K\) be a constructible \(\ell\)-adic sheaf on \(K\) which is "unramified", i.e. which extends to a twisted constant \(\ell\)-adic sheaf \(F\) on \(S\), and let \(u:F_K\longrightarrow T_\ell(A_K)\) be a homomorphism of pro-groups; then \(u\) factors into a homomorphism from \(F\) into \(T_\ell(A_K)^f\).
Indeed, we shall have
\[(5.7.1)\qquad u=(u( u))_{ u\geq0},\quad ext{with }u( u):F_K( u)\longrightarrow T_\ell(A_K)( u)={}_{\ell^ u}A_K.\]
By the universal property of \(A\), since \(F(\nu)\) is étale hence smooth over \(S\), the morphism \(u(\nu)\) comes from a homomorphism
\[v( u):F( u)\longrightarrow{}_{\ell^ u}A,\]
and by definition of the fixed part, this homomorphism factors as
\[v( u):F( u)\longrightarrow({}_{\ell^ u}A)^f.\]

Since the image of the morphism induced on the special fibers falls into the infinitely \(\ell\)-divisible part of \(A_o\), we conclude that \(v( u)\) factors even through \(({}_{\ell^ u}A^o)^f\). By passage to the limit over \( u\), we find a homomorphism
\[v:F\longrightarrow T_\ell(A^o)^f,\]
and the morphism induced on the generic fibers evidently gives the desired factorization.

Remarks 5.7.2. When \(\ell\ne p\), it is clear that the property 5.7 of the sub-pro-group \(T_\ell(A_K)^f\) of \(T_\ell(A_K)\) characterizes the latter uniquely, as the largest \(\ell\)-adic subsheaf of \(T_\ell(A_K)\) which is "unramified". When \(\ell=p\), the statement 5.7 ceases to be really reasonable, and we shall give a property distinctly stronger than the one stated in 5.7, which can then serve as an intrinsic characterization of the fixed part of \(T_p(A_K)\) (in terms of the Barsotti-Tate pro-group structure of \(T_p(A_K)\) alone), — at least in the case of unequal characteristics:

Theorem 5.8. (M. RAYNAUD). Let \(A_K\) be an abelian scheme with semi-stable reduction over the field of functions \(K\) of a henselian trait \(S\). Let \(\ell\) be a prime number (the interesting case being that where \(\ell=p\)), and consider the Barsotti-Tate pro-\(\ell\)-group \(X_\eta=T_\ell(A_K)\) and its "fixed part" \(X_\eta^f\), which is the generic fiber of the Barsotti-Tate pro-\(\ell\)-group \(X^f=T_\ell(A^o)^f\). Suppose, if \(\ell=p\), that we have \(\operatorname{car}.K=0\) (so that we have at our disposal over \(S\) the theorem of TATE [33, th.4]). Let \(F\) be a Barsotti-Tate pro-\(\ell\)-group over \(S\). Then every homomorphism \(F_\eta\longrightarrow X_\eta\) factors through \(X_\eta^f\).

Passing to the ind-\(\ell\)-groups associated with the Barsotti-Tate pro-\(\ell\)-groups under consideration, the preceding statement becomes equivalent to the following, thanks to the cited theorem of TATE:
Corollary 5.9. If \(F\) is a Barsotti-Tate ind-\(\ell\)-group, then every homomorphism \(u_\eta:F_\eta\longrightarrow A_\eta\) extends (in a unique way) to a homomorphism \(u:F\longrightarrow A\).

In other words, the characteristic "Néronian" property (1.1.2) of the Néron model remains valid for homomorphisms of Barsotti-Tate ind-group schemes, without any smoothness hypothesis on the latter. (Only the case of ind-étale groups would follow directly from the definition.)

Remark 5.9.1. In fact, M. RAYNAUD proves 5.9 without supposing that \(A_K\) has semi-stable reduction, and even for every Néronian group scheme \(A\) over \(S\) (whose generic fiber need not be an abelian scheme); of course, it is likewise unnecessary in the formulation 5.9 that \(S\) be henselian. RAYNAUD's proof of this more general case proceeds by reduction to the particular case considered in 5.8, using an unpublished property in the construction of Néron models. We shall confine ourselves here to giving RAYNAUD's proof of 5.9.

Proof of 5.9. We know [33] that \(F\) is an extension of a Barsotti-Tate group \(F'\) which is ind-étale by a Barsotti-Tate group \(F''\) with connected special fiber, so that for every integer \(n\), we have an exact sequence
\[0\longrightarrow F''(n)\longrightarrow F(n)\longrightarrow F'(n)\longrightarrow0,\]
with \(F'(n)\) connected and \(F''(n)_o\) purely infinitesimal. The given homomorphism \(u_\eta\) is defined by a sequence of homomorphisms
\[u_\eta(n):F(n)_\eta\longrightarrow A_\eta,\]

and everything comes down to extending these separately to \(u(n):F(n)\longrightarrow A\). Lemma 5.9.2 below then reduces us to proving that the homomorphism \(u''_\eta:F''_\eta\longrightarrow A_\eta\) induced by \(u_\eta\) extends to \(u'':F''\longrightarrow A\). Consequently, we may suppose that \(F\) has connected special fiber.
We may consider \(u_\eta\) as a homomorphism from \(F_\eta\) into the Barsotti-Tate ind-\(\ell\)-group \(Y_\eta\) associated with \(X_\eta=T_\ell(A_K)\), and then consider the composite
\[(*)\qquad v_\eta:F_\eta\longrightarrow Y_\eta\longrightarrow Y_\eta/Y_\eta^f.\]

By virtue of 5.6 this last group extends to an ind-étale Barsotti-Tate group \(Z\) over \(S\) (namely \(\underline M\otimes_{\mathbb Z}(\mathbb Q_\ell/\mathbb Z_\ell)\)). By virtue of the theorem of TATE cited in 5.8, the homomorphism (*) then extends to a homomorphism
\[v:F\longrightarrow Z.\]

Since \(F\) has connected special fiber and \(Z\) is ind-étale, this morphism is zero. Hence the same holds for \(v_\eta\), hence \(u_\eta\) factors through \(Y_\eta^f\). This is what had to be proved!
It remains to prove the

Lemma 5.9.2. Let \(S\) be a trait, \(A\) a Néronian group scheme over \(S\) (1.1.2),

\[1\longrightarrow G'\longrightarrow G\longrightarrow G''\longrightarrow1\]

an extension of group schemes over \(S\), with \(G''\) smooth,

\[u:G'\longrightarrow A\]

a homomorphism of groups, and

\[v_\eta:G_\eta\longrightarrow A_\eta\]

a homomorphism of groups extending \(u_\eta\). Suppose that the extension of \(G''\) by \(A\), the image of the extension \(G\) by \(u\), is representable (*), — which is the case for example if \(G\to G''\) admits a section locally for the "faithfully free" topology (cf. SGA 3 IV 6.3) and if \(A\) is quasi-projective over \(S\). Then there exists a unique group morphism

\[v:G\longrightarrow A\]

extending \(v_\eta\) and \(u\).

Indeed, the last hypothesis made reduces us to the case where \(G'=A\), \(u=\) is the identity, and where \(v_\eta:G_\eta\longrightarrow A_\eta\) is thus a projection, which we propose to extend to a projection \(v:G\longrightarrow A\). Now \(G\) is smooth, being an extension of two smooth group schemes, so that the conclusion follows from the definition of the Néronian property of \(A\).

Corollary 5.10. Let \(A_K\) be an abelian scheme over the field of functions \(K\) of a trait \(S\), \(\ell\) a prime number. When \(\ell=p\), we suppose \(\operatorname{car}.K=0\), in order to have at our disposal the theorem of TATE already invoked in 5.8. In order that \(A_K\) have good reduction over \(S\), it is necessary and sufficient that the Barsotti-Tate pro-\(\ell\)-group \(T_\ell(A_K)\) "have good reduction over \(S\)", i.e. extend to a Barsotti-Tate group over \(S\).
(This is the result conjectured in [8, 4.9].)

The necessity being clear, it remains to prove the sufficiency; note moreover that the case \(\ell\ne p\) has already been treated (2.2.9). We may suppose \(S\) henselian (2.2.9). Suppose first that \(A_K\) has semi-stable reduction over \(S\). Then 5.8 applied to \(F=X\) shows us that we have \[T_\ell(A_K)=T_\ell(A_K)^f,\] which also means that the maximal torus of \(A_o\) is zero, i.e. that \(A_o^o\) is an abelian scheme, so that the conclusion follows from (2.2.9 (iiio)). Let us now treat the general case. By virtue of 2.2.9 we may evidently suppose \(S\) strictly local. Let \(K'\) be a finite Galois extension such that \(A_{K'}\) has semi-stable reduction over the normalization \(S'\) of \(S\) in \(K'\) (3.6). If \(A^*\) denotes the corresponding Néron model, \(G=\operatorname{Gal}(K'/K)\) acts on the situation \((A^*,S')\), hence on \(A_o^{*o}\) and by what we saw in 4.1, it suffices to prove that \(G\) acts trivially on the latter, which will indeed imply that \(A_K\) has semi-stable reduction, i.e. that we are in the case already treated. Now let \(X\) be a Barsotti-Tate group over \(S\) which extends \(X_\eta=T_\ell(A_K)\). By virtue of the uniqueness theorem of TATE already invoked, there exists a (unique) isomorphism
\[X\times_S S'\xrightarrow{\sim}T_\ell(A^{*o})\]

which induces the identity on the generic fibers. By transport of structure, this isomorphism is compatible with the operations of \(G\). Now it is clear that \(G\) acts trivially on the special fiber of \(X\times_S S'\), hence it acts trivially on \(T_\ell(A_o^{*o})\), from which it evidently follows that it acts trivially on \(A_o^{*o}\), which completes the proof.

Remark 5.11. Once the theorem of TATE is proved without any restrictive hypothesis on \(\operatorname{car}.K\), the preceding results 5.8, 5.9, 5.10, as well as 5.13 below, would evidently be established without such a restriction.
Let us set down a definition, which may also be useful elsewhere:

Definition 5.12. Let \(S\) be a henselian trait, \(\ell\) a prime number, \(X_\eta\) a Barsotti-Tate pro-\(\ell\)-group over the generic point \(\eta\) of \(S\). We say that \(X\) has good reduction over \(S\) if \(X\) extends to a Barsotti-Tate group over \(S\) (cf. 5.10), and that it has virtual good reduction over \(S\) if there exists a filtration \((\operatorname{Fil}^i(X_\eta))_{i\geq0}\) of \(X\) by strict Barsotti-Tate subgroups (*) such that the Barsotti-Tate groups \(\operatorname{Gr}^i(X_\eta)=\operatorname{Fil}^i(X_\eta)/\operatorname{Fil}^{i+1}(X_\eta)\) have good reduction over \(S\); if the filtration can be chosen so as to be of length \(\leq n\) (i.e. so that at most \(n\) among the \(\operatorname{Gr}^i\) are \(\ne0\)), we say that \(X\) has virtual good reduction of level \(n\) over \(S\). Finally we say that \(X\) has essential good reduction over \(S\) (resp. essential virtual good reduction, resp. essential virtual good reduction of level \(n\)) if there exists a finite extension \(K'\) such that \(X_{K'}\) has good reduction (resp. virtual good reduction, resp. virtual good reduction of level \(n\)) over the normalization \(S'\) in \(K'\).

5.12.1. When \(\ell\ne p\), so that the category of Barsotti-Tate pro-\(\ell\)-groups over \(\eta\) resp. over \(S\) can be interpreted in terms of \(\ell\)-adic representations of \(\pi=\pi_1(\eta)\) resp. of \(\pi_o=\pi_1(S)\), we see at once that the six notions introduced are expressed in terms of the natural representation of the inertia group \(I\) on \(X_\eta(\bar K)\), by the property, respectively, that the latter be trivial, resp. unipotent, resp. unipotent of level \(n\), resp. essentially trivial, resp. essentially unipotent, resp. essentially unipotent of level \(n\).

5.12.2. Suppose we take \(X_\eta=T_\ell(A_K)\), where \(A_K\) is an abelian scheme over \(K\). We saw in 5.6 that if \(A_K\) has semi-stable reduction over \(S\), then \(X_\eta\) has virtual good reduction of level 2 (and we shall see a converse in a moment); hence by virtue of the semi-stable reduction theorem 3.6, \(X_\eta\) has essential virtual good reduction of level 2. It is appropriate to regard this statement as giving the "right" formulation of the geometric monodromy theorem III, in the case of the \(H^1\) of a proper smooth scheme over \(K\), without excluding the case \(\ell=p\) (which was not considered in loc. cit.). It would also be appropriate to reformulate the theorem
(or rather, for the moment, the conjecture) of geometric monodromy for the higher \(H^i\), given in III, so as to take into account "\(p\)-adic" coefficients, as was said in 0.4.
Let us give a recapitulative statement of the relations between the reduction properties of abelian schemes over \(K\) and of the associated Barsotti-Tate groups:

Proposition 5.13. Let \(S\) be a henselian trait, \(A_K\) an abelian scheme over its field of fractions, \(\ell\) a prime number. When \(\ell=p\), we suppose that \(K\) is of char. zero (cf. 5.11).

a) In order that \(A_K\) have good reduction over \(S\) (2.2.9), it is necessary and sufficient that \(T_\ell(A_K)\) have good reduction over \(S\) (5.12).

b) In order that \(A_K\) have essential good reduction over \(S\), i.e. that there exist a finite extension \(K'\) of \(K\) such that \(A_{K'}\) has good reduction over the normalization \(S'\) of \(S\) in \(K'\), it is necessary and sufficient that \(T_\ell(A_K)\) have essential good reduction over \(S\) (5.12). We may then take for \(K'\) a finite separable extension of \(K\).

c) The following conditions are equivalent:

1) \(A_K\) has semi-stable reduction over \(S\) (3.4).

2) \(T_\ell(A_K)\) has virtual good reduction of level 2 over \(S\) (5.12).

3) \(T_\ell(A_K)\) has virtual good reduction over \(S\).
Proof. Case a) is none other than 5.10 and has been included for the record.

b) The first assertion is a trivial consequence of a) and the definitions. To prove the last assertion in b), we may suppose that \(\ell\ne p\), but then thanks to a) it suffices to take for \(K'\) a finite separable extension of \(K\) corresponding to an open subgroup \(\pi'\) of \(\pi=\operatorname{Gal}(\bar K/K)\) whose intersection with the inertia group \(I\) is contained in the kernel of the representation of \(I\) on \(T_\ell(A_K(\bar K))\), which is open by hypothesis (cf. 5.12.1).

c) We have already pointed out that 1) \(\Longrightarrow\) 2) (5.12.2), it is trivial that 2) \(\Longrightarrow\) 3), and it remains to prove that 3) \(\Longrightarrow\) 1). For \(\ell\ne p\), this is none other than the Galois criterion of semi-stable reduction 3.5. For \(\ell=p\), we may proceed as follows. Let us choose a prime number \(\ell'\ne p\), and apply to it the considerations of 4.1. Suppose \(S\) strictly local, which is permissible (3.4.0), and take a finite Galois extension \(K'\) of \(K\), with group \(G\), such that \(A_{K'}\) has semi-stable reduction. We must prove that the representation of \(G\) on the three quotients occurring in (4.1.1) is trivial, or what amounts to the same thing, that the sum representation is trivial, or again, that the trace of the latter is "trivial", i.e. constant. Now we saw in 4.3 a) that this trace function is independent of the choice of \(\ell'\ne p\), and in 6.4 below we indicate a method of computing this function in terms of \(T_p(A_K)\) as well. This computation trivially gives a constant function in the case where \(T_p(A_K)\) has virtual good reduction, and this completes the proof.

Remark 5.13.1. It seems plausible that if \(X_\eta\) is a Barsotti-Tate pro-\(\ell\)-group over \(\eta\), which has virtual good reduction over \(S\), and essential virtual good reduction of level \(n\), then it has virtual good reduction of level \(n\). (This is true for \(\ell\ne p\).)
The following result, an easy consequence of 5.7 and of the semi-stable reduction theorem 3.6, was pointed out to me by J.P. SERRE:

Proposition 5.14. (Criterion of essential good reduction.) Let \(S\) be a trait with field of functions \(K\), \(A_K\) an abelian scheme over \(K\), \(\ell\) a prime number distinct from the characteristic of \(K\).

a) Suppose \(\ell\ne p=\operatorname{car}.k\). Then the following conditions are equivalent:

(i) \(A_K\) has essentially good reduction over \(S\), i.e. there exists a finite separable extension \(K'\) of \(K\) such that \(A_{K'}\) has good reduction relative to the normalization \(S'\) of \(S\) in \(K'\).

(ii) The action of the inertia group \(I\) on \(U_\ell=T_\ell(A(\bar K))\) is essentially trivial, i.e. is trivial on an open subgroup of \(I\), i.e. takes place through a finite quotient group \(\Gamma\) of \(I\).

(iii) The action of the inertia group \(I\) on \(U_\ell\otimes_{\mathbb Z_\ell}\mathbb Q_\ell\) is semi-simple.

b) Suppose \(\ell=p\); then, if \(n=\dim A_K\ne0\), the image of \(I\) in the group of automorphisms of \(U_\ell\) is not finite; more precisely, its image in the group \(\mathbb Z_\ell^*\) of automorphisms of \({}^{2n}\!\bigwedge U_\ell\) is of finite index. In order that \(A_K\) have essentially good reduction, it suffices (but it is by no means necessary) that the action of \(I\) on \(U_\ell\otimes_{\mathbb Z_\ell}\mathbb Q_\ell\) be semi-simple.

Proof. In the case \(\ell\ne p\), the equivalence of (i) and (ii) is a trivial consequence of the criterion of good reduction 2.2.9, and (ii) implies (iii) since \(\mathbb Q_\ell\) is of characteristic zero. The homomorphism \[\pi=\operatorname{Gal}(\bar K/K)\longrightarrow\mathbb Z_\ell^*\simeq\operatorname{Aut}({}^{2n}\!\bigwedge U_\ell)\] is equal to \(\chi^n\), where \(\chi\) is induced by the character \[\mathbb Z_\ell^*=\operatorname{Aut}(\mathbb Z_\ell(1))\] coming from the action of \(I\) on the \(\ell^\nu\)-th roots of unity, and we know that if \(\ell=p\), the image of \(I\) under this character is indeed of finite index [33]; the fact that the action of \(I\) on \(U_p\otimes\mathbb Q_p\) may fail to be semi-simple, even if \(A_K\) is an elliptic curve with good reduction, is seen in [28, IV A 2.4]. It remains to prove that (whatever \(\ell\) may be) (iii) implies (i). For this, we use the semi-stable reduction theorem 3.6, which allows us to reduce at once to the case where \(A_K\) has semi-stable reduction over \(S\) (since the hypothesis (iii) is preserved on replacing \(K\) by a finite separable extension \(K'\), i.e. \(I\) by an open subgroup \(I'\)). When \(\ell\ne p\), the action of \(I\) on \(U_\ell\otimes\mathbb Q_\ell\) is unipotent \(3.5 (v)\) and semi-simple, hence trivial, and we win by the criterion of good reduction 2.2.9. Let us give a proof valid without restriction on \(\ell\), and which gives a slightly more precise result:

Corollary 5.15. The notations being those of 5.14, suppose that \(A_K\) has semi-stable reduction over \(S\), and that the fixed part \((U_\ell\otimes\mathbb Q_\ell)^f\mathrel{\mathop=\limits^{\mathrm{dfn}}}(U_\ell^f)\otimes\mathbb Q_\ell\) admits a complement in \(U_\ell\otimes\mathbb Q_\ell\) stable under \(I\). Then \(A_K\) has good reduction.

It suffices to prove that under these conditions, we have \(U_\ell=U_\ell^f\), which by 5.6 indeed means that the toric part \(T_o\) of \(A_o^o\) is zero, hence that \(A_o^o\) is an abelian scheme, which indeed implies that \(A\) is an abelian scheme (2.2.9). We may evidently suppose \(k\) separably closed. Then the intersection of the complement postulated in 5.15 with \(U_\ell\) defines a sub-pro-\(\ell\)-group \(F_K\) of \(T_\ell(A_K)\), isogenous to the cotoric part \(T_\ell(A_K)/T_\ell(A_K)^f\). Since the latter is proétale and unramified by virtue of 5.6, the same holds for \(F\), and we conclude by 5.7 that \(F_K\) is contained in \(T_\ell(A_K)^f\), hence that \(F_K=0\), which indeed proves that \(U_\ell\) is equal to its fixed part.

pages.title: 6. Remarks on the construction of the toric part and the fixed part of \(T_p(A)\) in the case of non-semi-stable reduction.

blocks.html: 6. Remarks on the construction of the toric part and the fixed part of \(T_p(A)\) in the case of non-semi-stable reduction.

6.1. The construction of the toric part of \(T_p(A_K)\) given in 5.1 retains a meaning as soon as \(S\) is complete, without supposing \(A^o\) \(\ell\)-divisible, i.e. (in the case \(p=\ell\)) without supposing that we are in the case of semi-stable reduction (a hypothesis which had served only to enable us to formulate the orthogonality theorem 5.2). We can moreover construct \(T_p(A_K)^t\) assuming only \(S\) henselian, by showing that the toric part \(T_p(A_{\widehat K})^t\) over the completion, constructed by the method of 5.1, comes from a Barsotti-Tate sub-pro-group of \(T_p(A_K)\) (evidently determined in a unique way). More precisely, one manages to construct a Barsotti-Tate \(p\)-pro-group \(T_p(A)^t\) over \(S\) and a homomorphism \(T_p(A)^t\longrightarrow T_p(A)\) of projective systems, which is a monomorphism term by term, and which by inverse image over \(\widehat S\) gives the analogous objects constructed by the method of 5.1. This is seen by paraphrasing the construction of 5.1, but instead of lifting the maximal torus of \(T_o\) of the whole of \(A_o\), we confine ourselves, for every integer \(\nu\geq0\), to lifting the subgroup \({}_{p^\nu}T_o\) to a

subgroup of multiplicative type \(({}_{p^\nu}A_o^o)^t\) of \({}_{p^\nu}A_o^o\). For the existence and uniqueness of such a lifting, we note that the functor \((\operatorname{Sch}/S)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Ens}\), \[S'\longmapsto\text{set of finite subgroups of multiplicative type of }A^o_{S'},\] is representable by a scheme \(X\) of finite type over \(S\) (cf. the proof of SGA 3 XI 3.12 a)) which is étale over \(S\) by virtue of SGA 3 IX 3.6 bis; there is consequently one and only one section of \(X\) over \(S\) which extends a given section of \(X_s\) over \(s\), which proves our assertion. This reasoning shows moreover, more generally, that for every finite subgroup of multiplicative type \(H_o\) of \(A_o\), there is a unique finite subgroup of multiplicative type \(H\) of \(A\) which extends it; consequently, for every Barsotti-Tate sub-\(p\)-group \(H_o\) of \(A_o\) which is toroidal (i.e. whose components are of multiplicative type), there exists a unique Barsotti-Tate sub-\(p\)- group \(H\) of \(A\) which extends it, and which consequently furnishes a Barsotti-Tate sub-\(p\)-group of \({}_{p^\infty}A_K\), or, if one prefers, a Barsotti-Tate sub-\(p\)-pro-group of \(T_p(A_K)\). This procedure applies in particular to the case where \(H_o\) is the largest toroidal Barsotti-Tate sub-\(p\)-group of \(A_o\), which is obtained by taking the largest \(p\)-divisible subgroup \(\overline A_o\) of \(A_o^o\) (= \(p^N A_o^o\) for \(N\) large), and the "toroidal part" of \({}_{p^\infty}\overline A_o\) (defined for example thanks to SGA 3 XVII 7.2.1). We thus obtain a Barsotti-Tate subgroup of \({}_{p^\infty}A_K\), which might be called the toroidal part of the latter.

6.2. Let us take up again the largest \(p\)-divisible sub-group-scheme \(\overline A_o\) of \(A_o^o\), and consider the Barsotti-Tate \(p\)-group \(F_o={}_{p^\infty}(\overline A_o)\); it is immediate that it can be characterized as the largest Barsotti-Tate sub-\(p\)-group of \(A_o\) (or of \(A_o^o\)). It is natural to pose the question of extending this Barsotti-Tate group to a Barsotti-Tate subgroup \(F\) of \(A\), which would then play the role played by the fixed part \(T_\ell(A_K)^f\) of (2.2.3.2) in the case where \(A^o\) is \(\ell\)-divisible. In the semi-stable case, where we have \(\overline A_o=A_o^o\), the question is resolved in the affirmative by the method of 2.2. In the general case, when the problem under consideration has a solution, we see easily that it is unique, and that the corresponding sub-pro-groups of \(T_p(A_K)\) and of \(T_p(A_K^v)\) (which then still deserve the name of fixed part of the pro-groups under consideration) still satisfy the orthogonality theorem 5.2, and finally that the fixed part \(T_p(A_K)^f\) can still be characterized as indicated in 5.8.

6.2.1. Let us show however that in general the problem under consideration does not have an affirmative solution, i.e. that it is not possible in general to define the fixed part \(T_p(A)^f\) (the case of non-semi-stable reduction). Indeed, by virtue of a lifting theorem of SERRE-TATE [30], when \(S\) is complete, the existence of the fixed part is equivalent to that of infinitesimal liftings \(\overline A_n\) of \(\overline A_o\) in the \(A_n\), or again to that of a smooth group scheme \(\widehat{\overline A}\) over \(S\), an extension of an abelian scheme by a torus \(T\), with special fiber \(\overline A_o\), and of a formal homomorphism \(\widehat{\overline A}\longrightarrow\widehat A\) extending the inclusion on the special fibers. Hence, in the case where \(\overline A_o\) is an abelian scheme, i.e. where \(T_o=0\), this is equivalent to the existence of an abelian scheme \(\overline A\) extending \(\overline A_o\) and of a homomorphism \(\overline A\longrightarrow A\) extending the inclusion of the special fibers. It will therefore suffice to give a case where \(T_o=0\), where \(A_o^o\) is non-unipotent and non-proper, and where \(A_K\) is simple over \(K\).

6.2.2. Here is an example, due to M. RAYNAUD, based on the observation that if \(S\) is a trait, \(S'\) the normalization of \(S\) in a finite separable extension \(K'\) of \(K\), and \(A'\) an abelian scheme over \(S'\), then the group scheme \[A=\prod_{S'/S} A'/S'\quad\text{over }S\] is Néronian, i.e. is identified with the Néron model of its generic fiber (which is indeed an abelian scheme). The verification of this fact is immediate in terms of the definitions and of the fact that \(A'\) itself is Néronian over \(S'\). Then \(A_o=\prod_{S'_s/S_s}(A'\times_{S'}S'_s)/S'_s\) is an extension of an abelian scheme, nonzero if \(A'\ne0\), by a connected unipotent scheme, nonzero if \(A'\ne0\) and if \(S'\) is ramified over \(S\). It will therefore suffice to give an example where \(A'\ne0\), \(S'\) is ramified over \(S\) (a complete local trait), and \(A_K=\prod_{K'/K}A'_{K'}/K'\) is simple over \(K'\). Now let us take for \(K'\) a ramified quadratic extension of \(S\) (one always exists), and for \(A'\) an elliptic curve over \(S'\). Then \(A\) is of relative dimension 2, and to say that \(A_K\) is simple over \(K\) means that one cannot find a nonzero homomorphism \(B_K\longrightarrow A_K\), where \(B_K\) is an abelian scheme of dimension 1 over \(K\). Now the datum of such a homomorphism amounts to the datum of a nonzero homomorphism \(B_{K'}\longrightarrow A'_{K'}\), which will therefore necessarily be an isogeny, i.e. the condition that \(A_K\) be simple over \(K\) means that \(A'_{K'}\) is not isogenous to an elliptic curve coming from \(K\). But in the con- trary case, the invariant \(j\) of \(A'\) would be algebraically dependent (over the prime field) on the conjugate \(\bar j\) of \(j\) under the nontrivial \(K\)-automorphism of \(K'\), and if \(j\) is chosen such that \(a=j+\bar j=\operatorname{Tr}_{K'/K}j\) is transcendental over the prime field, it amounts to the same to say that \(j\) is algebraically dependent on \(a\). Now, for reasons of cardinality, there exists an element \(a\in V\) which is transcendental over the prime subfield \(P\) of \(K\), then an element \(b\) of \(V'\) (the normalization of \(V\) in \(K'\)) which is transcendental over \(P(a)\) and such that \(\operatorname{Tr}_{V'/V}b=0\), and it suffices to take \(j=a+b\in V'\). We may moreover suppose that \(b\in\mathfrak r(V')\), and that the image of \(a\) in \(k=V/\mathfrak m\) is a given element of \(k\), which we shall choose distinct from the excep- tional values \(j_o=0\) and \(j_o=12^3\) (NB this is possible even if \(k\) has only two elements, for then the two values of \(j_o\) under consideration coincide). Hence the image \(j_o\) of \(j\) in \(k(s')\) is \(\ne0,12^3\), and we then know that there indeed exists an elliptic curve over \(S'\) with invariant \(j\).

6.3. Let us return to the case where we only suppose \(S\) henselian, nothing more. In analogy with the definitions of 4.1 in the case \(\ell\ne p\), we can then define a filtration of \(T_p(A_K)\), \[(6.3.1)\qquad T_p(A_K)\supset T_p(A_K)^{ef}\supset T_p(A_K)^{et}\supset0\] by (strict) Barsotti-Tate sub-pro-groups, called respec- tively the essentially fixed part and the essentially toric part of \(T_p(A_K)\). For this, we choose a finite Galois extension \(K'\) of \(K\) such that \(A_{K'}\) has semi-stable reduction over the normalization \(S'\) of \(S\) in \(K'\), so that the fixed part \(T_p(A_{K'})^f\) of \(T_p(A_{K'})=(T_p(A_K))_{K'}\) is defined (2.2.3), as well as the toric part (6.1), which is moreover none other than the orthogonal complement of the fixed part \(T_p(A_{K'}^v)^f\) of \(T_p(A_{K'}^v)\) (5.2). It is clear that we have \(T_p(A_{K'})^t\subset T_p(A_{K'})^f\) (this follows from 5.3, or directly from the construction 6.1 of the toric part), and that the Galois group of \(K'/K\) leaves invariant these two strict sub-pro-groups of \((T_p(A_K))_{K'}\). Hence by descent we deduce from this the desired filtration (6.3.1). It does not depend on the choice of \(K'\), as follows from the property 3.3 of semi-stable reduction. Moreover, we shall evidently have by descent the orthogonality property \[(6.3.2)\qquad T_p(A_K)^{et}=(T_p(A_K^v)^{ef})^\perp,\quad T_p(A_K)^{ef}=(T_p(A_K^v)^{et})^\perp.\]

It would be possible to give an intrinsic characterization of the filtration (6.3.1), in terms of the Barsotti-Tate pro-group \(X=T_p(A_K)\) over the field of fractions \(K\) of \(S\), in the spirit of 5.8, — at least, for the moment, in the case of unequal characteristics: the essentially fixed part is the largest Barsotti-Tate sub-pro-group which has "essentially good reduction over \(S\)", i.e. which, after a finite separable extension \(K'\) of \(K\), gives a Barsotti-Tate pro-group \(X_{K'}\) having good reduction over the normalization \(S'\) of \(S\) in \(K'\), i.e. extends to a Barsotti-Tate pro-group over \(S'\) (necessarily unique up to unique isomorphism, by the theorem of TATE [33, th. 4] already cited). The essentially toric part is then defined in terms of the essentially fixed part by ortho- gonality (6.3.2).
Let us also point out the following immediate consequence of 5.8 and of the definitions, which for \(\ell=p\) reduces moreover to 5.12 a):

Proposition 6.3.3. Let \(\ell\) be a prime number. If \(\ell=p\), we suppose \(\operatorname{car}.K=0\) (*). In order that \(A_K\) have essentially good reduction over \(S\) (5.12 a)), it is necessary and sufficient that the essentially fixed part \(T_\ell(A_K)^{ef}\) (4.1.1 and 6.3.1) be equal to all of \(T_\ell(A_K)\).

6.4. Note that when \(\ell\ne p\), we brought out in 4.3 a) a remarkable integer-valued function on the inertia group \(I\) of \(S\), independent moreover of the choice of \(\ell\), and whose knowledge, in the case of a perfect residue field, suffices to reconstruct the conductor exponent of \(A\) (4.5), by taking the scalar product, over a sufficiently large quotient \(\Gamma=I/I'\) of \(I\), of the preceding function and the Artin conductor on \(\Gamma\) (cf. (4.6.2)). It may be interesting to observe that this integer-valued function on \(I\) can also be recovered from the knowledge of \(T_\ell(A_K)\) for \(\ell=p\) — at least (for the moment) in unequal characteristics. When the residue field \(k\) is finite, an analogous observation applies to the function with values in \(\mathbb Q\), defined on the subgroup \(\Pi'\) of \(\Pi\) formed by the elements whose image in \(\Pi_o\) is of the form \(f_q^n\) (\(n\in\mathbb Z\)), considered in 4.3 b). Let us indicate the principle of the verification of these assertions. We use the filtration (6.3.1), and we are reduced to attaching a suitable function (on \(I\) resp. on \(\Pi'\)) to a Barsotti-Tate \(p\)-pro-group \(Y\) over \(K\) which has "essentially good reduction over \(S\)", in the sense made precise in 6.5. (\(Y\) will be one of the three quotients coming from the filtration (6.3.1).)

In the case of 4.3 a), we reduce to the case of a perfect residue field (or even algebraically closed, if one wishes) by a suitable residual extension in \(S\) (EGA \(0_{\mathrm{III}}\) 10.3.1). Let us then take a finite Galois extension \(K'\) of \(K\) such that \(Y_{K'}\) has good reduction over \(S'\), whence a Barsotti-Tate group \(Y_{S'}\) over \(S'\) and an operation of \(G\) on \(Y_{S'}\) compatible with its action on \(S'\). If \(k'\) is the residue field of \(S'\), \(G\) acts (in a not necessarily faithful way) on \(k'\) (the field of invariants being \(k\)), and on \(Y_{k'}\), in a way compatible with its action on \(k'\). Now, according to the theory of DIEUDONNÉ [16 bis], to the Barsotti-Tate \(p\)-pro-group \(Y_{k'}\) is canonically associated a free module \(M\) of finite type (of rank equal to the rank of \(Y_{k'}\)) over the ring \(W(k')\) of Witt vectors over \(k'\), and \(G\) evidently acts on \(M\) in a way compatible with its operations on \(W(k')\) (via its operations on \(k'\)). We deduce from this a \(W(k')\)-linear operation of the inertia subgroup \(\Gamma\) of \(G\) on \(M\) (which is a quotient of the inertia group \(I\)), whence a trace function on \(\Gamma\) and hence on \(I\), which is the desired function. It is a priori with values in \(W(k')\) (*), but coincides with the analogous function defined in terms of the \(T_\ell\) for \(\ell\ne p\), when the operation of \(G\) on \(Y_{S'}\) is obtained from an operation of \(G\) on a smooth and \(p\)-divisible group scheme \(C'\) over \(S'\), by taking \(Y=T_p(C')\) (cf. [25 bis, th.7 p.737]); it is therefore in this case integer-valued. We proceed in an analogous way in the case of 4.3 b) to define a trace function on \(\Pi'\), using the procedure of the proof of 4.3.2 to "linearize" the operation in \(M\) defined by an element \(g\in\Pi\) which lies above an \(f_q^n\) with \(n\geq0\).

pages.title: 7. The Raynaud extension \(G\) over \(S\) attached to the abelian scheme \(A_K\) with semi-stable reduction. Duality of the abelian schemes \(B,B'\) over \(S\).

blocks.html: 7. The Raynaud extension \(G\) over \(S\) attached to the abelian scheme \(A_K\) with semi-stable reduction. Duality of the abelian schemes \(B,B'\) over \(S\).

7.1. Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, the spectrum of a ring \(V\), separated and complete for the topology defined by an ideal \(\mathfrak m\) of \(V\). Let \(A\) be a group scheme, smooth over \(S\), \(A^o\) its identity component, and suppose that \(A_o/A_o^o\) is finite (hence finite étale) over \(S_o\), and that \(A_o^o\) is an extension of an abelian scheme by a torus. We have set as usual \(A_n=A\times_S S_n\), where \(S_n=\operatorname{Spec}(V/\mathfrak m^{n+1})\). We shall be interested above all in the case where \(S\) is a trait, and where the generic fiber \(A_K\) is an abelian scheme with semi-stable reduction over \(S\), \(A\) being the Néron model of \(A_K\).

Let \(T_o\) be the maximal torus of \(A_o^o\), so that \(B_o=A_o^o/T_o\) is an abelian scheme. The construction of 5.1 then applies to furnish a formal subtorus \(\widehat T\) of the formal completion \(\widehat A\). We know that for every \(n\), the quotient \[(7.1.1)\qquad B_n=A_n^o/T_n\] is representable (SGA 3 VIA 3.2); more precisely it is a scheme, flat and of finite type over \(S_n\), whose inverse image over \(S_o\) is the abelian scheme \(B_o\). Consequently \(B_n\) is an abelian scheme over \(S_n\), and for variable \(n\), the \(B_n\) are deduced from one another by base change, hence they define a formal abelian scheme \[(7.1.2)\qquad \widehat B=(B_n)_{n\geq0}.\] We shall give conditions implying that this abelian scheme is algebraizable (EGA III 5.4.1), more precisely that it is the formal completion of a projective abelian scheme over \(S\), which we shall then denote by \(B\). By virtue of loc. cit. \(B\) is determined up to unique isomorphism (as an \(S\)-scheme; the group structure of \(\widehat B\) then comes in a unique way from a group structure on \(B\), by virtue of loc. cit. as well). This abelian scheme \(B\) will then be called the abelian part of the scheme \(A\), and sometimes denoted \(A^{\mathrm{ab}}\).

7.1.3. The projectivity of \(\widehat B\) means, by virtue of EGA III 5.4.5, that one can find an invertible Module \(\widehat M\) on \(\widehat B\), i.e. a sequence of invertible Modules \(M_n\) on the \(B_n\) which "glue" for variable \(n\), which is ample relative to \(S\), i.e. which is such that \(M_o\) is ample. The natural idea would be to start with an ample invertible Module \(M\) on \(G\), and to show that for every \(n\), the restriction \(L_n\) of \(L\) to \(G_n\) comes by inverse image from an invertible Module \(M_n\) on \(B_n\). I do not know whether such \(M_n\) necessarily exist (and strongly doubt it). We shall follow a slightly different method, by constructing a polarization of \(\widehat B\), i.e. a projective system of polarizations \(\varphi_n\) of the \(B_n\) which glue together. We know then [5] that the doubles of these polarizations canonically define (symmetric) invertible Modules \(M_n\) on the \(B_n\), which by their canonical nature will necessarily glue together, and will furnish the desired \(\widehat M\).

We shall interpret an element of \(\operatorname{NS}(B_n/S_n)\) as a symmetric divisorial correspondence on \((B_n,B_n)\) [5], i.e. as a biextension of \((B_n,B_n)\) by \(\mathbb G_{m,S_n}\). By virtue of VIII 3.5, these biextensions correspond bijectively to the biextensions of \((A_n,A_n)\) by \(\mathbb G_{m,S_n}\). To obtain a coherent system of such biextensions \(W_n\) it suffices to start from a \[(7.1.4)\qquad W,\ \text{symmetric biextension of }(A,A)\text{ by }\mathbb G_{m,S},\] taking the \(W_n\) to be deduced from \(W\) by the base changes \(S\longrightarrow S_n\). We must moreover suppose that the element of \(\operatorname{NS}(B_o)\) defined by \(W_o\) is a polarization of \(B_o\) in order to obtain in this way a polarization of \(\widehat B\).

Proposition 7.1.5. With the preceding notations for \(S,A,\widehat B\), suppose that \(S\) is normal. Then \(\widehat B\) is algebraizable, and the abelian scheme \(B\) over \(S\) from which it comes is projective over \(S\).

We may evidently suppose \(A=A^o\), and \(S\) integral. According to M. RAYNAUD [23 XI 1.13], the hypothesis that \(S\) is normal implies that \(A\) is quasi-projective over \(S\). Let then \(L\) be an invertible Module on \(A\) which is relatively ample, and let us form the bitrigidified Module \[\delta(L)=m^*(L)\operatorname{pr}_1^*(L)^{-1}\operatorname{pr}_2^*(L)^{-1}\quad\text{on }A\times_S A\text{ as in VIII 4.12.}\] Since the fibers of \(A_o\) have unipotent rank zero, the same holds for those of \(A\) (this follows easily from 3.1), hence by virtue of VIII 7.5, \(\delta(L)\) defines a biextension \(W\) of \((A,A)\), evidently symmetric, which by virtue of VIII 4.13 (i.e. essentially by virtue of RAYNAUD [23 XI 1.11]) satisfies the desired ampleness condition at the points of \(S_o\).
Remark 7.1.6. One can prove, without any normality hypothesis on \(S\), that \(\widehat B\) is always algebraizable, by reducing to the normal case as in 13.5.3 a).
7.2. Let us now note the

Lemma 7.2.1. Let \(S\) be the spectrum of a noetherian ring \(V\), separated and complete for the topology defined by an ideal \(\mathfrak m\) of \(V\), \(C\) the category of group schemes \(A\) over \(S\), smooth of finite type over \(S\), such that \(A/A^o\) is a finite étale group scheme over \(S\) and such that \(A^o\) is an extension of an abelian scheme \(B\) by an isotrivial torus \(T\) (SGA 3 IX 1.1), \(C'\) the category of groups in the category of formal schemes of finite type (EGA I 10.3.3) over \(\operatorname{Spf}(A)\), and \(A\longmapsto\widehat A\) the functor from \(C\) into \(C'\) "formal completion along \(A_o=A\times_S S_o\)", where \(S_o=\operatorname{Spec}(V/\mathfrak m)\). Then:

a) The preceding functor is fully faithful.

b) Let \(\mathcal A\) be an object of \(C'\), smooth over \(\widehat S=\operatorname{Spf}(A)\) (i.e. such that the ordinary schemes \(A_n=\mathcal A\times^{\widehat S}_{S_n}\) over the \(S_n=\operatorname{Spec}(A/\mathfrak m^{n+1})\) are smooth). Suppose that \(A_o/A_o^o\) is a finite étale group scheme over \(S_o\), and that \(A_o^o\) is an extension of an abelian scheme \(B_o\) by an isotrivial torus \(T_o\). Let \(\mathcal A^o=(A_n^o)_{n\geq0}\), \(\mathcal A/\mathcal A^o=(A_n/A_n^o)_{n\geq0}\). Then \(\mathcal A/\mathcal A^o\) is finite étale over \(\widehat S\), and \(\mathcal A^o\) is, in an essentially unique way, and functorially in \(\mathcal A\), an extension of a formal abelian scheme \(\mathcal B\) over \(S\) by a formal torus \(\mathcal T\) over \(S\), reducing respectively to \(B_o\) and \(T_o\). In order that \(\mathcal A\) belong to the essential image of the preceding functor \(C\longrightarrow C'\) (or, as we shall also say, in order that it be "algebraizable"), it is necessary and sufficient that the same be true of \(\mathcal B\).

7.2.1.1. Let us note first, over an arbitrary scheme \(S\), that if \(A\) is a smooth group scheme such that \(A^o\) is an extension of an abelian scheme by a torus, this extension structure is essentially unique, and is functorial in \(A\), in an evident sense. Indeed, our assertion reduces at once to the following one: every morphism from a torus \(T\) into an abelian scheme \(B\) is zero. Now it is well known that this is so on the geometric fibers [15], and we conclude thanks to SGA 3 IX 5.3. Applying this result to the inductive systems of smooth group schemes \(A_n\) over the \(S_n\) occurring in 7.2.1, we conclude from it an analogous result for formal group schemes over \(S\). Moreover, we have already pointed out in 7.1 that if \(A_o^o\) is an extension of an abelian scheme by a torus, the same holds for \(A_n^o\). This therefore proves the first assertion of b).

7.2.1.2. Suppose on the other hand that the formal group scheme \(\mathcal A\) over \(S\) is of the form \(\widehat A\), with \(A\in\operatorname{ob}C\). If \(A^o\) is an extension of an abelian scheme \(B\) by a torus \(T\), we see immediately that we have \(\mathcal B\simeq\widehat B\), which proves that \(\mathcal A\) algebraizable implies \(\mathcal B\) algebraizable. Conversely, suppose \(\mathcal B\) algebraizable, i.e. that it comes from an abelian scheme \(B\), and let us prove that \(\mathcal A\) is. Let us first prove that \(\mathcal A^o=(A_n^o)\) is algebraizable. For this, note that the extensions \(A_n^o\) of \(B_n\) by \(T_n\) can be interpreted, by virtue of VIII 3.7, as homomorphisms
\[(*)\qquad \underline M_n\longrightarrow B'_n,\]

where \(\underline M_n\) is the twisted constant group over \(S_n\) dual to \(T_n\) (SGA 3 X 5.7), and \(B'_n\) the abelian scheme dual to \(B_n\). Since the functor of restriction to \(S_o\), or to an arbitrary \(S_n\), induces an equivalence from the category of isotrivial tori over \(S\) into that of isotrivial tori over \(S_o\) (SGA 3 X 3.2), the \(T_n\) come from a torus \(T\) over \(S\), defined up to unique isomorphism, whose dual group we shall denote by \(\underline M\); its restriction to \(S_n\) is therefore canonically isomorphic to \(\underline M_n\). The isotriviality property of \(T\) is interpreted by the fact that \(\underline M\) is a scheme which is a sum of schemes \(\underline M^i\) finite over \(S\) (SGA 3 X 5.11). It follows at once that the homomorphisms (*) come from a unique homomorphism
\[(**)\qquad \underline M\longrightarrow B',\]

where \(B'\) is the abelian scheme dual to \(B\). This homomorphism in turn can be interpreted as an extension of \(B\) by \(T\), say \(A^o\), which manifestly furnishes an algebraization of \(\mathcal A^o\).

7.2.1.3. On the other hand, since by hypothesis \(A_o/A_o^o\) is a finite group scheme \(\Phi_o\) over \(S_o\), the \(A_n/A_n^o\) are likewise group schemes \(\Phi_n\) finite over \(S_n\) (their representability resulting from ), which are moreover necessarily étale over \(S\). There exists then an étale group scheme \(\Phi\) over \(S\), defined up to unique isomorphism, giving rise to the formal group scheme \(\mathcal A/\mathcal A^o\) defined by the \(\Phi_n\). Now, \(G=A^o\) being divisible, we see at once that the category of extensions of fppf sheaves of a finite locally constant group over \(S\), such that \(\Phi\), by the group scheme \(A^o\), is canonically equivalent to the category of torsors under the sheaf
\[H=\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_{\mathrm{gr}}(\Phi,G),\]

the equivalence in question being compatible with any base change. Applying the same description of the extensions of \(\Phi_n\) by \(G_n\) in terms of torsors under \(H_n\simeq\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_{\mathrm{gr}}(\Phi_n,G_n)\), the \(A_n\) define for us a coherent system of torsors \(P_n\) under the \(H_n\). Now, \(G\) being an extension of an abelian scheme by a torus, we conclude easily that \(H\) is a group scheme finite and locally free over \(S\), from which we conclude easily that the functor \(P\longmapsto\widehat P=(P_n)\) defines an equivalence between the category of torsors over \(S\) under \(H\), and the category of "formal" torsors \(\widehat P\) under \(\widehat H\), i.e. of coherent systems of torsors \(P_n\) under the \(H_n\). Hence the \(P_n\) defined by the extensions \(A_n\) come from a single torsor \(P\) over \(S\) under \(H\). From this we conclude an extension \(A\) of \(\Phi\) by \(G=A^o\) which "algebraizes" \(\mathcal A\), except that it remains to verify that it is representable. Now we may regard \(A\) as a torsor over \(\Phi\), with group \(A^o_\Phi\), and it is immediate that this torsor becomes trivial after extension of the base by the finite surjective locally free morphism \(P\times_S\Phi\longrightarrow\Phi\) (since the base change \(P\longrightarrow S\) splits the extension under consideration \(A\) of \(\Phi\) by \(A^o\)). We may then conclude by one of the numerous criteria for effectivity of descent of M. RAYNAUD [23 XI 3.1 6)].

7.2.1.4. This completes the proof of b), and it only remains to prove the assertion a) of full faithfulness. We leave to the reader the task of verifying that the latter is contained essentially in the method of proof of b), or, if he prefers, of seeking a direct independent proof of it.

7.2.1.5. We note that the reference to the delicate descent result of RAYNAUD becomes unnecessary if we suppose \(A^o\) quasi-projective over \(S\), or what amounts to the same thing, \(B\) projective over \(S\). This condition is satisfied in particular when we start from the formal group scheme \(\widehat A\) over \(S\), where \(A\) is as in 7.1.5, — which is the only case in which we shall use 7.2.1.

7.2.2. We shall apply 7.2.1 to the formal group scheme \(\widehat A\) considered in 7.1, in the favorable case considered in 7.1 where the abelian scheme \(\widehat B\) is algebraizable (cf. 7.1.5), and where we suppose moreover that the torus \(T_o\) over \(S_o\) is isotrivial. We thus find a group scheme \(A^\natural\) over \(S\), called the Raynaud Group of \(A\) over \(S\), characterized up to isomorphism by a canonical isomorphism of formal group schemes over \(\operatorname{Spf}(V)=\widehat S\):
\[(7.2.3)\qquad (A^\natural)^\wedge\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow}\widehat A.\]
It follows from 7.2.1 that
\[(7.2.4)\qquad \Phi=A^\natural/A^{\natural o}\]
is a finite étale group over \(S\), and that the identity component \[A^{\natural o}=A^{o\natural}\] is endowed with an extension structure
\[(7.2.5)\qquad 0\longrightarrow T\longrightarrow A^{\natural o}\longrightarrow B\longrightarrow0,\]

where \(B\) is the abelian scheme over \(S\) whose formal completion is the formal abelian scheme \(\widehat B\) constructed in 7.1, and \(T\) is characterized (up to unique isomorphism) as the isotrivial torus over \(S\) which lifts \(T_o\). This extension structure (7.2.5) gives, by restriction to the \(S_n\), the extension structures on the \(A_n^{\natural o}=A_n^o\) defined by (7.1.1).

7.2.6. It follows on the other hand immediately from 7.2.1 that the Raynaud Group \(A^\natural\), as well as the exact sequence (7.2.5), depend functorially on \(A\) (satisfying the conditions under consideration), and that their formation commutes with every base change \(S'\longrightarrow S\) associated with a homomorphism of adic topological rings \(V\longrightarrow V'\).

7.3. Let us now consider, for a fixed prime number \(\ell\), the pro-\(\ell\)-group \(T_\ell(A^o)\). Identifying a finite scheme over \(S\) with the corresponding formal scheme, and defining the "fixed part" \(T_\ell(A^o)^f\) as
usual, we find an isomorphism as in (5.1.5)
\[(7.3.1)\qquad T_\ell(A^o)^f\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow}T_\ell(\widehat A^o),\]
and defining the "toric part" as in (5.1.6)
\[(7.3.2)\qquad T_\ell(A^o)^t=T_\ell(\widehat T)\simeq T_\ell(T)\subset T_\ell(A^o)^f\simeq T_\ell(\widehat A^o),\]
we find at once, for the "abelian part", an isomorphism
\[(7.3.3)\qquad T_\ell(A^o)^{\mathrm{ab}}\mathrel{\mathop=\limits^{\mathrm{dfn}}} T_\ell(A^o)^f/T_\ell(A^o)^t\simeq T_\ell(\widehat B).\]

We have not yet used here the hypothesis that \(\widehat B\) is algebraizable, a hypothesis which allowed us to construct the abelian scheme \(B\) and the Raynaud extension of \(B\) by \(T\). Since we evidently have a canonical isomorphism (a particular case of (7.3.1) applied to the abelian scheme \(B\)!) \(T_\ell(\widehat B)\simeq T_\ell(B)\), the isomorphism (7.3.3) also gives us:
\[(7.3.4)\qquad T_\ell(A^o)^{\mathrm{ab}}\simeq T_\ell(B).\]
Let us also note, in passing, that (7.2.3) and (7.3.1) also imply a canonical isomorphism
\[(7.3.5)\qquad T_\ell(A^o)^f\simeq T_\ell(A^{\natural o}),\]
taking into account that the second member is already a pro-finite group, i.e. identical with its fixed part. The canonical exact sequence
\[(7.3.6)\qquad 0\longrightarrow T_\ell(A^o)^t\longrightarrow T_\ell(A^o)^f \longrightarrow T_\ell(B)\longrightarrow0,\]
which can in any case (without any algebraizability hypothesis on \(\widehat B\)) be interpreted as the exact sequence deduced by application of the functor \(T_\ell\) from the exact sequence
\[0\longrightarrow\widehat T\longrightarrow\widehat A^{o\natural} \longrightarrow\widehat B\longrightarrow0,\]
