can also be considered as deduced by application of the functor \(T_\ell\) from the canonical exact sequence of ordinary group schemes
\[0\longrightarrow T\longrightarrow A^{\natural o}\longrightarrow B\longrightarrow0.\]

7.4. Suppose now given a second group scheme \(A'\) over \(S\), satisfying the same hypotheses as \(G\), and suppose that \(A,A'\) has connected fibers. Let \(W\) be a biextension of \((A,A')\) by \(\mathbb G_{mS}\). From it we thus deduce a pairing (VIII 4.2)
\[(7.4.1)\qquad T_\ell(A)\times T_\ell(A')\longrightarrow T_\ell(\mathbb G_{mS})=\mathbb Z_\ell(1)\]
and by restriction to the fixed parts a pairing
\[(7.4.2)\qquad T_\ell(A)^f\times T_\ell(A')^f =T_\ell(\widehat G)\times T_\ell(\widehat G')\longrightarrow\mathbb Z_\ell(1),\]

which can also be interpreted as the pairing defined by the biextension \(\widehat W\) of \((\widehat A,\widehat A')\) by \(\widehat{\mathbb G}_{mS}\), deduced from \(W\) by completion (defining here, for simplicity, a biextension of formal group schemes as a coherent family of biextensions \(W_n\) of the \((A_n,A'_n)\) by \(\mathbb G_{mS_n}\)). Now we know (VIII 3.5) that \(W_n\) comes from a biextension \(W_n^{\mathrm{ab}}\) of \((B_n,B'_n)\) (where \(B'\) is the abelian part of \(G'\)), so that (7.4.2) factors as
\[(7.4.3)\qquad T_\ell(A)^{\mathrm{ab}}\times T_\ell(A')^{\mathrm{ab}} =T_\ell(\widehat B)\times T_\ell(\widehat B')\longrightarrow\mathbb Z_\ell(1),\]
which is also the pairing defined by the biextension
\[\widehat W_n^{\mathrm{ab}}=(W_n^{\mathrm{ab}})^\wedge\]

of \((\widehat B,\widehat B')\) by \(\widehat{\mathbb G}_{mS}\). When \(\widehat B,\widehat B'\) are algebraizable, so that we have abelian schemes \(B,B'\) over \(S\), then by virtue of EGA IV 5.1.4 \(\widehat W^{\mathrm{ab}}\) comes from a biextension, defined up to unique isomorphism,
\[W^{\mathrm{ab}}\quad\text{of }(B,B')\text{ by }\mathbb G_{mS},\]
and the corresponding pairing
\[(7.4.4)\qquad T_\ell(B)\times T_\ell(B')\longrightarrow T_\ell(\mathbb G_{mS})\]
is canonically isomorphic to (7.4.3).
Denoting by \(D B'\) the abelian scheme dual to \(B'\), \(W^{\mathrm{ab}}\) defines a homomorphism
\[(7.4.5)\qquad u:B\longrightarrow D(B'),\]

whose properties reflect those of the pairings (7.4.4), i.e. (7.4.3). We see for example that the pairing (7.4.3) is a perfect duality at a point \(t\in S\) if and only if \(u_t:B_t\longrightarrow D(B')_t\) is an isogeny whose kernel is of rank prime to \(\ell\). Since this implies that \(u\) itself is an isogeny (\(S\) being local, hence connected), and since \(\ker u\) is flat and finite over \(S\), so that \(\ker u\) has the same rank at every point, we see that the condition just considered is independent of the chosen point \(t\).

7.4.6. This completes in particular the proof of 5.4, since under the conditions of that theorem (where we may suppose \(S\) complete), applying the foregoing to \(A^o\) and \(A'{}^o\), we have established that the pairing (7.4.3) is for every \(\ell\) a perfect duality at the generic point \(\eta\), so that the same holds at the point \(s\).

7.4.7. We see likewise by the preceding considerations that the orthogonality theorem 5.2 becomes clear, without recourse to Lemma 5.2.4. Indeed, we established along the way in 7.4 that the pairing (5.2.3.1)=(7.4.2) vanishes on the toric parts on both sides, hence factors into a pairing (7.4.3), for which it remains to establish that it is separated on the generic fibers of the pro-groups under consideration, knowing that it is so on the special fibers. This comes indeed from the fact that if (7.4.5) induces an isogeny on the special fibers, it is an isogeny.

7.4.8. Let us return to the general conditions of 7.4, and suppose given an open set \(U\subset S-S_o\), such that \(A_U\) and \(A'_U\) are abelian schemes, dual to each other via the biextension \(W_U\). Let us consider the restrictions to \(U\) of the exact sequence (7.3.6) and of the analogous exact sequence relative to \(A'\), so that we find on the Barsotti–Tate pro-\(\ell\)-groups \(T_\ell(A_U)\) and \(T_\ell(A'_U)\) canonical filtrations
\[(7.4.9)\qquad \left\{\begin{aligned} T_\ell(A_U)&\supset T_\ell(A_U)^f\supset T_\ell(A_U)^t\supset0,\\ T_\ell(A'_U)&\supset T_\ell(A'_U)^f\supset T_\ell(A'_U)^t\supset0. \end{aligned}\right.\]

This granted, I claim that these two filtrations are dual to each other, relative to the pairing (7.4.1) (which here is a perfect duality (VIII 3.2)), i.e. that we again have the relation (5.2.2) and the symmetric relation (with \(A_K,A'_K\) replaced by \(A_U\) and \(A'_U\)). We have moreover seen above that this orthogonality statement amounts also to saying that the biextension \(W^{\mathrm{ab}}\) of \((B,B')\) by \(\mathbb G_{mS}\) is a perfect duality over \(U\) (or, what amounts to the same thing if \(U\) is dense in \(S\), over \(S\)). This is a property which it suffices to verify at every point \(\eta\) of \(U\), and then the usual argument reduces us to the case where \(S\) is a trait with generic point \(\eta\), which is already known.

The orthogonality relation allows us on the other hand to extend to the present case the formulas (5.5.7), (5.5.8) and (5.5.9), where we replace the operation of restriction to the generic point \(\eta\) by that of restriction to the open set \(U\); consequently, 5.6 also extends to the present case. We refrain from rewriting here the preceding formulas and statement in the present context.
Let us summarize, from the point of view of Néron models, the results obtained concerning the abelian parts and their pairings:

Proposition 7.5. Let \(S\) be a complete trait, with field of functions \(K\). To every abelian scheme \(A_K\) over \(K\) having semi-stable reduction over \(S\), the procedure of 7.1, applied to the identity component of the Néron model \(A\), of \(A_K\) makes it possible to define the abelian part \(B=A^{\mathrm{ab}}\) of the latter, which is an abelian scheme over \(S\), depending functorially on \(A_K\). For every other abelian scheme \(A'_K\) with semi-stable reduction, one can associate to every divisorial correspondence \(W_K\) on \((A_K,A'_K)\) a unique divisorial correspondence \(W^{\mathrm{ab}}\) on \((A^{\mathrm{ab}},A'{}^{\mathrm{ab}})\), such that for every integer \(n\geq0\), \(W_n^{\mathrm{ab}}\) has as inverse image under \(A_n^o\longrightarrow B_n=A_n^{o\mathrm{ab}}\), \(A'{}^o_n\longrightarrow B'_n=A'{}^{o\mathrm{ab}}_n\) the biextension \(W_n\) of \((A_n^o,A'{}^o_n)\) deduced from \(W\) by reduction, where \(W\) is the canonical prolongation (1.4) of \(W_K\) to \((A^o,A'{}^o)\).

The formation of \(W^{\mathrm{ab}}\) is compatible with the formation of inverse images of correspondences under morphisms of abelian schemes \(\bar A_K\longrightarrow A_K\), \(\bar A'_K\longrightarrow A'_K\). If \(W_K\) is a perfect duality, the same holds for \(W^{\mathrm{ab}}\). If \(W_K\) is the divisorial correspondence of \((A_K,A'_K)\) associated with a polarization of \(A_K\), then \(W^{\mathrm{ab}}\) is associated with a polarization of \(A^{\mathrm{ab}}\).

The assertion of compatibility of the formation of the \(W^{\mathrm{ab}}\) with inverse images is trivial, thanks to the indicated characterization of the \(W^{\mathrm{ab}}\). The complement concerning polarizations was seen in 7.1.5 at the same time as the proof of the algebraizability of \(\widehat B\).
7.6. Let us continue to suppose that \(S\) is a complete trait, \(A\) being the Néron model of an abelian scheme \(A_K\) over \(K\). We then set
\[(7.6.1)\qquad A_K^\natural=(A^\natural)_K,\]

where \(A^ atural\) is the Raynaud scheme (7.2.2), a successive extension of the finite étale group \(\Phi\) by the abelian scheme \(B\) by the torus \(T\). Hence \(A_K^ atural\) is itself an extension of \(\Phi_K\) by \(A_K^{ atural o}=A^{ atural o}_K\simeq A_K^{ atural o}\), which is an extension of the abelian scheme \(B_K\) by the torus \(T_K\). For every prime number \(\ell\), we have by virtue of (7.3.5) and (7.3.2):
\[(7.6.2)\qquad T_\ell(A_K^\natural)\simeq T_\ell(A_K^{\natural o}) \simeq T_\ell(A_K)^f,\qquad T_\ell(T_K)\simeq T_\ell(A_K)^t.\]
7.6.3. We note that by virtue of 3.3, the formation of \(A_K^{\natural o}\) commutes with any base change for a morphism of complete traits \(S'\longrightarrow S\).

pages.title: 8. The Raynaud extension \(A_K^{\natural o}\) in the non-semi-stable case, and the ind-group \(A_K^b\)

blocks.html:
8. The Raynaud extension \(A_K^{\natural o}\) in the non-semi-stable case, and the
ind-group \(A_K^b\)

8.1. Let us continue to suppose the trait \(S\) complete, but let us no longer necessarily suppose that \(A_K\) has semi-stable reduction. Let as usual \(K'\) be a finite Galois extension of \(K\) such that \(A_{K'}\) has semi-stable reduction with respect to the normalization \(S'\) of \(S\) in \(K'\). Consider the Raynaud extension \(A_{K'}^\natural\) associated with \(A_{K'}\) (7.6.1). It is clear that the Galois group of \(K'/K\) acts by transport of structure on this group scheme, whence by Galois descent a group scheme over \(K\), say \(A_{K,K'}^\natural\), which depends on the choice of the extension \(K'\). However its identity component does not depend on it, up to canonical isomorphism, thanks to 7.6.3, and it will be denoted simply \(A_K^{\natural o}\). We see by descent that \(A_K^{\natural o}\) is an extension
\[(8.1.1)\qquad 0\longrightarrow T_K\longrightarrow A_K^{\natural o} \longrightarrow B_K\longrightarrow0,\]

where \(T_K\) is a torus and \(B_K\) an abelian scheme over \(K\). Likewise, by virtue of the definition of the essentially fixed and essentially toric parts of \(T_\ell(A_K)\) (4.1 and 6.5), the isomorphisms (7.6.2) give canonical isomorphisms
\[(8.1.2)\qquad T_\ell(A_K^{\natural o})\simeq T_\ell(A_K)^{ef},\qquad T_\ell(T_K)\simeq T_\ell(A_K)^{et},\]
whence by passage to the quotient
\[(8.1.3)\qquad T_\ell(B_K)\simeq T_\ell(A_K)^{ef}/T_\ell(A_K)^{et}.\]
This therefore determines two of the quotients occurring in the filtration
\[(8.1.4)\qquad T_\ell(A_K)\supset T_\ell(A_K)^{ef} \supset T_\ell(A_K)^{et}\supset0\]
in terms of the extension of group schemes (8.1.1). To determine the third quotient \(T_\ell(A_K)/T_\ell(A_K)^{ef}\), we note that by virtue of (6.5.2) we have a canonical isomorphism
\[(8.1.5)\qquad T_\ell(A_K)/T_\ell(A_K)^{ef}\simeq D(T_\ell(A'_K)^{et}),\]

where \(D=\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(-,T_\ell(\mathbb G_m))\) denotes the Cartier dual (in the sense of Barsotti-Tate pro-groups), and where \(A'_K\) denotes the abelian scheme dual to \(A_K\). Let us then consider the exact sequence of type (8.1.1)
\[(8.1.6)\qquad 0\longrightarrow T'_K\longrightarrow A'{}^{\natural o}_K \longrightarrow B'_K\longrightarrow0\]
associated with the abelian scheme \(A'_K\) over \(K\), and the corresponding isomorphisms of type (8.1.2). The formula (8.1.5) thus gives us
\[(8.1.7)\qquad T_\ell(A_K)/T_\ell(A_K)^{ef}\simeq D(T_\ell(T'_K)) \simeq M_K\otimes_{\mathbb Z}\mathbb Z_\ell,\]
where we have set (generalizing the notations introduced in (5.5.2)):
\[(8.1.8)\qquad M_K=D(T'_K),\qquad M'_K=D(T_K),\]

and where now \(D\) denotes the Cartier dual \(\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(-,\mathbb G_{mK})\) in the sense of ordinary group schemes. Thus \(M_K\) and \(M'_K\) are twisted constant Groups over \(K\), which over \(K'\) become constant and isomorphic to groups of the form \(\mathbb Z^r\) (\(r=\dim T=\dim T'\)).

8.2. The conditions are those of 8.1. It is clear that the extension (8.1.1) depends functorially on the abelian scheme \(A_K\), and that its formation commutes with every morphism of traits \(\widetilde S\longrightarrow S\). Choosing a polarization of \(A_K\), whence an isogeny \(A_K\longrightarrow A'_K\), we deduce from it a homomorphism from (8.1.1) into (8.1.6), which is seen at once (by looking at the \(T_\ell\)) to be an isogeny term by term. Likewise, using 7.5 and descent, we see that every biextension by \(\mathbb G_{mK}\) of abelian schemes \(A_K,\bar A_K\) over \(K\) defines a biextension of \((B_K,\bar B_K)\) by \(\mathbb G_{mK}\), where \(B_K,\bar B_K\) denote the abelian quotients of the Raynaud extensions corresponding to \(A_K\) and to \(\bar A_K\) respectively; when \(W\) is a perfect duality, the same holds for the corresponding biextension of \((B_K,\bar B_K)\). In particular, with the notations of 7.6, the abelian schemes \(B_K\) and \(B'_K\) are canonically dual to each other. The correspondence between biextensions on \((A_K,\bar A_K)\) and biextensions on \((B_K,\bar B_K)\) has evident functoriality properties for variable \(A_K,\bar A_K\), which are deduced by descent from the analogous properties stated in 7.5, and whose statement we leave to the reader. We likewise leave to the reader the compatibility of the preceding map with any change of traits \(\widetilde S\longrightarrow S\).

8.3. Let us return to the group \(A_{K,K'}^\natural\), and study its variance in \(K'\). It is clear that two isomorphic extensions \(K',K'_1\) of \(K'\) give canonically isomorphic groups, so that we may confine ourselves to the subextensions \(K'\) of the separable closure \(\bar K\) of \(K\). If then \(K'\subset K''\) are two such finite Galois subextensions of \(\bar K\), it follows at once from 3.3 that we have a canonical open immersion
\[(8.3.1)\qquad A_{K,K'}^\natural\lhook\joinrel\longrightarrow A_{K,K''}^\natural,\]

allowing us to identify the first group with an open subgroup of the second. For variable \(K'\), we see that the \(A_{K,K'}^\natural\) thus form an inductive system of smooth groups of finite type over \(K\), with transition morphisms which are open immersions. If we set
\[(8.3.2)\qquad \Phi_{K,K'}=A_{K,K'}^\natural/A_{K,K'}^{\natural o},\]
the \(\Phi_{K,K'}\) themselves thus form an inductive system of finite étale groups over \(K\), which will be studied in more detail below.
We shall set
\[(8.3.3)\qquad A_K^b=\varinjlim_{K'}A_{K,K'}^\natural,\]

the limit being taken in the category of fppf sheaves on \(K\). Since the transition morphisms in the inductive system under consideration are open immersions, it is moreover clear that this limit is in fact representable by a group scheme (in general not quasi-compact) over \(K\), which we shall denote, as is proper, by the same symbol \(A_K^b\). We note that we have, for this group scheme
\[(8.3.4)\qquad A_K^{bo}=A_K^{\natural o},\qquad A_K^b/A_K^{bo}\simeq\varinjlim_{K'}\Phi_{K,K'}.\]
This last group will be determined in 11.10: it is canonically isomorphic to \(M_K\otimes\mathbb Q/\mathbb Z\) (with the notation (8.1.8)).

pages.title: 9. Definition of the monodromy pairing \(M_\ell\otimes M'_\ell\longrightarrow \mathbb Z_{\ell S}\)

blocks.html: 9. Definition of the monodromy pairing \(M_\ell\otimes M'_\ell\longrightarrow \mathbb Z_{\ell S}\)

9.1. In the present section we suppose \(S\) henselian, and that \(A_K\) has semi-stable reduction over \(S\), or what amounts to the same thing, that \(A'_K\) has semi-stable reduction over \(S\) (3.5.1). We then have at our disposal over \(S\) two twisted constant \(\mathbb Z\)-Modules \(M\) and \(M'\) (5.5.2), which over a suitable finite étale covering of \(S\) (hence over \(S\) itself if \(S\) is strictly local, i.e. if \(k\) is separably closed) split into constant groups with value \(M\) resp. \(M'\) isomorphic (non-canonically) to \(\mathbb Z^r\), \(r\) being the common dimension of the maximal tori of \(A_o\) and of \(A'_o\). We shall consider, for every prime number \(\ell\), the corresponding \(\ell\)-adic sheaves
\[(9.1.1)\qquad M_\ell=M\otimes_{\mathbb Z}\mathbb Z_\ell,\qquad M'_\ell=M'\otimes_{\mathbb Z}\mathbb Z_\ell,\]
whose geometric meaning is made explicit by the formulas (5.5.7) and (5.5.8). In the present section, we shall define a canonical pairing (called the "monodromy pairing")
\[(9.1.2)\qquad u_\ell:M_\ell\otimes M'_\ell\longrightarrow\mathbb Z_\ell,\]

which will be defined in terms of the Barsotti-Tate pro-\(\ell\)-groups \(T_\ell(A^o)\) and \(T_\ell(A'{}^o)\), and even of the generic fiber \(T_\ell(A_K)\) of the first of these groups (subject, when \(\ell=p\), to having at our disposal the theorem of Tate [33, th.4], i.e. for the moment to being then in the case of unequal characteristics). When \(\ell\ne p\), it amounts to the same to say that the pairing (9.1.2) is described in terms of the Galois \(\pi\)-module
\[(9.1.3)\qquad U=T_\ell(A_K)(\overline K)=T_\ell(\overline A_K)\]
considered in (2.5.3), and more precisely, we shall see that its knowledge is essentially equivalent to that of the action of the monodromy group \(I\) on \(U\).

9.2. Case \(\ell\ne p\). Let us therefore first place ourselves in the simpler case where \(\ell\ne p\), taking up again the notations introduced in (2.5.3). Note that up to torsion, the relation \(V=U^I\) also means that \(V^\perp\) is the module generated by the elements \(gx-x\) (\(u\in U\), \(g\in I\)), and that in any case we have
\[(9.2.1)\qquad gx-x\in V^\perp\quad\text{for }x\in U,\ g\in I.\]
We thus have, for \(g\in I\):
where \(g\longmapsto u(g)\) is a homomorphism from \(U\) into \(V^\perp\) which vanishes on \(V=U^I\), or what amounts to the same thing
\[(9.2.3)\qquad u\in\widetilde{\operatorname{Hom}}(U/V,V^\perp) \simeq (U/V)^\vee\otimes V^\perp.\]

In the case under consideration of semi-stable reduction, i.e. when we have \(V^\perp\subset V\), the formula (9.2.2) expresses that the operation \(g\) in \(U\) is a transvection parallel to \(V^\perp=W\), and the composition of these transvections is none other than the addition of the corresponding homomorphisms \(u\) (9.2.3). Hence the operation of the inertia group \(I\) on \(U\) is then made entirely explicit by a group homomorphism
\[(9.2.4)\qquad I\longrightarrow (U/V)^\vee\otimes V^\perp.\]
Now by the orthogonality theorem applied both for \(A_K\) and \(A'_K\) we have
\[V^\perp=W,\qquad (U/V)^\vee\simeq W'(-1),\]

where \(W'\) is the toric part of \(U'=T_\ell(A'_K(\overline K))\), and where the \((-1)\) denotes the Tate twist. On the other hand, the homomorphism (9.2.4) factors through \(I(\ell)\simeq\mathbb Z_\ell(1)\), so that the homomorphism (9.2.4) can be interpreted as a canonical homomorphism
\[(9.2.5)\qquad u:\mathbb Z_\ell(1)\longrightarrow W'{}^\vee\otimes W(-1),\]
or again as an element
\[(9.2.6)\qquad u\in W'{}^\vee\otimes W(-2).\]

Recalling the definition of \(W\) and of \(W'\), as the groups of points with values in \(\overline k\) of the two twisted constant \(\ell\)-adic sheaves \(T_\ell(A^o)^t\) and \(T_\ell(A'{}^o)^t\), whose special fibers are respectively \(T_\ell(T_o)\) and \(T_\ell(T'_o)\), we find the canonical isomorphisms

\[(9.2.7)\qquad \left\{\begin{aligned} W&\simeq T_\ell(T_o)(\overline k)\simeq \widetilde M\otimes\mathbb Z_\ell(1),\\ W'&\simeq T_\ell(T'_o)(\overline k)\simeq \widetilde M'\otimes\mathbb Z_\ell(1), \end{aligned}\right.\]
\(M\) and \(M'\) denoting the groups of characters relative to \(\overline k\) of \(T_o\) and of \(T'_o\) respectively:
\[(9.2.8)\qquad T_o=D(M),\qquad T'_o=D(M').\]

Consequently, the element \(u\) of (9.2.6), which expresses the operation of the inertia group \(I\) on the Tate module \(U\) of \(A_K\), can be interpreted as a canonical element of \((\widetilde M'{}^\vee\otimes_{\mathbb Z}\widetilde M)\otimes_{\mathbb Z}\mathbb Z_\ell\), invariant moreover under \(\pi_o=\pi/I=\operatorname{Gal}(\overline k/k)\), since by definition it is invariant by transport of structure:

\[(9.2.9)\qquad u\in(\widetilde M'{}^\vee\otimes_{\mathbb Z}\widetilde M\otimes_{\mathbb Z}\mathbb Z_\ell)^{\pi_o} \simeq\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathbb Z_\ell}(M\otimes M',\mathbb Z_\ell) \simeq\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathbb Z_\ell}(M_\ell\otimes M'_\ell,\mathbb Z_\ell).\]
This is (up to the notation \(u\) instead of \(u_\ell\)) the element (9.1.2) that we wished to define.
To define (9.1.2) in the general case, we shall need some preliminaries, which we shall develop in the two following sections.
9.3. Extensions panachées.
Let \(\mathcal C\) be an abelian category, and
three objects of \(\mathcal C\). We are interested in the classification of objects \(X\) of \(\mathcal C\), equipped with a three-step filtration
\[(*)\qquad X^o=X\supset X^1\supset X^2\supset X^3=0,\]
with given isomorphisms
\[X^o/X^1\xrightarrow{\sim}P,\qquad X^1/X^2\xrightarrow{\sim}R, \qquad X^2/X^3=X^2\xrightarrow{\sim}Q.\]
Setting
we see that \(X\) gives rise to two ordinary extensions
\[(9.3.2)\qquad 0\longrightarrow P\longrightarrow F\longrightarrow R\longrightarrow0, \qquad 0\longrightarrow R\longrightarrow E\longrightarrow Q\longrightarrow0,\]

of \(R\) by \(P\) and of \(Q\) by \(R\) respectively. Our point of view here will be to start, in addition to the objects (9.3.1), from extension structures (9.3.2) assumed also to be given, and to consider the corresponding three-step filtered structures \(X\). Precisely, we shall call an extension panachée of the extension \(E\) by the extension \(F\) an object \(X\) of \(\mathcal C\), equipped with a three-step filtration (*), and with given isomorphisms
\[(9.3.3)\qquad E\simeq X^o/X^2,\qquad F\simeq X^1/X^3=X^1,\]

compatible with the filtrations, i.e. transforming respectively \(X^1/X^2\) into \(\operatorname{Im}(R\longrightarrow E)\) and \(X^2\) into \(\operatorname{Im}(P,F)\), and such finally that the isomorphism \(R\xrightarrow{\sim}F/P\ (\simeq X^1/X^2)\) deduced from (9.3.3) is equal to the one deduced from the first exact sequence (9.3.2). For \(P,Q,R\) and the extensions \(E\) and \(F\) fixed, the extensions panachées of \(E\) by \(F\) form a category in an evident way, which is in fact a groupoid (every arrow in it is invertible). The group of automorphisms of an extension panachée \(X\) of \(E\) by \(F\) is canonically isomorphic to the group \(\operatorname{Hom}(Q,P)\), thanks to the isomorphism
\[(9.3.4)\qquad \operatorname{Hom}(Q,P)\xrightarrow{\sim} \operatorname{Aut}_{\mathrm{extpan}}(X),\qquad u\longmapsto\operatorname{id}_X+\overline u,\]
where \(\overline u\) is the endomorphism of the object of \(\mathcal C\) underlying \(X\) composed of
\[X\longrightarrow Q\xrightarrow{u}P\longrightarrow X,\]
where the extreme arrows are the canonical epimorphism resp. the canonical monomorphism coming from the extension panachée structure given on \(X\).

It would be appropriate to make precise the structures of the category \(\operatorname{EXTPAN}(E,F)\) of extensions panachées of \(E\) by \(F\), by considering the latter as a "torsor" under the "group-category" (VII 2.5) \(\operatorname{EXT}(Q,P)\) of the
extensions of \(Q\) by \(P\), with the aid of a natural functor (analogous to the Brauer composition of ordinary extensions)
\[(9.3.5)\qquad w:\operatorname{EXT}(Q,P)\times\operatorname{EXTPAN}(E,F) \longrightarrow\operatorname{EXTPAN}(E,F),\qquad (G,X)\longmapsto GX,\]

which is such that for every object \(X\) of \(\operatorname{EXTPAN}(E,F)\), the partial functor \(G\longmapsto GX\) is an equivalence from \(\operatorname{EXT}(Q,P)\) into \(\operatorname{EXTPAN}(E,F)\). Let us content ourselves with giving a summary description of the functor \(w\). For this, let us regard an extension panachée \(X\) of \(E\) by \(F\) as defining an ordinary extension (also denoted \(X\)) of \(Q\) by \(F\) (considered as an object of \(\mathcal C\)), and for every extension \(G\) of \(Q\) by \(P\), let us denote by \(\overline G\) the extension of \(Q\) by \(F\) that it defines, thanks to the inclusion \(P\longrightarrow F\). We then set
\[(9.3.6)\qquad GX=w(G,X)=\overline G X,\]

where the sign \(\overline G\) denotes the Brauer composition in the category of extensions of \(Q\) by \(F\). The three-step filtration of the second member \(X^1\) of (9.3.6) is clear, as is the second of the isomorphisms of type (9.3.3) associated with \(X^1\); to define the first isomorphism of type (9.3.3) for \(X^1\), we note that the functor \(Y\longmapsto\widetilde Y\), \(\operatorname{EXT}(Q,F)\longrightarrow\operatorname{EXT}(Q,R)\) deduced from the given morphism \(F\longrightarrow R\) (9.3.2) is compatible with the Brauer composition, hence transforms \(X^1\) into \(\widetilde X^1\) canonically isomorphic to \(\widetilde{\overline G}\widetilde X\). Now the first isomorphism (9.3.3) gives us an isomorphism \(\widetilde X\simeq E\), while it is clear that we have a canonical isomorphism of \(\widetilde{\overline G}\simeq\) (the extension deduced from \(G\) by the composite \(P\longrightarrow F\longrightarrow R\)) with the trivial extension, since the composite in question \(P\longrightarrow R\) is zero. From this results a canonical isomorphism \(\widetilde X^1\simeq E\), which makes precise the extension panachée structure of \(E\) by \(F\) on the second member of (9.3.6). We leave to the reader the task of defining \(w\) on the arrows of \(\operatorname{EXT}(Q,P)\times\operatorname{EXTPAN}(E,F)\), of verifying that the functor (9.3.5) indeed has the announced property, namely that for every fixed extension panachée \(X\), the functor \(G\longmapsto GX\) is an equivalence of \(\operatorname{EXT}(Q,P)\) with \(\operatorname{EXTPAN}(E,F)\), and of proving the associativity formula
where \(G_1G_2\) denotes the Brauer composition in \(\operatorname{EXT}(Q,P)\).

blocks.title: Proposition 9.3.8.
blocks.html:
Proposition 9.3.8. Given two extensions \(E\) and \(F\) (9.3.2) in the abelian category \(\mathcal C\), we consider the category \(\operatorname{EXTPAN}(E,F)\) of the extensions panachées of \(E\) by \(F\). We then have the following:

a) \(\operatorname{EXTPAN}(E,F)\) is a groupoid. For every object of it, the group of automorphisms is canonically isomorphic to \(\operatorname{Ext}^0(Q,P)=\operatorname{Hom}(Q,P)\).

b) The set \(\operatorname{Extpan}(E,F)\) of isomorphism classes of objects of \(\operatorname{EXTPAN}(E,F)\) is empty, or is in a natural way a torsor under the group \(\operatorname{Ext}^1(Q,P)\).

c) One can define in a canonical way a class

\[(9.3.9)\qquad c(E,F)\in\operatorname{Ext}^2(Q,P),\]

whose vanishing is necessary and sufficient in order that we have \(\operatorname{Extpan}(E,F)\ne\varnothing\), i.e. in order that there exist an extension panachée of \(E\) by \(F\).

It remains to prove d). For this we note that an extension panachée of \(E\) by \(F\) can also be described as an extension \(X\) of \(E\) by \(P\), equipped with an isomorphism of extensions of \(R\) by \(P\)
\[X^1\simeq F,\]

where \(X^1\) denotes the image of the extension \(X\) under the morphism \(R\longrightarrow E\) given in (9.3.2). There exists therefore such an extension panachée if and only if there exists an element of \(\operatorname{Ext}^1(E,P)\) whose image in \(\operatorname{Ext}^1(R,P)\) is the class \(\xi\) of the extension \(F\). Considering then the exact sequence of the \(\operatorname{Ext}^i(-,P)\) associated with the second exact sequence (9.3.2):
\[\cdots\longrightarrow\operatorname{Ext}^1(E,P)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Ext}^1(R,P) \xrightarrow{\partial}\operatorname{Ext}^2(Q,P)\longrightarrow\cdots,\]
it suffices to define \(c(E,F)\) by the formula
\[(9.3.10)\qquad c(E,F)=\partial\xi,\qquad\text{where }\xi=\operatorname{cl}(F)\in\operatorname{Ext}^1(R,P).\]
Suppose now that we have an exact functor
\[(9.3.11)\qquad r:\mathcal C\longrightarrow\mathcal C'\ ,\]

where \(\mathcal C'\) is a second abelian category. Let us denote by \(P',Q',R',E',F'\) the objects of \(\mathcal C'\) deduced from \(P,Q,R,E,F\) by applying the functor \(r\), so that \(E'\) is an extension of \(Q'\) by \(R'\), and \(F'\) an extension of \(R'\) by \(P'\). It is then clear that we have compatibility, up to canonical isomorphism, in the diagram of functors

\[(9.3.12)\qquad \begin{array}{ccc} \operatorname{EXT}(Q,P)\times\operatorname{EXTPAN}(E,F)&\xrightarrow{w}&\operatorname{EXTPAN}(E,F)\\ \downarrow&&\downarrow\\ \operatorname{EXT}(Q',P')\times\operatorname{EXTPAN}(E',F')&\xrightarrow{w}&\operatorname{EXTPAN}(E',F'), \end{array}\]

where the vertical arrows denote the evident functors induced by the exact functor \(r\), and where \(w'\) is the arrow analogous to \(w\) (9.3.5), with \(\mathcal C\) replaced by \(\mathcal C'\). From this we deduce analogous compatibilities of the structures considered in 9.3.8 with the functor \(r\), which we leave to the reader to make explicit.

We shall use the following consequence of this commutativity. Suppose that \(\operatorname{EXTPAN}(E,F)\) is not empty, and let us then choose an object \(X\) in \(\operatorname{EXTPAN}(E,F)\). Then thanks to \(G'\longmapsto G'X'\) (where \(X'=r(X)\)), the category \(\operatorname{EXTPAN}(E',F')\) is equivalent to the category \(\operatorname{EXT}(Q',P')\), hence an element \(Y'\) of \(\operatorname{EXTPAN}(E',F')\) is known up to isomorphism when we know the isomorphism class of the corresponding element of \(\operatorname{EXT}(Q',P')\), i.e. when we know the corresponding element of \(\operatorname{Ext}^1(Q',P')\), say
\[c_X(Y')\in\operatorname{Ext}^1(Q',P').\]
When we replace \(X\) by another object \(X_1=HX\), where \(H\) is an extension of \(Q\) by \(P\), we see at once that we obtain
\[c_{X_1}(Y')=c_X(Y')-\operatorname{cl}(H'),\]
thanks to the associativity formula (9.3.7). Consequently, for fixed \(Y'\), and variable \(X\), the \(c_X(Y')\) run exactly through a class
\[(9.3.13)\qquad c(Y')\in\operatorname{Ext}^1(Q',P')/\operatorname{Im}\operatorname{Ext}^1(Q,P),\]

which is therefore canonically associated with the extension panachée \(Y'\) of \(E'\) by \(F'\). By construction, its vanishing is necessary and sufficient in order that the extension panachée \(Y'\) be isomorphic to the transform under \(r\) of an extension panachée \(X\) of \(E\) by \(F\).

9.4. Let \(S\) be a henselian trait, with generic point \(\eta\), \(n\) an integer \(>0\), \(\Lambda=\mathbb Z/n\mathbb Z\), \(\mathcal C\) the category of \(\Lambda\)-Modules on \(S\) (for the fppf site on \(S\), cf. VIII 0.2), \(\mathcal C'\) the category of \(\Lambda\)-Modules on \(\eta\). Let \(P,R,Q\) be objects of \(\mathcal C\), and \(E,F\) extensions of \(Q\) by \(R\) and of \(R\) by \(P\) respectively (9.3.2). We suppose that \(Q\) is a locally free Module of finite type over \(\Lambda_S\), and that \(P\) is representable by a finite group of multiplicative type over \(S\) (SGA 3 IX), hence that it is of the form

where \(D=\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(-,\mathbb G_{mS})\) denotes Cartier duality. We consider the restrictions of \(P,R,Q,E,F\) to \(\eta\), say \(P_\eta,R_\eta,\ldots\), and we suppose given an extension panachée (9.3) \(X_\eta\) of \(E_\eta\) by \(F_\eta\). Under these conditions, we shall define canonically a pairing
\[(9.4.1)\qquad c(X_\eta):Q\otimes_\Lambda Q^*\longrightarrow\Lambda_S \qquad(\Lambda=\mathbb Z/n\mathbb Z).\]
For this, let us first suppose \(S\) strictly local, so that \(Q\) and \(Q^*\) are constant, say \(Q=Q_o{}_S\), \(Q^*=Q_o^*{}_S\), and the datum of (9.4.1) amounts to that of an element
\[(9.4.2)\qquad c(X_\eta)\in\check Q_o\otimes\check Q_o^*,\]

where the sign \(\check{\ }\) denotes the ordinary dual \(\operatorname{Hom}(-,\Lambda)\). For this, we apply the considerations of 9.3, taking for \(r:\mathcal C\longrightarrow\mathcal C'\) the restriction functor. Let us note first that there exists an extension panachée of \(E\) by \(F\): indeed, the obstruction to the existence of such an extension lies in \(\operatorname{Ext}^2(Q,P)\) (9.3.8 c)), and it suffices to prove that we have
\[\operatorname{Ext}^2(Q,P)=0.\]
This is a particular case of the

blocks.title: Lemma 9.4.3.
blocks.html:
Lemma 9.4.3. Under the preceding conditions, with \(S\) strictly local, we have isomorphisms

a) \[\operatorname{Ext}^i_\Lambda(Q,P)\simeq H^i(S,\check Q\otimes P),\qquad \operatorname{Ext}^i_\Lambda(Q_\eta,P_\eta)\simeq H^i(\eta,\check Q\otimes P),\]

on the other hand we have a canonical isomorphism

b) \[\check Q\otimes P\simeq D(Q\otimes Q^*),\]

finally, for every finite constant group \(H=H_{oS}\) over \(S\), we have

c) \[H^i(S,D(H))=H^i(\eta,D(H)_\eta)=0\quad\text{for }i\geq2,\]

and canonical isomorphisms

d) \[H^1(S,D(H))\simeq G_m(S)\otimes\check H_o=V^*\otimes\check H_o,\qquad H^1(\eta,D(H)_\eta)\simeq G_m(\eta)\otimes\check H_o=K^*\otimes\check H_o,\]

where the sign \(\check{\ }\) here denotes \(\operatorname{Hom}(-,\mathbb Q/\mathbb Z)\).

The formulas a) and b) result trivially from the fact that \(Q\) is a locally free \(\Lambda_S\)-Module of finite type. To prove c), we are evidently reduced to the case where \(H_o=\mathbb Z/m\mathbb Z\), i.e. \(D(H)=\mu_m\), and then the cohomology exact sequence associated with the Kummer exact sequence
\[0\longrightarrow\mu_m\longrightarrow\mathbb G_m\xrightarrow{m.\mathrm{id}}\mathbb G_m\longrightarrow0\]
reduces us to proving the formulas
\[H^i(S,\mathbb G_m)=H^i(\eta,\mathbb G_m)=0\quad\text{for }i\geq1.\]

The \(H^i\) are equal to the \(H^i_{\mathrm{et}}\) computed for the étale site [6, th. 11.7]; they are therefore zero for \(S\) since \(S\) is strictly local. The fact that they are also zero for \(\eta\) is a well-known fact [6, th. 1.1].
It remains to describe the isomorphisms d). In fact, over an arbitrary base \(U\), we have a canonical homomorphism
\[(9.4.3.1)\qquad G_m(U)\otimes\check H_o\longrightarrow H^1(U,D(H_{oU})),\]

which is described, when \(H_o\) is annihilated by the integer \(m\geq0\), with the aid of the coboundary homomorphism \(G_m(U)\xrightarrow{\partial}H^1(U,\mu_m)\) associated with the Kummer exact sequence, by taking the composite
\[G_m(U)\otimes\check H_o\longrightarrow H^1(U,\mu_m)\otimes\check H_o \longrightarrow H^1(U,\mu_m\otimes\check H_o)\longrightarrow H^1(U,D(H_{oS})),\]

where the second arrow is deduced from the evident homomorphism \(\check H_o\longrightarrow\operatorname{Hom}(\mu_m,\mu_m\otimes\check H_o)\), and the third is a particular case of b) (in which \(n\) is replaced by \(m\)). We leave to the reader the task of verifying that (9.4.3.1) does not depend on the choice of the integer \(m\) such that \(mH_o=0\). On the other hand I claim that the homomorphism (9.4.3.1) is always injective, and that its cokernel is zero if \(\operatorname{Pic}(U)=0\). (In fact, the cokernel is always isomorphic to \(\operatorname{Hom}(H_o,\operatorname{Pic}(U))\), but that matters little here.) To see this, we are indeed reduced to the case where \(H_o\) is of the form \(\mathbb Z/n\mathbb Z\), and then the assertion follows at once from the cohomology exact sequence associated with the Kummer exact sequence (*) above. This proves formula d). From it we conclude at once:

blocks.title: Corollary 9.4.4.
blocks.html:
Corollary 9.4.4. With the notations of 9.4.3 we have a canonical isomorphism
\[\text{e)}\qquad H^1(\eta,D(H)_\eta)/\operatorname{Im}H^1(S,D(H))\simeq\check H_o,\]

whence (combining with a) and b)) a canonical isomorphism

\[(9.4.5)\qquad \operatorname{Ext}^1(Q_\eta,P_\eta)/\operatorname{Im}\operatorname{Ext}^1(Q,P) \simeq\check Q_o\otimes\check Q_o^*.\]

Returning to the definition of (9.4.2) when \(S\) is strictly local, we note that the relation \(\operatorname{Ext}^2(Q,P)=0\) implies the possibility of defining a canonical element (9.3.13), where we take for \(Y'\) the given extension panachée \(X_\eta\) of \(E_\eta\) by \(F_\eta\). The formula (9.4.5) makes it possible to identify this element \(c(X_\eta)\) with an element of \(\check Q_o\otimes\check Q_o^*\), which makes precise the definition of (9.4.2). We find moreover, by virtue of the general considerations of 9.3, that the element in question can be interpreted as the obstruction to extending \(X_\eta\) to an extension panachée \(X\) of \(E\) by \(F\).
9.4.6. The definition of (9.4.1) in the case where \(S\) is henselian now reduces immediately to the strictly local case, by Galois descent from the strict henselization of \(S\).

blocks.title: Proposition 9.4.7.
blocks.html:
Proposition 9.4.7. The data \(P,Q,R,E,F\) are as above, except that it is unnecessary to suppose the trait \(S\) henselian. We consider the functor
\[(9.4.7.1)\qquad X\longmapsto X_\eta\]

from the category of extensions panachées of \(E\) by \(F\) into the category of extensions panachées of \(E_\eta\) by \(F_\eta\). For every extension panachée \(X_\eta\) of \(E_\eta\) by \(F_\eta\), we consider the pairing

\[(9.4.7.2)\qquad c_o(X_\eta):Q_o\otimes Q_o^*\longrightarrow\Lambda_s\]

induced by the pairing of type (9.4.1) defined over the henselization \(S^h\) of \(S\). This granted, the functor (9.4.7.1) is fully faithful, and for an extension panachée \(X_\eta\) of \(E_\eta\) by \(F_\eta\), \(X_\eta\) belongs to the essential image of this functor if and only if the pairing (9.4.7.2) is zero.

9.4.7.3. This statement retains a meaning and remains valid when we assume only that \(S\) is a connected regular noetherian scheme of dimension 1: instead of a single pairing (9.4.7.2), we must then consider a pairing \(Q_s\otimes Q_s^*\longrightarrow\Lambda_s\) for each closed point \(s\) of \(S\). It is in this more general form that it will be most convenient for us to prove 9.4.7.
a) For every extension panachée \(X\), the map
\[\operatorname{End}(X)\longrightarrow\operatorname{End}(X_\eta)\]

is bijective. As the two members are respectively the sections of \(\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(P,Q)=D(Q\otimes Q^*)\) over \(S\) and over \(\eta\), the assertion comes from the fact that \(S\) is normal and that \(D(Q\otimes Q^*)\) is finite over \(S\). From this it follows at once that if \(X\) and \(Y\) are two extensions panachées, and if we already know that they are isomorphic, then
\[\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(X,Y)\longrightarrow\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}(X_\eta,Y_\eta)\]
is bijective.

b) If \(X\) and \(Y\) are two extensions panachées, every isomorphism \(X_\eta\xrightarrow{\sim}Y_\eta\) comes from an isomorphism \(X\xrightarrow{\sim}Y\). Thanks to a) the question is local for the étale topology, which allows us to reduce to the case where \(S\) is strictly local. But in this case 9.4.3 shows us that the map
\[\operatorname{Ext}^1(P,Q)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Ext}^1(P_\eta,Q_\eta)\]
is identified with the map
\[V^*\otimes\check H_o\longrightarrow K^*\otimes\check H_o\]

which is injective (since the exact sequence \(0\longrightarrow V^*\longrightarrow K^*\longrightarrow\mathbb Z\longrightarrow0\) is split), hence it is injective, which implies (thanks to Corollary 9.3) that if \(X_\eta\) and \(Y_\eta\) are isomorphic, the same is true of \(X\) and \(Y\). We therefore conclude by a).
Obviously a) and b) imply that the functor \(X\longmapsto X_\eta\) is fully faithful.

c) Let \(X_\eta\) be an extension panachée over \(\eta\); let us prove that it extends to \(S\) if and only if the pairings (9.4.6.2) are zero. Here again, the uniqueness already proved shows that the question is local for the étale topology, which again reduces us to the case \(S\) strictly local. Then the conclusion is true by construction, as was pointed out just before 9.4.6.
Thus the proof of 9.4.7 is complete.
9.5. Suppose now fixed a prime number \(\ell\), Barsotti-Tate pro-\(\ell\)-groups over \(S\),
\[(9.5.1)\qquad P=(P(n))_{n\geq0},\quad R=(R(n))_{n\geq0},\quad Q=(Q(n))_{n\geq0},\]
and extensions of Barsotti-Tate pro-\(\ell\)-groups
\[(9.5.2)\qquad 0\longrightarrow P\longrightarrow F\longrightarrow R\longrightarrow0,\qquad 0\longrightarrow R\longrightarrow E\longrightarrow Q\longrightarrow0,\]
thus defining for every integer \(n\geq0\) extensions of \(\Lambda_n\)-Modules where \(\Lambda_n=\mathbb Z/\ell^{n+1}\mathbb Z\):
\[(9.5.3)\qquad 0\longrightarrow P(n)\longrightarrow F(n)\longrightarrow R(n)\longrightarrow0,\qquad 0\longrightarrow R(n)\longrightarrow E(n)\longrightarrow Q(n)\longrightarrow0.\]

We shall assume \(Q\) of étale type and \(P\) of multiplicative type. Suppose finally given over \(\eta\) a Barsotti-Tate pro-\(\ell\)-group \(X_\eta\) which is a extension panachée of \(E_\eta\) by \(F_\eta\), by which we mean that it is equipped with a three-step filtration (as a Barsotti-Tate pro-\(\ell\)-group), giving rise to partial extensions equipped with isomorphisms with \(E_\eta\) and \(F_\eta\) respectively. It follows that for every \(n\), \(X_\eta(n)\) is equipped with a structure of extension panachée of \(E(n)_\eta\) by \(F(n)_\eta\). We can then associate to it a pairing of type (9.4.1):
\[(9.5.4)\qquad c(X(n)_\eta):Q(n)\otimes Q^*(n)\longrightarrow\Lambda_n=\mathbb Z/\ell^{n+1}\mathbb Z,\]
where the \(Q^*(n)\) are the finite étale groups over \(S\) defined by
\[P(n)\simeq D(Q^*(n)),\qquad\text{i.e.}\qquad Q^*(n)=D(P(n)),\]

\(D\) again denoting Cartier duality. The \(Q^*(n)\) form a pro-\(\ell\)-group in an obvious way, say \(Q^*\). We leave to the reader the task of verifying that for variable \(n\), the homomorphisms (9.5.4) define a homomorphism of projective systems, i.e. a homomorphism of twisted constant \(\ell\)-adic sheaves over \(S\):
\[(9.5.5)\qquad c(X_\eta):Q\otimes Q^*\longrightarrow\mathbb Z_{\ell S}.\]
Let us point out in passing the following result (which will not be used in the sequel):

blocks.title: Proposition 9.5.6.
blocks.html:
Proposition 9.5.6. The data \(P,Q,R,E,F\) are the same as above, except that it is unnecessary to assume the trait \(S\) henselian. We consider the functor
\[(9.5.6.1)\qquad X\longmapsto X_\eta\]

from the category of Barsotti-Tate extensions panachées of \(E\) by \(F\) to the category of extensions panachées of \(E_\eta\) by \(F_\eta\). For every Barsotti-Tate group extension panachée \(X_\eta\) of \(E_\eta\) by \(F_\eta\), we consider the corresponding pairing

\[(9.5.6.2)\qquad Q_o\otimes Q_o^*\longrightarrow\mathbb Z_{\ell s},\]

induced by the pairing of type (9.5.5) defined over the henselization of \(S\). This granted, the functor (9.5.6.1) is fully faithful, and its essential image consists of the Barsotti-Tate groups \(X_\eta\) extensions panachées of \(E_\eta\) by \(F_\eta\) such that the corresponding pairing (9.5.6.2) is zero. Moreover, when \(\operatorname{car}K=0\) (so that we may apply the theorem of TATE [33 th. 4]), then \(X_\eta\) belongs to the essential image if and only if \(X_\eta\) extends to a Barsotti-Tate group over \(S\).
9.5.6.3. We leave to the reader the task of extending this statement to the case where we assume only that \(S\) is a connected regular noetherian scheme of dimension 1.

The first assertion of 9.5.6 is an immediate consequence of 9.4.6, applied to the components of the Barsotti-Tate groups under consideration. It remains to prove that if a Barsotti-Tate group \(X_\eta\), an extension panachée of \(E_\eta\) by \(F_\eta\), extends to a Barsotti-Tate group \(X\) over \(S\), it also extends as an extension panachée. Now by virtue of the theorem of TATE [33], the homomorphism \(X_\eta\longrightarrow Q_\eta\) extends uniquely to a homomorphism
\[X\longrightarrow Q.\]

For every integer \(n\), the corresponding homomorphism \(X(n)\longrightarrow Q(n)\), being surjective on the generic fibers, is surjective; as \(Q(n)\) is étale over \(S\), hence \(X(n)\longrightarrow Q(n)\) is flat, it follows that it is an epimorphism. Hence \(X\longrightarrow Q\) is an epimorphism component by component. If then \(\overline F\) denotes the kernel of \(X\longrightarrow Q\), it follows that \(\overline F\) is a Barsotti-Tate group and \(X\) is an extension of \(Q\) by \(\overline F\). Moreover the isomorphism \(F_\eta\xrightarrow\sim\overline F_\eta\) extends, by the theorem of TATE, to an isomorphism \(F\xrightarrow\sim\overline F\). Consequently, \(X\) appears as an extension of \(Q\) by \(F\). Considering then the quotient \(\overline E=X/P\), we see again, by applying the theorem of TATE, that we have an isomorphism \(E\xrightarrow\sim\overline E\). It follows that \(X\) is an extension panachée of \(E\) by \(F\) extending the extension panachée \(X_\eta\), qed.
Remarks 9.5.6.4. a) Of course, the last assertion of 9.5.6 should be valid without restriction on \(K\), since this should be the case for the theorem of Tate invoked.

b) One can give another proof of the good reduction criterion 5.10, by reducing as in 5.10 to the case where \(A_K\) has semi-stable reduction, and then applying 9.5.6 to \(T_\ell(A_K)\) considered as an extension panachée (cf. 9.6), which implies \(u_\ell=0\), and using 11.6.2 a) below which implies that we have \(T_\ell(A_K)^t=0\).

9.5.8. When \(\ell\ne p\), the Barsotti-Tate groups under consideration over \(S\) resp. over \(K\) are interpreted simply in terms of twisted constant \(\ell\)-adic sheaves over \(S\), or equivalently, in terms of representations of \(\pi_o=\operatorname{Gal}(\bar k/k)\) resp. of \(\pi=\operatorname{Gal}(\bar K/K)\) on free \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-modules of finite type. One then observes that the homomorphism (9.5.6) is the canonical homomorphism which expresses the action of the inertia subgroup \(I\subset\pi\) on \(X_\eta(\bar K)\), defined by the method of 9.2. To verify this, we reduce immediately to the situation of 9.4, assuming there that \(n\) is equal to a power of \(\ell\), and that \(R\) is a locally constant \(\Lambda\)-Module (just like \(Q\) and \(P\)), so that the same is also true of \(E\), \(F\) and \(X_\eta\). The verification of the identity of the two definitions is then left to the reader.

9.6. We are now in a position to define the announced canonical pairing (9.1.2), without restriction on \(\ell\), in such a way that for \(\ell\ne p\) this definition coincides with the one given in 9.2. For this, we apply the considerations of 9.5 to the following situation:
\[(9.6.1)\qquad\left\{\begin{aligned} P&=T_\ell(A^o)^t=\check M'_\ell(1),& Q&=M_\ell,& R&=T_\ell(A^o)^f/T_\ell(A^o)^t,\\ F&=T_\ell(A^o)^f,& E&=D(F')=D(T_\ell(A'^o)^f), \end{aligned}\right.\]

where in the last formula \(D\) denotes the Cartier dual in the sense of Barsotti-Tate pro-\(\ell\)-groups, i.e. \(D(Y)\) is the projective system of the \(D(Y(n))\), for the usual transition morphisms. The structure of \(F\) as an extension of \(R\) by \(P\) is clear. Similarly we have on \(F'=T_\ell(A'^o)^f\) a structure of extension of \(R'\) by \(P'=T_\ell(A'^o)^t=\check M_\ell(1)\). Applying Cartier duality, we conclude that \(E=D(F')\) is equipped with a structure of extension of \(D(P')=M_\ell\) by \(D(R')\). Now \(D(R')\) is canonically isomorphic to \(R\) by virtue of 5.5 (proved in 7.4.6), which makes precise the structure of \(E\) as an extension of \(Q\) by \(R\).
As extension panachée \(X_\eta\) of \(E_\eta\) by \(F_\eta\), we shall take
\[(9.6.2)\qquad X_\eta=T_\ell(A_K)=X_\eta^o\supset X_\eta^1=T_\ell(A_K)^f\supset X_\eta^2=T_\ell(A'_K)^t\supset0.\]
The isomorphism of extensions of \(R_\eta\) by \(P_\eta\)
\[X_\eta^1\xrightarrow\sim F_\eta\]
is clear. The isomorphism of extensions of \(Q\) by \(R\):
\[X_\eta/X_\eta^2\xrightarrow\sim E_\eta=D(F'_\eta)\]
is clear thanks to the orthogonality theorem 5.2.

We therefore have at our disposal the data required to define a pairing (9.5.6), which here (as \(Q^*=\check M'_\ell\)) takes the form (9.1.2). The fact that this definition is compatible with that of 9.2 follows from 9.5.8. The considerations of 5.8 show on the other hand, subject to the validity of the theorem of TATE [33] (hence in any case when \(\operatorname{car}K=0\)) that the pairing in question can be described in terms of the Barsotti-Tate \(\ell\)-pro-group \(X_\eta=T_\ell(A_K)\) over \(S\) alone (since its three-step filtration, and the extensions \(F\) and \(E\) which extend \(X_\eta^1\) and \(X_\eta/X_\eta^2\), can be defined canonically in terms of the pro-\(\ell\)-group \(X_\eta=T_\ell(A_K)\) alone).

Remark 9.7. Let us no longer necessarily assume that \(A_K\) has semi-stable reduction over the henselian trait \(S\), and let \(K'\) be a finite Galois extension of \(K\) such that \(A_{K'}\) has semi-stable reduction over the normalization \(S'\) of \(S\) in \(K'\) (3.6). With the notations of (8.1.3), we then define, by Galois descent from the monodromy pairing for \(A_{K'}\),
a pairing
\[(*)\qquad u_\ell^{K'}:\underline M_K\otimes\underline M'_K\longrightarrow\mathbb Z_{\ell K}.\]
The latter a priori depends on the choice of \(K'\), but it follows from (10.4.3) below that the pairing
\[(9.7.1)\qquad u_\ell:\underline M_K\otimes\underline M'_K\longrightarrow\mathbb Q_{\ell K}\]
given by

does not depend on it, where \(e(S'/S)=\operatorname{long}(V'/\mathfrak mV')\). We may again call (9.7.1) the monodromy pairing for the abelian scheme \(A_K\) over \(K\) and the prime number \(\ell\) under consideration, since in the case where \(A_K\) itself has semi-stable reduction, it obviously coincides with the restriction to \(\eta\) of the monodromy pairing (9.1.2). It follows from 10.4 below that this pairing is in fact always with values in \(\mathbb Q_K\), and is independent of \(\ell\).

One may ask whether it takes values in \(\mathbb Z_K\), which amounts to saying that (9.7.1) takes values in \(\mathbb Z_{\ell K}\) for every prime number \(\ell\). This is the case at least if \(A_K\) is an elliptic curve, the only case requiring a verification being then that where \(\underline M_K\simeq\underline M'_K\) has rank 1, so that \(\underline M_K\otimes\underline M'_K\) is canonically isomorphic to \(\mathbb Z_K\), and the monodromy pairing (9.7.1) can be interpreted simply as an ordinary rational number. One finds thanks to the results of NÉRON [19] that the latter is equal to \(v(j)\), \(j\) being the absolute invariant of \(A_K\) and \(v\) the normalized valuation of \(K\).

pages.title: 10. Properties of the monodromy pairing. Theorem of integrality and positivity

blocks.html:
10. Properties of the monodromy pairing. Theorem of integrality and
positivity

In the present section, we shall first give certain essentially trivial formal properties of the monodromy pairings, the (somewhat tedious) details of the verifications being left to the reader. On the other hand, we state a deeper theorem (10.4) concerning these pairings, and indicate how one can reduce its proof to a special case of Jacobians, which will be treated (by transcendental means) in § 12 (which is moreover logically independent of § 11).
10.1. Transport of structure. It is clear, from the definition given of the monodromy pairing (9.1.2), that this pairing is compatible with transport of structure in the situation \((S,A_K,A'_K)\).

10.2. Symmetry. If we start from the dual scheme \(A'_K\) of \(A_K\) and apply to it the construction of § 9, we must introduce the dual scheme \((A'_K)'\) of \(A_K\), which is canonically isomorphic to \(A_K\) by the biduality theorem ([5] or VIII 3.2). Let us denote by \(u_\ell^{A'_K}\) the monodromy pairing (9.1.2) relative to \(A'_K\), so that we similarly find a pairing
\[(10.2.1)\qquad u_\ell^{A'_K}:\underline M'_\ell\otimes\underline M_\ell\longrightarrow\mathbb Z_\ell.\]
This granted, I claim that the latter is deduced from \(u_\ell^{A_K}\) by the symmetry
\[s:\underline M_\ell\otimes\underline M'_\ell\xrightarrow\sim\underline M'_\ell\otimes\underline M_\ell,\]
i.e. we have the formula
\[(10.2.2)\qquad u_\ell^{A'_K}=u_\ell^{A_K}\circ s.\]

Taking into account the known anticommutativity property of the pairings \(\varphi_\xi\) ([5] or VIII 2.2.11), the symmetry formula (10.2.2) can be reformulated as follows, in terms of a fixed polarization \(\xi\) of \(A_K\), which defines a homomorphism \(\widetilde\xi:A_K\longrightarrow A'_K\), whence a corresponding homomorphism (t being the initial of "toric")
\[(10.2.3)\qquad \xi_t:\underline M\longrightarrow\underline M',\]
allowing us to form the pairing
\[(10.2.4)\qquad \varphi^t_{\xi,\ell}:\underline M_\ell\otimes\underline M_\ell\longrightarrow\mathbb Z_\ell, \qquad \varphi^t_{\xi,\ell}(x,y)=u_\ell^{A_K}(x,\xi_t(y));\]
the preceding form \(\varphi^t_{\xi,\ell}\) is symmetric:
\[(10.2.5)\qquad \varphi^t_{\xi,\ell}(x,y)=\varphi^t_{\xi,\ell}(y,x).\]

In fact, this statement is valid for every element \(\xi\) of the Néron-Severi group of \(A_K\) (not necessarily a polarization); but it is only when \(\xi\) is non-degenerate, i.e. defines an isogeny \(A_K\to A'_K\) hence also an isogeny (10.2.3), that the symmetry relation (10.2.5) is equivalent to the symmetry relation (10.2.2). Moreover in the case where \(\xi\) is a polarization, we shall considerably sharpen (10.2.5) in 10.4 b) below.

Let us finally point out a third way of interpreting the symmetry relation. For this, consider a biextension \(= correspondance divisorielle\) \(\xi\) on a pair \((A_K,\overline A_K)\) of abelian schemes with semi-stable reduction over \(K\), which defines a homomorphism
\[A_K\longrightarrow\overline A'_K,\]

and by passage to the Néron models therefore defines a homomorphism on the maximal tori of the special fibers, hence in the opposite direction a homomorphism on the character Groups, whence a homomorphism
\[(10.2.6)\qquad \xi_t:\underline{\overline M}\longrightarrow\underline M',\]
where \(\underline{\overline M}\) is defined in terms of \(\overline A_K\) as \(\underline M\) is in terms of \(A_K\). We can therefore form the pairing
\[(10.2.7)\qquad \varphi^t_\xi:\underline M_\ell\otimes\underline{\overline M}_\ell\longrightarrow\mathbb Z_\ell, \qquad \varphi^t_\xi(x,y)=\varphi^t(x,\xi_t(y)).\]

Let us note in passing that for a variable abelian scheme \(A_K\) with semi-stable reduction, the twisted constant Group \(\underline M\) is a covariant functor in \(A_K\), and that then the formation of the pairings (10.2.7) associated to biextensions of abelian schemes with semi-stable reduction over \((K,S)\) is compatible, in an obvious sense, with the formation of inverse images of biextensions. Of course, when \(\xi\) is the canonical ("Weil") biextension of \((A_K,A'_K)\), the pairing (10.2.7) is none other than the monodromy pairing (9.2.1). — This granted, the symmetry relation can be expressed as follows: consider the symmetric \({}^s\xi\) of \(\xi\), which is a biextension of \((\overline A_K,A_K)\), whence a pairing
\[\varphi^t_{{}^s\xi}:\underline{\overline M}_\ell\otimes\underline M_\ell\longrightarrow\mathbb Z_\ell;\]
we then have the symmetry relation

\[(10.2.8)\qquad \varphi^t_{{}^s\xi}=\varphi^t_\xi\circ s, \quad\text{where}\quad s:\underline{\overline M}_\ell\otimes\underline M_\ell\xrightarrow\sim \underline M_\ell\otimes\underline{\overline M}_\ell\]
is again the symmetry isomorphism. One may compare this relation with the antisymmetry relation of VIII 2.2.11.
10.3. Change of trait. Now let
\[(10.3.1)\qquad g:S'\longrightarrow S\]
be a dominant morphism of henselian traits, associated to an injective local homomorphism of discrete valuation rings
\[V\longrightarrow V';\]
let us set as usual
\[(10.3.2)\qquad e(S'/S)=\operatorname{long}_{V'}V'/\mathfrak mV',\]
\(\mathfrak m\) being the maximal ideal of \(V\). It is then known that the homomorphism induced by (10.3.1) on the "tame" parts of the inertia groups
\[(10.3.3)\qquad g_*:I_t'\simeq\prod_{\ell\ne p}\mathbb Z_\ell(1)(k') \longrightarrow I_t\simeq\prod_{\ell\ne p}\mathbb Z_\ell(1)(k)\]
is given by
identifying the two members of (10.3.3) by means of the obvious isomorphisms \(\mathbb Z_\ell(1)(k)\xrightarrow\sim\mathbb Z_\ell(1)(k')\) \((I_{k'/k})\).

Let us note on the other hand that, for an abelian scheme \(A_K\) over \(K\) with semi-stable reduction over \(S\), the formation of the twisted lattices \(\underline M\), \(\underline M'\) commutes with every base change \(S'\to S\) as above, as follows at once from the definitions (in particular 3.4). We can therefore consider the pairing \(u_\ell^{A_{K'}}\) defined by \(A_{K'}\) (\(K'\) = field of fractions of \(V'\)) as a pairing
\[u_\ell^{A_{K'}}:\underline M_{\ell S'}\otimes\underline M'_{\ell S'}\longrightarrow\mathbb Z_{\ell S'},\]

and compare it with the pairing \(u_{\ell S'}^{A_K}\) deduced from \(u_\ell^{A_K}\) by base change. When \(\ell\ne p\), the construction of the pairings \(u_\ell\) given in 9.2, combined with formula (10.2.2), shows immediately that we then have

This formula in fact remains valid also for \(\ell=p\), as one sees by going back to the general construction given in 9.3 to 9.6, reducing to a compatibility statement analogous to (10.3.5) for the isomorphism 9.4.4 e), for a change of strictly local traits \(S'\longrightarrow S\). (One will note that the isomorphism in question is deduced from the isomorphism \(K^*/V^*\xrightarrow\sim\mathbb Z\) deduced from the norm, thus giving rise to the commutative diagram
\[\xymatrix{ K^*/V^* \ar[r]^{\sim}\ar[d] & \mathbb Z\ar[d]^{e(S'/S)}\\ K'^*/V'^* \ar[r]^{\sim} & \mathbb Z. }\]

Here is a deeper result concerning the monodromy pairings, of which we shall give a proof by transcendental means in § 12, relying on results which will be proved later in the Seminar. (We shall moreover not use 10.4 in the sequel of the Seminar, any more than the other results of the present exposé.)

blocks.title: Theorem 10.4.
blocks.html:
Theorem 10.4.

Let \(S\) be a henselian trait, \(A_K\) an abelian scheme over \(K\) with semi-stable reduction over \(S\). Consider the lattices \(M\) and \(M'\) associated as in 9.1 (cf. 5.5.2). Then we have the following:

a) There exists a unique pairing

\[(10.4.1)\qquad u:M\otimes M'\longrightarrow\mathbb Z_S\]

such that for every prime number \(\ell\), \(u\otimes_{\mathbb Z}\mathbb Z_\ell\) is the monodromy pairing \(u_\ell\) of (9.1.2). This pairing is separating.

b) Let \(\xi\) be a polarization of \(A_K\), thus defining a homomorphism (10.2.3)

\[\xi_t:M\longrightarrow M',\]

and consider the form

\[(10.4.2)\qquad \varphi_\xi^t:M\otimes M\longrightarrow\mathbb Z_S, \qquad \varphi^t(x,y)=u(x,\xi_t(y)).\]

This form is symmetric, positive and non-degenerate.
Of course, in terms of the geometric fiber \(M_o\) of \(M\) at a given geometric point over \(S\), the preceding assertion means that the corresponding form
\[M_o\otimes M_o\longrightarrow\mathbb Z\]
is symmetric, positive and non-degenerate. As for the pairing (10.4.1), we shall again call it the monodromy pairing.
10.5. Reduction of 10.4 to a particular case

10.5.1. The uniqueness of a \(u\) satisfying the stated condition (even for a single \(\ell\)) is clear. On the other hand, the existence simply means that each \(u_\ell\) in fact maps \(M\otimes M'\) into \(\mathbb Z_S\), and that the homomorphism thus obtained \(M\otimes M'\longrightarrow\mathbb Z_S\) is independent of \(\ell\). In fact, it even suffices to prove the analogous assertion for the \(u_\ell\otimes_{\mathbb Z_\ell}\mathbb Q_\ell\), and one \(u_0:M_0\otimes M'_0\longrightarrow\mathbb Q_S\).
Suppose then that the existence has been established after a base change \(S'\longrightarrow S\) as in 9.3. Then it follows immediately from (10.3.5) that there exists a homomorphism
\[u:M\otimes M'\longrightarrow\frac1{e(S'/S)}\mathbb Z_S\subset\mathbb Q_S\]
compatible with the \(u_\ell\otimes_{\mathbb Z_\ell}\mathbb Q_\ell\), whence a). As for b), it is then trivial that if it is true for \(A_{K'}\), it is also true for \(A_K\).

Consequently, we may, in order to prove 10.4, allow ourselves any dominant change of traits \(S'\longrightarrow S\). This allows us in particular to assume \(S\) complete with algebraically closed residue field. In this case, \(M\) and \(M'\) are constant group schemes, whose values \(M,M'\) are free modules of finite type over \(\mathbb Z\), and a) and b) can advantageously be reformulated in terms of the latter.

10.5.2. Suppose now that \(A_K\) is isomorphic to a direct factor of an abelian scheme \(\overline A_K\) over \(K\) with semi-stable reduction, and that 10.4 is proved for the latter. Then it follows at once that 10.4 is valid also for \(A_K\), taking into account the obvious functorial behavior of the lattices \(M,M'\), and of the monodromy pairings, with respect to direct sums of abelian schemes with semi-stable reduction.

It is likewise immediate, thanks to 10.5.1, that the validity of 10.4 depends only on the isogeny class of \(A_K\). Combined with the preceding remark, and with the "Poincaré reducibility theorem" [15], this shows us that if \(A_K\) is a quotient of an abelian scheme \(\overline A_K\) for which 10.4 is valid, the same holds for \(A_K\).

10.5.3. Let us now use the well-known fact [31, th. 11 (p.20)] that, after possibly making a finite extension over \(K\) (which is permissible, thanks to 10.5.1), every abelian scheme \(A_K\) is a quotient of the Jacobian \((=\operatorname{Pic}_{X_K/K}^o)\) of a proper, smooth and geometrically connected curve \(X_K\) over \(K\). Taking 10.5.2 into account, we are thus reduced to proving 10.4 in the case of such a Jacobian.

10.5.4. By a theorem of ARTIN-WINMORD [2] (already cited and used in V), after possibly making a further finite extension \(K'\) of \(K\), we may assume that \(X_K\) extends to a projective and flat scheme over \(S\), regular, and such that the geometric special fiber \(X_{\bar s}\) has as its only singular points ordinary quadratic singular points (i.e. here, normal crossings of two branches). In this case, we shall prove 10.4 a) in 12.5 below, by making the monodromy pairing (10.4.1) entirely explicit in terms of the geometric data of the situation, and we shall verify at the same time 10.4 b) in the case of the canonical polarization of the Jacobian.

10.5.5. It remains to see that if for a given \(A_K\) with semi-stable reduction, 10.4 a) is true, and 10.4 b) is true for one given polarization \(\xi_o\) of \(A_K\), then this conclusion remains true for every polarization \(\xi\) of \(A_K\). Indeed, if \(\widetilde\xi_o\), \(\widetilde\xi\) denote the homomorphisms
\[\widetilde\xi_o,\ \widetilde\xi:A_K\longrightarrow A'_K\]
defined by \(\xi_o\), \(\xi\), which are therefore isogenies, we shall have
\[\widetilde\xi=\widetilde\xi_o\,\alpha={}^{t}\!\alpha^*\,\widetilde\xi_o, \quad\text{with }\alpha\in\operatorname{End}(A_K)\otimes_{\mathbb Z}\mathbb Q,\]

where \(\alpha\longmapsto\alpha^*\) denotes the canonical involution of the algebra \(\operatorname{End}(A_K)\otimes_{\mathbb Z}\mathbb Q\) defined by the polarization \(\xi_o\), and where the exponent \(t\) in \({}^{t}(\alpha^*)\) denotes the transpose (associating to an endomorphism of \(A_K\) an endomorphism of \(A'_K\)). On the other hand, it is known [15, Chap V th.3] that we have
\[\alpha=\sum_i\beta_i^*\beta_i, \quad\text{with }\beta_i\in\operatorname{End}(A_K)\otimes_{\mathbb Z}\mathbb Q,\]
whence
\[\widetilde\xi=\sum_i{}^t\!\beta_i\,\widetilde\xi_o\,\beta_i,\]
or again, in terms of the biextensions \(= correspondances divisorielles\) associated to the polarizations under consideration (VIII 4.14):
\[(10.5.5.1)\qquad \delta(\xi)=\sum_i\delta(\xi_o)\circ(\beta_i,\beta_i).\]
By what was said after (10.2.7), this formula implies (with the notations of (10.4.2))
\[(10.5.5.2)\qquad \varphi_\xi^t=\sum_i\varphi_{\xi_o}^t(\beta_i,\beta_i) \quad\in\operatorname{Hom}(M_\mathbb Q\otimes_\mathbb Q M_\mathbb Q,\mathbb Q).\]
It indeed implies that if \(\varphi_{\xi_o}^t\) is symmetric and positive, the same is true of \(\varphi_\xi^t\).

Finally, the fact that \(\varphi_{\xi_o}^t\) is non-degenerate is obviously equivalent to the assertion that the pairing (10.4.1) is separating (taking into account that \(\xi_{o t}:M\otimes_\mathbb Z\mathbb Q\longrightarrow M'\otimes_\mathbb Z\mathbb Q\) is an isomorphism, \(\widetilde\xi_o\) being an isogeny), and is therefore likewise independent of the chosen polarization. One may moreover note that to verify that (10.4.1) is separating, it suffices to verify the same assertion for the monodromy pairing \(u_\ell\) (9.1.2) for one prime number \(\ell\). Taking \(\ell\ne p\), we can use the description 9.2 of \(u_\ell\); the fact that \(u_\ell\) is separating on the left (hence on both sides, for a reason of rank) is then trivial.

pages.title: 11. Connected components of the Néron model: duality relation, asymptotic behavior

blocks.html:
11. Connected components of the Néron model: duality relation,
asymptotic behavior
11.0. The aim of the present section is the study of the finite étale \(k\)-group

already considered in 1. For every prime number \(\ell\), we denote by \(\Phi_o(\ell)\) the \(\ell\)-primary component of the latter, so that the knowledge of \(\Phi_o\) is equivalent to that of the \(\Phi_o(\ell)\), thanks to the formula
\[(11.0.2)\qquad \Phi_o=\prod_\ell\Phi_o(\ell).\]

Let us note that the groups \(\Phi_o\), \(\Phi_o(\ell)\) are determined respectively by the knowledge of the ordinary finite groups \(\Phi_o(\bar k)\), \(\Phi_o(\ell)(\bar k)\) and of the natural action of \(\pi_o=\operatorname{Gal}(\bar k/k)\) on the latter. On the other hand, it is clear that \(\Phi_o(\bar k)\) is also the group of connected components of the Néron model of \(A_{\widetilde K}\), where \(\widetilde K\) is the field of fractions of the strict henselization of \(S\) relative to the chosen separable closure \(\bar k\) of \(k\). This therefore allows us, for the study of \(\Phi_o\), to reduce to the case where \(S\) is strictly local, — the actions of \(\pi_o=\operatorname{Gal}(\widetilde K/K)\) on \(\Phi_o(\bar k)\) being recovered automatically by transport of structure. One could similarly reduce to the case where \(S\) is moreover complete, thanks to the base change \(\widehat S\longrightarrow S\), thanks to the fact that the formation of the Néron model commutes with it [21, th. 3.4 b)].
11.1. Suppose then \(S\) strictly local, so that \(\Phi_o\) can be identified with an ordinary finite group \(\Phi_o(k)\), and we find
\[(11.1.1)\qquad \Phi_o(k)\simeq A_o(k)/A_o^o(k)\simeq A(S)/A^o(S)\simeq A(K)/A(K)^o,\]

where the first equality comes from the fact that \(A_o\) is smooth over \(k\), the second from the "Hensel lemma" taking into account the fact that \(A\) is smooth over \(S\) and \(S\) henselian, and finally the third comes from the relation \(A(S)\xrightarrow\sim A(K)\) and from the fact that we set
\[(11.1.2)\qquad A(K)^o\mathrel{\mathop=^{\rm df}} A^o(S),\]

so that \(A(K)^o\) appears as a natural subgroup of finite index in \(A(K)\) (not to be confused with \(A^o(K)=A(K)\)!). The isomorphisms (11.1.1) are obviously functorial in \(A\), and compatible with transport of structure in \((S,A_K)\), and in particular with the action of possible Galois groups.

Suppose now that \(\ell\) is a prime number \(\ne p\), so that \(A^o\) is \(\ell\)-divisible, and more precisely \(\ell\,\operatorname{id}_{A^o}\) is an étale and surjective morphism, so that this morphism induces a surjective endomorphism of \(A^o(S)=A(K)^o\), which is therefore an \(\ell\)-divisible group. It follows at once that for every integer \(n>0\) prime to \(p=\operatorname{car}(k)\), we have the relation
\[(11.1.3)\qquad {}_n\Phi_o(k)\simeq {}_nA(K)/{}_nA(K)^o, \qquad\text{for }(n,p)=1.\]
Now by definition (2.2.3) of the fixed part of \({}_nA_K\), we have
\[(11.1.4)\qquad {}_nA(K)^o={}_n(A_K)^f(\bar K)\simeq T_n(A_K)^f(\bar K)\otimes_{\mathbb Z_n}\mathbb Z/n\mathbb Z.\]
Taking for \(n\) a power of \(\ell\), and setting as in (2.5.3)
\[(11.1.5)\qquad U=T_\ell(A(\bar K)),\quad\text{whence }T_\ell(A(\bar K))^f=U^I,\]
where \(U^I\) denotes the submodule of \(U\) formed by the invariants under the inertia group \(I\), we can write (11.1.3) in the form
\[(11.1.6)\qquad {}_n\Phi_o(\ell)\simeq (U_n)^I/(U^I)_n, \qquad n=\ell^{r+1},\]

where the index \(n\) denotes reduction mod. \(n\), i.e. the tensor product over \(\mathbb Z_\ell\) with \(\mathbb Z_\ell/n\mathbb Z_\ell\). The transition morphisms between the groups appearing in the two members of (11.1.6) being obvious, we deduce from (11.1.6), by passage to the inductive limit over the powers of \(\ell\), the

Proposition 11.2.
Suppose \(S\) strictly local, and let \(\ell\) be a prime number \(\ne p\). Then we have a canonical isomorphism \[(11.2.1)\qquad \Phi_o(\ell)\simeq (U\otimes D_\ell)^I/U^I\otimes D_\ell,\] where \(U\) is defined in (11.1.5) and where we set \(D_\ell=\mathbb Q_\ell/\mathbb Z_\ell\).
Thus, the group \(\Phi_o(\ell)\) can be made explicit in terms of the sole knowledge of the Tate module \(U=T_\ell(A(\bar K))\) as a Galois module under the action of the inertia group \(I\).
11.3. We are going to use the explicit expression (11.2.1) to sketch the definition of a canonical pairing
\[(11.3.1)\qquad \Phi_o(\ell)\otimes\Phi'_o(\ell)\longrightarrow\mathbb Q/\mathbb Z,\]

which is a perfect duality, — \(\Phi'_o\) denoting as in § 1 the group of connected components associated to the Néron model of the dual \(A'_K\) of \(A_K\). There is hardly any doubt (but the writer has dispensed with carrying out the verification) that this pairing is none other, possibly up to sign, than the one defined in (1.2.1), and the reader is invited as an exercise to establish the desired compatibility (with the correct sign!).
Let us set
\[U'=T_\ell(A'(\bar K)),\qquad T=T_\ell(1)(\bar K)=T_\ell(\bar K^*).\]
We have a perfect pairing of free \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-modules of finite type, compatible with the actions of \(I\):
\[(11.3.2)\qquad \varphi:U\otimes U'\longrightarrow T, \qquad (T\simeq\mathbb Z_\ell,\text{ non-canonically}),\]
and, by virtue of the known structure of \(I\) (I ), an exact sequence
\[(11.3.3)\qquad 1\longrightarrow R\longrightarrow I\longrightarrow T\longrightarrow1,\]
with \(R\) a profinite group prime to \(\ell\). If now we start from data (11.3.2) and (11.3.3), and introduce the groups
\[(11.3.4)\qquad \Phi=(U\otimes D_\ell)^I/U^I\otimes D_\ell,\quad \Phi'=(U'\otimes D_\ell)^I/U'^I\otimes D_\ell, \quad(D_\ell=\mathbb Q_\ell/\mathbb Z_\ell)\]
we are going to define a perfect duality
\[(11.3.5)\qquad \Phi\otimes\Phi'\longrightarrow\mathbb Q/\mathbb Z,\]
which will give us in particular the announced pairing (11.3.1).
Consider the exact sequence of \(I\)-modules
\[(11.3.6)\qquad 0\longrightarrow U\mathrel{\mathop\longrightarrow^{i}}U\otimes_{\mathbb Z_\ell}\mathbb Q_\ell \mathrel{\mathop\longrightarrow^{j}}U\otimes D_\ell\longrightarrow0,\]
and the associated cohomology exact sequence, taking the cohomology of \(I\) defined by the continuous cochains with coefficients in the topological groups under consideration:

\[(11.3.7)\qquad 0\longrightarrow H^0(I,U)\mathrel{\mathop\longrightarrow^{i^o}}H^0(I,U\otimes\mathbb Q_\ell) \mathrel{\mathop\longrightarrow^{j^o}}H^0(I,U\otimes D_\ell)\mathrel{\mathop\longrightarrow^{\partial}}H^1(I,U) \mathrel{\mathop\longrightarrow^{i^1}}H^1(I,U\otimes\mathbb Q_\ell),\]
where the terms are identified respectively with
\[U^I,\qquad (U\otimes\mathbb Q_\ell)^I\simeq U^I\otimes_\mathbb Z\mathbb Q, \qquad (U\otimes D_\ell)^I,\qquad H^1(I,U)\otimes_\mathbb Z\mathbb Q.\]
It is immediate that \(\Phi\) defined by (11.3.4) is none other than \(\operatorname{Coker}j^o\), hence is canonically isomorphic to \(\operatorname{Ker}i^1\), i.e. we find a canonical exact sequence
\[(11.3.8)\qquad 0\longrightarrow\Phi\longrightarrow H^1(I,U)\longrightarrow H^1(I,U)\otimes_\mathbb Z\mathbb Q,\]
which shows that \(\Phi\) is canonically isomorphic to the torsion subgroup of \(H^1(I,U)\).
On the other hand, from the exact sequence (11.3.3) we deduce an isomorphism, for every \(n=\ell^r\):
\[(11.3.9)\qquad H^1(I,T_n)\simeq\operatorname{Hom}(I,T_n)\xleftarrow\sim \operatorname{Hom}(T,T_n)\xleftarrow\sim\mathbb Z/n\mathbb Z\]
(NB we let \(I\) act trivially on \(T\), hence on \(T_n=T/nT\)); whence, for every finite \(I\)-\(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-module \(M\), if
\[(11.3.10)\qquad M'=\operatorname{Hom}(M,T\otimes D_\ell)\]
denotes its "Cartier dual", a canonical pairing
\[(11.3.11)\qquad H^i(I,M)\otimes H^{1-i}(I,M')\longrightarrow H^1(I,T\otimes D_\ell)\simeq D_\ell,\]

where the last isomorphism is deduced from the isomorphisms (11.3.9) by passage to the inductive limit over the powers \(n\) of \(\ell\). It is well known that these pairings are perfect dualities (cf. for example SGA 5 I 5). Taking in these pairings \(M\) of the form \(U_n\), hence \(M'=U'_n\), we find by passage to the limit a perfect pairing
\[(11.3.12)\qquad H^0(I,U\otimes D_\ell)\times H^1(I,U')\longrightarrow D_\ell,\]

where the second factor of the first member is a \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-module of finite type. A further passage to the limit in the pairings (11.3.12) (for transition morphisms of the form \(\ell^r\operatorname{id}\)) gives us a second perfect pairing
\[(11.3.13)\qquad H^0(I,U\otimes\mathbb Q_\ell)\times H^1(I,U'\otimes\mathbb Q_\ell)\longrightarrow D_\ell\]

of finite-dimensional vector spaces over \(\mathbb Q_\ell\). Denoting by \(i'{}^1,j'{}^1\) the morphisms defined as in (11.3.6), with \(U\) replaced by \(U'\), one then easily finds that the following homomorphisms
\[H^0(I,U\otimes\mathbb Q_\ell)\mathrel{\mathop\longrightarrow^{j^0}} H^0(I,U\otimes D_\ell)\] \[H^1(I,U'\otimes\mathbb Q_\ell)\mathrel{\mathop\longleftarrow^{i'{}^1}} H^1(I,U')\]

are transposes of each other, via the pairings (11.3.12) and (11.3.13). It follows at once that \(\operatorname{coker}j^0\) and \(\operatorname{Ker}i'{}^1\) are dual to each other via a canonical pairing with values in \(D_\ell\), which is the announced pairing (11.3.5).

11.4. The preceding description of a pairing (11.3.1), via the expression (11.2.1) for the \(\ell\)-primary component of the group of connected components, is valid only for \(\ell\ne p\). Assuming now that \(A_K\) has semi-stable reduction over \(S\), we are again going to define pairings which are perfect dualities
\[(11.4.1)\qquad \Phi_o(\ell)\otimes\Phi'_o(\ell)\longrightarrow\mathbb Q/\mathbb Z\]

without restriction on \(\ell\). As in 11.4, it would be appropriate to establish that we recover (up to a sign perhaps, which it would be advisable to make precise) the pairing defined in (1.2.1), which would thus establish Conjecture 1.3 in the case of semi-stable reduction, as in the case where we restrict to the \(\ell\)-primary components for \(\ell\ne p\).

To define (11.4.1), we are going to give, essentially, an expression of \(\Phi_o(\ell)\) in terms of the Barsotti-Tate group \(T_\ell(A_K)\) over \(K\), which will play in the present case the same role as 11.2 in the case treated previously:

Theorem 11.5.
Suppose \(S\) henselian, and that \(A_K\) has semi-stable reduction over \(S\). Let \(\ell\) be a prime number, and consider the monodromy pairing (9.1.2), whence a canonical homomorphism \[\widetilde u_\ell:M_\ell\longrightarrow\check M'_\ell.\] Let \(\Phi(\ell)=\operatorname{coker}\widetilde u_\ell\); then we have a canonical isomorphism \[(11.5.1)\qquad \Phi_o(\ell)\xrightarrow\sim\Phi(\ell)\times_S s.\]

From this statement follows immediately the definition of a perfect pairing (11.4.1), since we find, applying 11.5 to \(A'_K\) instead of \(A_K\) and using (10.2.2), that we also have a canonical isomorphism
\[\Phi'_o(\ell)\xrightarrow\sim\Phi'(\ell)\times_Ss, \quad\text{where }\Phi'(\ell)=\operatorname{coker}({}^t\widetilde u_\ell:\check M'_\ell\longrightarrow\check M_\ell).\]

Remarks 11.5.2. a) Theorem 11.5 implies in particular that \(\widetilde u_\ell\) is an isogeny, i.e. that \(u_\ell\) is separating, including in the case \(\ell=p\), where this assertion is not trivial from the definition of 9.6. One will note that we do not use here the (distinctly deeper) theorem 10.4, which will be proved in the following section.
b) Using 10.4, which provides us with a monodromy pairing \(u:M\otimes M'\longrightarrow\mathbb Z_S\), and denoting by \(\Phi\) the cokernel of the corresponding homomorphism
\[\widetilde u:M\longrightarrow\check M',\]
we find an equivalent formulation of 11.5 independent of the choice of an \(\ell\), by the canonical isomorphism
\[(11.5.3)\qquad \Phi_o\xrightarrow\sim\Phi\times_Ss.\]
11.6. To define the isomorphism (11.5.1), we may restrict ourselves to the case where \(S\) is strictly local (11.0), so that \(M\) and \(M'\) are constant, with values

where \(T_o\) and \(T'_o\) are the maximal tori of \(A_o\) and of \(A'_o\) respectively. Similarly identifying \(\Phi_o\) with an ordinary finite group, we must define a canonical isomorphism between its component \(\Phi_o(\ell)\) and
\[\Phi(\ell)=\operatorname{coker}(\widetilde u_\ell:M_\ell\longrightarrow\check M'_\ell).\]
In fact, we are going to define directly a canonical isomorphism
\[(11.6.2)\qquad \Phi_o(\ell)\xrightarrow\sim \ker(\widetilde u_\ell\otimes D_\ell:M_\ell\otimes D_\ell\longrightarrow\check M'_\ell\otimes D_\ell)\ ;\]

as \(\Phi_o(\ell)\) is finite, and \(M_\ell\) and \(\check M'_\ell\) have the same rank, it will follow first that \(\widetilde u_\ell:M_\ell\longrightarrow\check M'_\ell\) is an isogeny, whence it then follows that the second member of (11.6.2) is canonically isomorphic to \(\Phi(\ell)\):
\[(11.6.3)\qquad \Phi(\ell)=\operatorname{coker}\widetilde u_\ell\xrightarrow\sim \ker(\widetilde u_\ell\otimes D_\ell),\]
since we can write, identifying \(M_\ell\) by \(\widetilde u_\ell\) with a submodule of finite index in \(\check M'_\ell\):
\[M_\ell\otimes D_\ell=V/M_\ell,\qquad \check M'_\ell\otimes D_\ell=V/\check M'_\ell,\]

where \(V=M_\ell\otimes\mathbb Q_\ell\xrightarrow\sim\check M'_\ell\otimes\mathbb Q_\ell\). From (11.6.2) and (11.6.3) the isomorphism to be defined (11.5.1) follows at once by composition, so that everything comes down to defining the canonical isomorphism (11.6.2).

Writing \(M_\ell\otimes D_\ell\) (resp. \(\check M'_\ell\otimes D_\ell\)) as the inductive limit of the \(M_n\) (resp. of the \(\check M'_n\)), where \(n\) runs over the powers of \(\ell\), it amounts to the same to define (11.6.2), or canonical isomorphisms
\[(11.6.4)\qquad {}_n\Phi_o\xrightarrow\sim \operatorname{Ker}(\widetilde u_n:M_n\longrightarrow\check M'_n),\qquad n=\ell^{r+1},\]

compatible with the obvious transition morphisms, for variable \(n\) (where \(\widetilde u_n\) now denotes, by abuse of notation, the homomorphism deduced from \(\widetilde u_\ell:M_\ell\longrightarrow\check M'_\ell\) by reduction mod. \(n\)).
For this, consider the group scheme \({}_nA\) over \(S\), which is flat and quasi-finite over \(S\) (2.2.1), thanks to the semi-stable reduction hypothesis. We have a canonical filtration
\[(11.6.5)\qquad {}_nA\longrightarrow({}_nA^o)^f\longrightarrow({}_nA^o)^t\longrightarrow0,\]

where the exponent \(f\) (resp. the exponent \(t\)) denotes the "fixed part" (2.2.3) (resp. the "toric part" (5.1)) of the group scheme it affects; for the definition of the toric part in 5.1, one will note that we had assumed \(S\) complete, which is permissible here (11.0). (We have moreover seen in 6.1 that the construction generalizes to the case \(S\) henselian, if one insists …) Let
this is a flat, separated and quasi-finite group scheme over \(S\) (SGA 3 VIA 3.2 and VIB 9.2) with generic fiber
is a finite open subgroup scheme of \({}_nA\); more precisely, we see immediately that we have
\[(11.6.10)\qquad {}_n\Phi\xrightarrow\sim({}_nY)^f.\]
Composing (11.6.9) and (11.6.8.1), we find an open immersion
\[(11.6.11)\qquad {}_n\Phi\lhook\joinrel\longrightarrow M_n.\]
On the other hand, we have \({}_n\Phi\times_Ss={}_nY\times_Ss={}_nA_o/{}_nA_o^o\), and as \(A_o^o\) is \(n\)-divisible (2.2.1), we find an isomorphism
\[(11.6.12)\qquad {}_n\Phi\times_Ss\xrightarrow\sim{}_n\Phi_o.\]
Lemma 11.6.13. The image of the canonical homomorphism (11.6.11) is equal to the kernel of \(\widetilde u_n:M_n\longrightarrow\check M'_n\).

To prove the lemma, let us note that \(K_n=\ker\widetilde u_n\) is also the left annihilator of the canonical pairing \(u_n:M_n\times M'_n\longrightarrow(\mathbb Z/n\mathbb Z)_S\), whose explicit definition is given in 9.4 and 9.6, in terms of the extensions
\[(11.6.14)\quad0\longrightarrow\check M'_n(1)\longrightarrow F_n\longrightarrow R_n\longrightarrow0,\quad0\longrightarrow\check M_n(1)\longrightarrow F'_n\longrightarrow R'_n\longrightarrow0\]
and of the extension panachée \(X_{n\eta}\) of \(E_{n\eta}\) by \(F_{n\eta}\), where
\[(11.6.15)\qquad E_n=D(F_n),\qquad0\longrightarrow R_n\longrightarrow E_n\longrightarrow M_n\longrightarrow0.\]
