It then follows immediately from 9.4.6 that \(K_n\) is the largest of the finite étale subgroups \(K'_n\) of \(M_n\) for which the induced extension panachée extends over \(S\). This condition is fulfilled for \({}_n\Phi\), since we have \(({}_nA)^f\) at our disposal, hence \({}_n\Phi\subset K_n\). Conversely, the extension corresponding to \(K_n\) provides \(\overline X_n\longrightarrow A\), which factors through \(({}_nA)^f\); thus \(K_n\subset{}_n\Phi\), qed.

Remarks 11.7. The preceding construction makes it possible to reconstruct, up to unique isomorphism, the extension panachée (11.6.5), and consequently \(({}_nA)^f\), from the extensions (11.6.14) over \(S\) and from \(X_{n\eta}=({}_nA_K)^f\). One then deduces \({}_nA\) by gluing the two extensions along their common open set.

11.8. Consider, as in 8.3, the separable closure \(\overline K\) of \(K\) as the inductive limit of its finite subextensions \(K'\). For every such \(K'\), let \(A[K']\) be the Néron model of \(A_{K'}\), and
The groups \(\Phi[K']\) form an inductive system; let us set
\[(11.8.2)\qquad\Psi=\underset{K'}{\lim}\,\Phi[K'].\]
For \(K'\) large enough, \(A[K']\) has semi-stable reduction, and (11.6.2) gives
\[(11.8.3)\qquad\Psi[K'](\ell)\xrightarrow\sim\ker(\widetilde u[K']_\ell\otimes D_\ell:M_\ell\otimes D_\ell\longrightarrow\check M'_\ell\otimes D_\ell).\]
By passage to the limit, we obtain
\[(11.8.4)\qquad\Psi(\ell)\lhook\joinrel\longrightarrow M_\ell\otimes D_\ell,\qquad(11.8.5)\qquad\Psi\longrightarrow M\otimes\mathbb Q/\mathbb Z.\]
Theorem 11.9. The canonical homomorphism (11.8.5) is an isomorphism.
We already know that it is a monomorphism; it remains to use the characterization (11.8.3) and the behavior of \(\widetilde u(K')\) given by (10.3.4).

Reducing to the case where \(S\) is strictly local, which is permissible, we are thus reduced, in order to prove 11.9, to the following well-known statement (which will be applied to \(S(K'_o)\)): if \(S\) is a strictly local trait with field of fractions \(K\), then for every integer \(n\geq1\), there exists a separable extension \(K'\) of \(K\) of degree a multiple of \(n\) (cf. I).

Corollary 11.10.

Under the conditions (\(S\) complete) and with the notations of 8.3, the ind-finite group scheme \(A^{b}_{\overline K}/A^{bo}_{\overline K}\) over \(\overline K\) of connected components of the group scheme \(A^b_{\overline K}\) is canonically isomorphic to \(M_{\overline K}\otimes\mathbb Q/\mathbb Z\), where \(M_{\overline K}\) is the twisted constant group over \(\overline K\) defined in (8.1.8).
This is an immediate consequence of (8.2.4) and of 11.9, taking into account the definition of \(M_{\overline K}\).

Remarks 11.11. a) When we restrict to the \(\ell\)-primary components for \(\ell\ne p\), Theorem 11.9 is immediate in terms of 11.2, and does not require recourse to the more delicate considerations of 11.6 (via 9.4). Similarly, when we assume \(K\) of characteristic zero, 11.9 can be proved without using (11.8.3), for it is immediate that the image of \({}_n\Phi(K')(\ell)\) in \(M_n={}_n(M_\ell\otimes D_\ell)\) contains the image of the group \({}_nA(K')\) of points of \({}_nA\) with values in \(K'\), which is equal to \({}_nA(\overline K)\) and hence surjects onto \(M_n\) for \(K'\) large enough. When \(\operatorname{car}.K=p\), it seems on the contrary that there is no "trivial" proof (i.e. not using 9.4) of 11.10 for the \(p\)-primary component.
b) The fact that there is an isomorphism \(\Psi\simeq(\mathbb Q/\mathbb Z)^r\), and the correct value of \(r\), had been conjectured in 1954 by J.P. SERRE.

pages.title: 12. Néron model of a Jacobian and Picard Lefschetz formula

blocks.html: 12. Néron model of a Jacobian and Picard Lefschetz formula
Let us first recall the following general results of M. RAYNAUD [22, th.5 and th.7, 22 bis]:

Theorem 12.1 (M. RAYNAUD).

Let \(S\) be a strictly local trait, \(f:X\longrightarrow S\) a proper and flat scheme over \(S\), with fibers of dimension 1, such that we have

\[(12.1.1)\qquad f_*(\mathcal O_X)\xrightarrow\sim\mathcal O_S\]

and that the local rings of \(X\) are factorial. For every maximal point \(x_i\) of \(X_o\), let

\[(12.1.2)\qquad d_i=\operatorname{long}\mathcal O_{X_o,x_i},\qquad d_i^t=\mu_i d_i,\]

where \(\mu_i\) is the radicial multiplicity of \(k(x_i)\) over \(k\) (EGA IV 4.7.4) (which is a power of the characteristic exponent of \(k\)). Let \(d\) (resp. \(d^t\)) be the gcd of the integers \(d_i\) (resp. \(d_i^t\)); thus we have \(d=d^t\) if \(k\) is perfect. Let us set

\[(12.1.3)\qquad P=\operatorname{Pic}_{X/S}: (\mathrm{Sch})/S^o\longrightarrow(\mathrm{Ab})^o.\]

a) Consider the following conditions:

  1. 1) \(d^t=1\).
  2. 2) \(X\) possesses a 0-cycle of degree 1.
  3. 3) There exists an invertible Module \(L\) on \(X\times P^o\) such that the corresponding homomorphism \(P^o\longrightarrow\operatorname{Pic}_{X/\eta}/n=P_\eta\) is the canonical inclusion. (NB it is known (J.P. MURRE) that \(P_\eta\) is representable by a group scheme locally of finite type over \(\eta\), cf. e.g. SGA 6 XII 1.5).
  4. 4) \(X\) is cohomologically flat over \(S\) (EGA III 7.8.1) (or again: cohomologically flat in dimension 0, or again: we have \(k\xrightarrow\sim H^0(X_o,\mathcal O_{X_o})\), and \(d=1\)).
  5. 5) \(P\) is representable (or again: representable by a smooth group scheme over \(S\)).
  6. 6) \(P^o\) is representable (or again: representable by a smooth group scheme over \(S\)). (NB For the definition of \(P^o\), cf. SGA 3 VIB 3.1.)
  7. 7) \(P^o\) is separated over \(S\), i.e. satisfies the valuative criterion of separation EGA II 7.2.3 (or again: every section \(s\) of \(P^o\) over \(S\) such that \(s(\eta)=0\) is zero).
  8. 8) \(d=1\).

We then have the implications

\[(12.1.4)\qquad 1)\Longleftrightarrow2)\Longrightarrow3)\Longrightarrow4) \Longleftrightarrow5)\Longleftrightarrow6)\Longleftrightarrow7)\Longrightarrow8).\]

In particular, if \(d=d^t\), for example if \(k\) is perfect, conditions 1) to 8) are equivalent.

b) Assume \(d^t=1\), so that \(P\) is representable by a smooth group scheme over \(S\). Then the schematic closure of the unit section of \(P\) is a subgroup scheme \(E\) étale over \(S\), and if \(I\) is the set of reduced irreducible components \(X_o^i\) of \(X_o\), we obtain an isomorphism

\[(12.1.5)\qquad \mathbb Z^I\xrightarrow\sim\Gamma(S,E)\]

by associating to every \(i\in I\) the section \(s_i\) of \(P\) over \(S\) (zero at \(\eta\)) defined by the invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_X(X_o^i)\) on \(S\). (NB As the local rings of \(X\) are factorial by hypothesis, the cycles \(X_o^i\) are indeed divisors, which gives a meaning to \(\mathcal O_X(X_o^i)\) as the invertible sheaf associated to the divisor in question (EGA IV 21.2.8.1).) We have

\[(12.1.6)\qquad P^o\cap E=\text{zero section of }P.\]

c) Under the conditions of b), the quotient

\[(12.1.7)\qquad Q=P/E\]

is representable by a smooth and separated group scheme over \(S\), and the latter is Néronian, i.e. satisfies the universal property (1.1.2) in terms of its special fiber.

d) Under the conditions of c), assume moreover that the generic geometric fiber of \(X\) is integral. Then we have a canonical exact sequence

\[(12.1.8)\qquad 0\longrightarrow A\longrightarrow Q\mathrel{\mathop{\longrightarrow}^{\deg}} \mathbb Z_S\longrightarrow0,\]

where \(\deg\) is induced by the "degree" homomorphism \(P\longrightarrow\mathbb Z_S\), and where \(A\) is a smooth separated group scheme of finite type over \(S\), which is likewise Néronian, i.e. which is a Néron model of

\[(12.1.9)\qquad A_\eta=P_\eta^o=\operatorname{Pic}_{X_\eta/\eta}^o.\]

On the other hand, the composite homomorphism \(P^o\longrightarrow P\longrightarrow Q\) induces an isomorphism

\[(12.1.10)\qquad P^o\xrightarrow\sim A^o.\]

12.1.11. The case that will interest us is that where \(X_\eta\) is smooth over \(\eta\), so that (taking 12.1.1 into account) the generic geometric fiber of \(X\) is smooth and connected (hence integral), and where \(X_\eta\) possesses a 0-cycle of degree 1. Then all the conditions considered in 12.1 (and in particular conditions 1) to 8) of a)) are satisfied; on the other hand \(A_\eta=P_\eta^o\) is then an abelian scheme, and d) gives us a very explicit construction of its Néron model \(A\). In particular (12.1.10) provides us with an isomorphism
\[(12.1.12)\qquad A_o^o\xrightarrow\sim\operatorname{Pic}_{X_o/k}^o,\]

which shows in particular that \(A\) has semi-stable reduction over \(S\) if and only if \(\operatorname{Pic}_{X_o/k}^o\) has unipotent rank zero. When \(X\) is regular and \(X_o\) is separable over \(k\), it is known that this is the case if and only if the geometric special fiber \(\overline X_o\) has as its only singular points ordinary quadratic points (i.e. having the same completed local ring as the union of the two coordinate axes of the affine space \(\mathbb E^2\) at the origin). The verification of this fact, going along with the "well-known" description of the Picard scheme of a proper separable curve over a field \(k\), using EGA IV 21, § 5, is left to the reader as an exercise. One will note moreover that the fact that "\(\overline X_o\) has ordinary quadratic singularities" implies "semi-stable reduction of \(A_\eta=\operatorname{Pic}_{X_\eta/\eta}^o\)" is also an immediate consequence of the "geometric monodromy theorem" III, taking into account the Galois criterion of semi-stable reduction 3.5; but this argument does not give the important isomorphism (12.1.10) and its consequence (12.1.12).

12.2. For the proof of 12.1, we refer to [22 bis]; we shall moreover need only the case \(X_eta\) smooth over \(eta\). Let us note moreover that in [22bis] relations between Picard schemes and Néron models are proved under conditions distinctly more general than those of 12.1 c) and d); as their statement is more technical, we have refrained from including them in our recollection. Let us only point out that b), c), d) are easy consequences of a), and that in a) the only delicate points are the implications \(3\Longrightarrow4\Longrightarrow5\) and \(7\Longrightarrow4\), the most difficult being the first. This point is trivial on the other hand if we assume that \(X_o\) is separable over \(k\) (which implies that \(X\) admits a section over \(S\) since \(S\) is strictly local, hence that condition 1), \(d^t=1\), is satisfied). We advise the reader to carry out the proof in this particularly simple case, assuming moreover, if need be, that \(X_eta\) is smooth, hence \(P_eta^o\) an abelian scheme, which then makes it possible to dispose of the Néron model \(A\) of the latter, and of \(A^o\) to represent \(\operatorname{Pic}_{X/S}^o\). We shall moreover use 12.1 only in this particular case, and when we assume in addition that \(\overline X_o\) has no singular points other than ordinary quadratic points, i.e. (12.1.11) that \(A_eta\) has semi-stable reduction: this is the case considered in 12.5.4, to which we restrict ourselves from now on.

12.3. Let \(C\) be a separable and proper curve over a field \(k\), which we assume for simplicity separably closed (or even algebraically closed, if the reader prefers). If \(\widetilde C\) is the normalization of \(C\), it is known that \(\operatorname{Pic}_{\widetilde C/k}^o\) has "reductive rank" zero (i.e. its maximal torus is zero), so that the maximal torus of \(\operatorname{Pic}_{C/k}^o\) is contained in
\[ N=\ker(\operatorname{Pic}_{C/k}\longrightarrow\operatorname{Pic}_{\widetilde C/k}), \]
hence is canonically isomorphic to the maximal torus of the latter group.

Now the structure of \(N\) can easily be made explicit with the help of EGA IV 21.8.5: one finds that \(N\) is canonically an extension of a torus \(T\), whose description we shall make explicit, by a connected unipotent group \(U\) (which likewise admits a dévissage, over \(k\), by a composition series with quotients isomorphic to the additive group \(\mathbb G_a\)). Moreover, if \(T'\) is the maximal torus of \(N\), then \(T'\cap U\) is both unipotent and of multiplicative type, hence zero (SGA 3 XVII 2.4), and the image of \(T'\) in \(T\) is identical to \(T\) (SGA 3 XII 7.1 e)), so that \(T'\longrightarrow T\) is an isomorphism. Hence \(T\) is canonically isomorphic to the maximal torus of \(N\), hence of \(\operatorname{Pic}_{C/k}^o\).

It is in order to make explicit the structure of \(N\) that it will be convenient to assume \(k\) separably closed, so that the irreducible components \(C_i\) of \(C\) are geometrically irreducible, and the non-normal points of \(C\) are radicial over \(k\). This said, let \(J\) be the set of irreducible components of \(\widetilde C\), and for every \(x\in C\), let (J\(x\)) be the set of "branches" of \(C\) passing through \(x\) (i.e. of the points of \(\widetilde C\) above \(x\)); finally let
\[(12.3.1)\qquad R(x)=\mathbb Z^{J(x)}/\operatorname{Im}\mathbb Z,\]
where \(\mathbb Z\longrightarrow\mathbb Z^{J(x)}\) is the diagonal map, and
\[(12.3.2)\qquad R=(\mathop{\bigoplus}\limits_x R_x)/\operatorname{Im}\mathbb Z^J,\]

where it obviously suffices to extend the sum to the finite set of points of \(C\) which belong to at least two branches of \(C\). Hence \(R\) is a \(\mathbb Z\)-module of finite type. We then have a canonical isomorphism
\[(12.2.3)\qquad T\;(\mathrel{\mathop{\simeq}}\text{ maximal torus of } \operatorname{Pic}_{C/k}^o)\xrightarrow\sim R\mathbin{\mathop\otimes}\mathbb G_{m,k}.\]
In other words, the character group \(M\) of \(T\) is given by
\[(12.2.4)\qquad M\mathrel{\mathop=\limits^{\mathrm{dfn}}}D(T)=\check R \xrightarrow\sim\ker(\mathop{\bigoplus}\limits_x\check R(x)\longrightarrow\mathbb Z^J),\]
where (check R\(x\)) can also be interpreted as the set of integer-valued functions on (J\(x\)) whose sum of values is zero.

12.3.5. We shall have to use only the case where through a point \(x\) of \(C\) there pass at most two branches (which is the case in particular for ordinary quadratic points!). For a point such as \(\operatorname{card}J(x)=2\), (R\(x\)) is thus isomorphic to \(\mathbb Z\), but the isomorphism depends on the choice of an order on the set of the two branches \((C',C'')\) passing through \(x\), or what amounts to the same, the choice of one of its branches \(C'\) (by associating to it the element of (R\(x\)) which is the canonical image of \(0_{C'}+1_{C''}\)). We thus then find an isomorphism
\[(12.3.6)\qquad R=\mathbb Z^I/\operatorname{Im}\mathbb Z^J,\quad T\simeq\mathbb G_m^I/\operatorname{Im}\mathbb G_m^J,\quad M=\ker(\mathbb Z^I\longrightarrow\mathbb Z^J),\]
where \(I\) denotes the set of the \(x\in C\) belonging to two branches of \(C\), and subject to a choice of signs as just made explicit.

Remarks 12.3.7. A sometimes convenient way of making explicit the character group \(M\) of the maximal torus \(T\) of \(\operatorname{Pic}_{C/k}^o\) is the following: we consider the "(non-oriented) graph of dimension 1" (N. BOURBAKI, Groupes et Algèbres de Lie, Chap IV, Annexe) \(\Gamma(C)\) whose vertices are the irreducible components of \(\widetilde C\), two vertices \(\widetilde C_i\) and \(\widetilde C_j\) being joined by a set of edges in bijective correspondence with the set \(\widetilde C_i\mathbin{\mathop\times_C}\widetilde C_j\) of sets of two distinct branches of \(C\) contained in \(\widetilde C_i\) resp. \(\widetilde C_j\) passing through the same point of \(C\), a set equal to \(\widetilde C_i\mathbin{\mathop\times_C}\widetilde C_j\) if \(i\ne j\), to \(\widetilde C_i\mathbin{\mathop\times_C}\widetilde C_i-\Delta(\widetilde C_i\)) modulo symmetry (\(\Delta:\widetilde C\longrightarrow\widetilde C\times_C\widetilde C\) being the diagonal) if \(i=j\) (hence empty if \(C_i\) is geometrically unibranch). This granted, we have canonical isomorphisms
\[(12.3.7)\qquad M=D(T)\xleftarrow\sim H_1(\Gamma(C),\mathbb Z),\quad \check M=R\xrightarrow\sim H^1(\Gamma(C),\mathbb Z).\]
We shall not need this combinatorial interpretation of \(M\), and shall restrict ourselves to defining the canonical homomorphism
\[H_1(\Gamma(C),\mathbb Z)\longrightarrow M,\]

leaving to the reader the task of proving that it is an isomorphism. So let \(I\) be the set of points of \(C\) belonging to at least two branches of \(C\), \(J\) (as above) the set of irreducible components of \(\widetilde C\) (or of \(C\), which amounts to the same), \(K\) the set of "branches" of \(C\) at the points of \(I\), i.e. the inverse image of \(I\) in \(\widetilde C\). We thus have a canonical map
\[(12.3.9)\qquad h:K\longrightarrow I\times J,\qquad h=(f,g),\]
and the description (12.3.4) of \(M\) is expressed in terms of \(h\) as
\[(12.3.10)\qquad M\xrightarrow\sim\ker\bigl(\mathbb Z^{(h)}: \mathbb Z^{(K)}\longrightarrow\mathbb Z^{(I\times J)}\bigr),\]

for the expression (12.3.3) of \(R=\check M\) can manifestly be interpreted as the cokernel of the homomorphism \(\mathbb Z^{I\times J}\longrightarrow\mathbb Z^K\) induced by \(h\), which is the transpose of \(\mathbb Z^{(h)}\). Let us note moreover that the graph \(\Gamma(C)\) can also be made explicit in an obvious way in terms of \(h\), by \(\Gamma_o=J\), \(\Gamma_1=(K\times_I K-\Delta(K))/\text{symmetry}\).
On the other hand, choosing an orientation on each of the edges of \(\Gamma(C)\), and denoting by \(\Gamma_1\) the set of these edges \((x_i,C',C'')\), we have
\[(12.3.11)\qquad H_1(\Gamma(C),\mathbb Z)= \ker\bigl(d:\mathbb Z^{(\Gamma_1)}\longrightarrow\mathbb Z^{(J)}\bigr),\]
where the differential operator is given by
This granted, the homomorphism (12.3.8) is induced by the homomorphism \(v\) of chain complexes:
\[(12.3.13)\qquad v=(v_o,v_1):C_\bullet(\Gamma(C))= [\mathbb Z^{(\Gamma)}\longrightarrow\mathbb Z^{(J)}] \longrightarrow[\mathbb Z^{(K)}\longrightarrow\mathbb Z^{(I\times J)}]\]
given by
\[(12.3.14)\qquad v_1(x,C',C'')=(x,C'')-(x,C'),\qquad v_o(\widetilde C_j)=(0,\widetilde C_j).\]

12.4. We now place ourselves in the case considered at the end of 12.2. By virtue of (12.1.12) and of (12.3.6) applied to \(C=X_o\), the character group \(M\) of the maximal torus \(T\) of the special fiber \(A_o\) of the Néron model \(A\) of \(A=\operatorname{Pic}_{X_\eta/\eta}^o\) is given by
\[(12.4.1)\qquad M=\ker(\mathbb Z^I\mathrel{\mathop{\longrightarrow}\limits^d} \mathbb Z^J),\]
where \(I\) is the set of singular points of \(X_o\), and \(J\) the set of its irreducible components, the homomorphism \(\mathbb Z^I\longrightarrow\mathbb Z^J\) being given by

\(C_i'\) and \(C_i''\) being the two irreducible components of \(\widetilde C\) which correspond to the branches passing through the double point \(x_i\). The operator \(d\) depends on the choice of the order \((C_i',C_i'')\) that one fixes between these two branches, and the isomorphism (12.4.1) obviously depends on this system of choices.
However, if for \(i\in I\) we denote by
\[(12.4.3)\qquad \varphi_{x_i}:M\longrightarrow\mathbb Z\]

the composite \(M\longrightarrow\mathbb Z^I\mathrel{\mathop{\longrightarrow}\limits^{\mathrm{pr}_i}} \mathbb Z\), where the first homomorphism comes from (12.4.1), one observes at once (going back to the expression (12.3.4)) that \(\varphi_{x_i}\) is well determined up to sign; more precisely, it depends only on the order taken between the two branches \(C_i',C_i''\) passing through \(i\), and changes sign when this order is changed. It follows in particular that the bilinear form
\[(12.4.4)\qquad \varphi_{x_i}\otimes\varphi_{x_i}\in \check M\otimes_{\mathbb Z}\check M\simeq\operatorname{Hom}(M\otimes M,\mathbb Z)\]
is determined canonically in terms of the singular point \(x_i\), without ambiguity of sign. We may therefore set
\[(12.4.5)\qquad u=\sum_i\varphi_{x_i}\otimes\varphi_{x_i}: M\otimes_{\mathbb Z}M\longrightarrow\mathbb Z,\]

and we thus find a well-determined pairing, i.e. independent of any choice of signs. It is clear that it is positive definite, for it is the restriction to \(M\subset\mathbb Z^I\) of the standard quadratic form \(\sum_i X_i^2\) on \(\mathbb Z^I\).

The notation \(M\) for the character group of \(T_o\) is not a priori in agreement with that of the preceding sections, where we wrote \(M'\), \(M\) being the analogous group relative to the abelian scheme \(A_K'\) dual to \(A_K\). But as \(A_K\) is a Jacobian, it is equipped with a canonical polarization \(\xi\) [5], inducing a canonical isomorphism
\[(12.4.6)\qquad \psi_\xi:A_K\xrightarrow\sim A_K',\]

by means of which we shall identify \(A_K\) and \(A_K'\), so that there is no longer any need to distinguish \(M\) and \(M'\). In particular, for every prime number \(\ell\), the monodromy pairing (9.1.2) can be considered as a pairing
\[(12.4.7)\qquad u_\ell:M_\ell\otimes_{\mathbb Z_\ell}M_\ell \longrightarrow\mathbb Z_\ell.\]
This granted, we can state the main result of the present section:

blocks.title: Theorem 12.5 (\(\text{« Picard--Lefschetz formula »}\)).
blocks.html:
Theorem 12.5 (\(\text{« Picard--Lefschetz formula »}\)).

Let \(S\) be a strictly local trait, \(X\) a regular scheme, projective and flat over \(S\), with fibers of dimension 1, whose generic geometric fiber is smooth and connected, and whose special geometric fiber admits as its only singular points ordinary quadratic points. With the notations introduced in 12.4, for every prime number \(\ell\), the monodromy pairing (12.4.7) is equal to the pairing deduced from (12.4.5) by extension of scalars from \(\mathbb Z\) to \(\mathbb Z_\ell\).

12.6. One will note that this statement proves 10.4 in the particular case considered, when in 10.4 b) we take for \(\xi\) the canonical polarization considered in 10.4. As we showed in 10.5, this suffices to imply 10.4 in the general case.

12.7. Proof of 12.5. Case \(\ell\ne p\). It relies in an essential way on the cohomological Picard-Lefschetz formula in the situation considered in 12.5, a formula giving the action of the monodromy group \(I\) on
\[(12.7.1)\qquad H^1(X_{\overline\eta},\mathbb Z_\ell)\simeq H^1(A_{\overline\eta},\mathbb Z_\ell)\simeq \operatorname{Hom}(T_\ell(A_{\overline\eta}),\mathbb Z_\ell)\]

in the case \(\ell\ne p\). This formula, which will be proved (in a distinctly more general case, moreover) in a later exposé (Exp XV), is indeed essentially identical to the assertion 12.5 for the \(\ell\) considered, taking into account the definition 9.2 of \(u_\ell\), for \(\ell\ne p\). It then remains to treat the case \(\ell=p\). We shall do so by a method of reduction to the case of characteristic zero (and where moreover \(X_o\) has only one singular point), which we are now going to set out.

Remarks 12.7.2. One will note moreover that, once the announced reduction is made, the usual arguments would allow us to reduce to the case where the base field is the field \(\mathbb C\) of complex numbers, and then to invoke the Picard-Lefschetz formula in its classical form, — except that there does not seem to be a satisfactory reference for this formula. The proof of the Picard-Lefschetz formula given in our Seminar will proceed in an analogous way, by reduction to the transcendental case, amenable to properly transcendental methods. One can therefore say that in any event, the proof of 12.5 (hence of 10.4) that we propose here is of a transcendental nature. Let us point out moreover that the only other known proof of 10.4 comes from rigid-analytic theory [24], and as such must be regarded likewise as being essentially "transcendental". It would be interesting to find purely algebraic proofs of these results (but it is by no means obvious that this is possible!).

12.8. Proof of 12.5: reduction to the case where \(S\) is of characteristic zero. We are going to use an argument relying on the same principle as the proof of the "theorem of essentially tame action of the monodromy on the \(\Pi_1\)" in V. Let us note that we may assume \(S\) complete thanks to 10.3. Let \(W\) be a Cohen \(p\)-ring with residue field \(k\), and let us choose a homomorphism \(W\longrightarrow V\) inducing the identity on the residue fields (EGA \(0_{\mathrm{IV}}\) 19.8.6). Let \(L=(x_\lambda)_{\lambda\in L}\) be the set of singular points of \(X_o\), and consider the formal moduli scheme \(\underline S\) of \(X_o\) over \(W\), and the "universal curve" \(\underline f:\underline X\longrightarrow \underline S\) with special fiber \(X_o\) (VI). We saw in loc. cit. that \(\underline S\) is isomorphic to the spectrum of a ring of formal power series \(W[[T_1,\ldots,T_N]]\), and that one can find in \(\underline S\) a divisor with normal crossings
\[(12.8.1)\qquad D=\sum_{\lambda\in L}D_\lambda,\]

whose irreducible components \(D_\lambda\) are indexed by the preceding set \(L\), each \(D_\lambda\) being described by an equation of the form \(T_{i_\lambda}=0\) (where we may assume, if we wish, \(i_\lambda=\lambda\), if we index \(L\) by a set of integers \([1,r]\)), \(D_\lambda\) being the image under \(\underline f:\underline X\longrightarrow\underline S\) of the component \(D_\lambda'\) passing through the singular point \(x_\lambda\) of \(X_o\) of the non-smoothness locus \(D'=\bigcup_{\lambda\in L}D_\lambda'\) of the morphism \(\underline f\). By the very construction, \(f:X\longrightarrow S\) is isomorphic to the inverse image of \(\underline f:\underline X\longrightarrow\underline S\) by a suitable \(W\)-morphism \(g:S\longrightarrow\underline S\):
\[(12.8.2)\qquad \begin{CD} X @>>> \underline X\\ @V f VV @VV \underline f V\\ S @>{g}>> \underline S. \end{CD}\]
Let us set
\[(12.8.3)\qquad P=\operatorname{Pic}_{\underline X/\underline S}^o;\]

as the special fiber of \(\underline f\) is geometrically reduced of dimension 1, it follows that \(\underline f\) is cohomologically flat in dimension 0 (EGA III 7.8.1) and that \(P\) is formally smooth over \(\underline S\) (this last fact resulting from the fact that \(H^2(X_o,\mathcal O_{X_o})=0\), \(X_o\) being of dimension 1). To simplify, we shall admit that \(P\) is representable by a group scheme over \(\underline S\) (which will necessarily be a smooth group scheme with connected fibers); this is a particular case of an unpublished result of MUMFORD cited in [22] (considerably generalized by RAYNAUD in the cited note, in the case where the base is a trait). As no proof of the results of MUMFORD or of RAYNAUD has been published, the reader will perhaps prefer to use the general results of M. ARTIN [1], which give the representability of \(P\) (under the sole hypothesis of starting from an \(f\) proper, flat and cohomologically flat in dimension 0) by an "algebraic space" in the sense of ARTIN, which would suffice for our purpose. Setting
\[(12.8.4)\qquad U=\underline S-\operatorname{supp}D,\]
so that \(U\) is the largest open set of \(\underline S\) above which \(\underline X\) is smooth, we thus see that
\[P|U=\operatorname{Pic}^o_{\underline X_U/U}\]

is an abelian scheme over \(U\), whose inverse image on \(\eta\) under \(g:\eta\longrightarrow U\) is the abelian scheme \(A_\eta\) over \(\eta\) that we propose to study. More precisely, we have a canonical isomorphism over \(S\) (12.1.10):
\[(12.8.5)\qquad P\mathbin{\mathop{\times}_{\underline S}}S\simeq A^o.\]

As the special fiber \(P_o=A_o^o\) is \(\ell\)-divisible for every \(\ell\), the same is true of \(P\) itself, all of whose fibers are therefore extensions of an abelian variety by a torus. Consider the pro-\(\ell\)-group \(T_\ell(P)\) over \(\underline S\), whose inverse image under \(g\) is none other than \(T_\ell(A^o)\). Applying the constructions 7.3, we define a filtration of this pro-group by a "fixed part" and a "toric part"
\[(12.8.6)\qquad T_\ell(P)\supset T_\ell(P)^f=T_\ell(\widehat P) \supset T_\ell(P)^t=T_\ell(T)\supset0,\]
whose inverse image under \(g\) is the analogous filtration of \(T_\ell(A^o)\), studied in §§ 2 and 5.

Consider the restriction of this filtration above the open set \(U\) of \(\underline S\), on which we have at our disposal the canonical autoduality of \(P_U\), as the Jacobian of a smooth curve scheme over \(U\), whence a corresponding pairing
\[(12.8.7)\qquad \varphi:T_\ell(P_U)\otimes T_\ell(P_U) \longrightarrow T_\ell(G_{mU}),\]

which is an autoduality (in the sense of Barsotti-Tate groups) of \(T_\ell(P_U)\). We saw in 7.4.8 that for this pairing, \(T_\ell(P_U)^f\) and \(T_\ell(P_U)^t\) are the orthogonal complements of each other. From this it follows that
\[E_U=T_\ell(P_U)/T_\ell(P_U)^t\]
is canonically isomorphic to the Barsotti-Tate pro-group dual to \(T_\ell(P_U)^f=F_U\), hence extends canonically to a Barsotti-Tate group \(E\) over \(\underline S\), namely
\[(12.8.8)\qquad E=D(F),\quad\text{where }F=T_\ell(P)^f.\]

We are then in a typical situation of extensions panachées (9.3), and we can take up again the constructions of 9.4 to 9.6 (which we apologize for having written up in the worn-out and dreary context of a base scheme reduced to a trait!): \(T_\ell(P_U)\) is an extension panachée of \(E_U\) by \(F_U\), where \(E\) and \(F\) are Barsotti-Tate groups over \(\underline S\) which are extensions, dual to each other:

\[(12.8.9)\qquad \begin{CD} 0 @>>> P @>>> F @>>> R @>>> 0\\ @. @| @. @|\\ @. T_\ell(P) @>{\sim}>> \operatorname{Hom}(M,T_\ell(G_{m\underline S})) @. T_\ell(P)^f \end{CD}\] \[\begin{CD} 0 @>>> R @>>> E @>>> Q @>>> 0\\ @. @| @. @|\\ @. D(R) @. @. M\otimes\mathbb Q_\ell/\mathbb Z_\ell{}_{\underline S}. \end{CD}\]

(NB \(R\) is canonically self-dual, as follows from the autoduality (12.8.7) and from the orthogonality statement pointed out above.) The only difference with loc. cit. is that the pairing one finds from \(M_\ell\times M_\ell\) takes values in a group
\[(G_m(U)/G_m(\underline S))\otimes_{\mathbb Z}\mathbb Z_\ell\]
which is no longer canonically isomorphic to \(\mathbb Z_\ell\), but to \(\mathbb Z_\ell^L\), thanks to the isomorphism
\[(12.8.10)\qquad \mathbb Z^L\xrightarrow\sim G_m(U)/G_m(\underline S)\]

which maps the element of index \(\lambda\) of the canonical basis of \(\mathbb Z^L\) to the class of \(T_{i_\lambda}\), in the second member, where \(T_{i_\lambda}\) is an equation of the divisor \(D_\lambda\). We thus find a canonical pairing
\[(12.8.11)\qquad \underline u_\ell:M_\ell\otimes M_\ell \longrightarrow\mathbb Z_\ell^L.\]

One verifies in an essentially trivial way that the monodromy pairing \(u_\ell\) (12.4.7) on \(S\) that we propose to study is the composite of \(\underline u_\ell\) with the homomorphism \(\mathbb Z_\ell^L\longrightarrow \mathbb Z_\ell\) coming, by tensoring with \(\mathbb Z_\ell\) over \(\mathbb Z\), from the homomorphism
\[\begin{CD} G_m(U)/G_m(\underline S) @>>> G_m(\eta)/G_m(S)\\ @V\wr VV @V\wr VV\\ \mathbb Z^L @>>> \mathbb Z \end{CD}\]
induced by \(g:S\longrightarrow\underline S\) (sending \(\eta\) into \(U\)). This homomorphism is given by an element
\[(12.8.12)\qquad (d_\lambda)_{\lambda\in L}\in\mathbb Z^L, \qquad(d_\lambda\geq1\text{ for all }\lambda\in L)\]

where for every \(\lambda\in L\), \(d_\lambda\) is the multiplicity of the inverse image in \(S\) of the divisor \(D_\lambda\) on \(\underline S\), which moreover depends only on the completion \(\widehat{\mathcal O}_{X,x_\lambda}\) as a \(V\)-algebra (VI). It is known moreover (VI) that for a given \(\lambda\), we have \(d_\lambda=1\) if and only if \(X\) is regular at the point \(x_\lambda\). As in 12.5 we assumed \(X\) regular, i.e. the \(d_\lambda\) equal to 1, we thus see that 12.5 will be a consequence of the following lemma, in which \(X,S,g\) no longer appear:

blocks.title: Lemma 12.8.13.
blocks.html:
Lemma 12.8.13.

If \(\lambda\in L\), the composite of (12.8.11) with \(\operatorname{pr}_\lambda\) is equal to the form \(\varphi_{x_\lambda}\otimes\varphi_{x_\lambda}\) (12.4.4).

To prove this last statement, consider the completed strict localization \(\underline S^\lambda\) of \(\underline S\) at the maximal point of \(D_\lambda\), and \(\underline X^\lambda=\underline X\mathbin{\mathop{\times}_{\underline S}} \underline S^\lambda\). Then the special fiber \(X_o^\lambda\) has only one singular point \(y_\lambda\), which is again ordinary quadratic. In the corresponding monodromy pairing
\[(12.8.14)\qquad u_\ell^\lambda:M_\ell^\lambda\otimes M_\ell^\lambda \longrightarrow\mathbb Z_\ell,\]

\(M^\lambda\) is thus of rank 1 or zero. We leave to the reader the task of verifying the following easy facts, which make explicit, in the particular case where we need it, the behavior of the preceding constructions under localization:

a) There exists a unique subtorus \(T_\lambda\) of \(P\times_{\underline S}D_\lambda\), whose restriction to the maximal point \(s_\lambda\) of \(D_\lambda\) is the maximal torus of \(P_{s_\lambda}\) (which is of dimension 0 or 1).

b) Consider the transposed homomorphism \(M\longrightarrow M^\lambda\), on the character groups, of the inclusion \(T_{\lambda,o}\lhook\joinrel\longrightarrow T_o=\) maximal torus of \(P_o\), and the form \(\varphi_{y_\lambda}:M^\lambda\longrightarrow\mathbb Z\), defined up to sign, defined as in 12.4.3 (with \(X\) replaced by \(\underline X^\lambda\)). We then have commutativity, up to sign, in
\[(12.8.15)\qquad \begin{CD} M @>>> M^\lambda\\ @V\varphi_{x_\lambda}VV @VV\varphi_{y_\lambda}V\\ \mathbb Z @= \mathbb Z. \end{CD}\]

(In fact, one observes that the choice of an order on the set of the two branches of \(X_o\) passing through \(x_\lambda\) makes it possible to make an analogous choice at the point \(y_\lambda\) of \(X_o^\lambda\), and for two choices which correspond to each other in this way, the preceding diagram is commutative.)
c) We have commutativity in the following diagram
\[\begin{CD} M_\ell\otimes M_\ell @>>> M_\ell^\lambda\otimes M_\ell^\lambda\\ @V\operatorname{pr}_\lambda\underline u_\ell VV @VV u_\ell^\lambda V\\ \mathbb Z_\ell @= \mathbb Z_\ell, \end{CD}\]
where the notations are those of 12.8.13 and (12.8.14).
From these relations it follows at once that to prove 12.8.13, it suffices to prove the analogous relation for \(\underline X^\lambda\):
\[u_\ell^\lambda=(\varphi_{y_\lambda}\otimes\varphi_{y_\lambda}) \otimes\mathbb Z_\ell,\]

which is none other than 12.5 in the case of \(\underline X^\lambda\) over \(\underline S^\lambda\). Now \(\underline S\) is a trait of characteristic zero. This therefore completes the announced reduction.

Remarks 12.9. One will note that this reduction reduces us at the same time to the case where \(X_o\) has only one singular point, which is the one classically considered in the Picard-Lefschetz formula. Let us note moreover that in this case, \(X_o\) has one or two irreducible components, these cases corresponding respectively to \(\operatorname{rang}M=1\) and \(\operatorname{rang}M=0\) (the latter case being the one where the Jacobian of the generic fiber has good reduction).

12.10. The proof given in 12.8 provides us with a result more general than 12.5, valid without assuming that \(X\) is regular, by introducing the integers \(d_\lambda\geq1\) of (12.8.12): one then finds that the monodromy pairings are all deduced from the pairing
\[(12.10.1)\qquad u=\sum_{\lambda\in L}d_\lambda\, \varphi_{x_\lambda}\otimes\varphi_{x_\lambda}: M\otimes M\longrightarrow\mathbb Z.\]

pages.title: 13. Links with the transcendental theory: the complex analytic case.

blocks.html: 13. Links with the transcendental theory: the complex analytic case.
For the general notions relating to analytic spaces, we refer to [2].
13.1. Recollections on analytic groups over S.

Let \(S\) be an analytic space, \(A\) an analytic space over \(S\), equipped with a structure of commutative group over \(S\) (i.e. \(A\) is a commutative group of the category of analytic spaces over \(S\)). We assume \(A\) smooth over \(S\), and we denote by
\[(13.1.1)\qquad \underline E=\operatorname{Lie}(A/S)\]

the locally free module on \(S\), the inverse image under the zero section of the relative tangent sheaf of \(A\) over \(S\). We shall here take for granted the definition of the exponential homomorphism
\[(13.1.2)\qquad \exp:E\longrightarrow A,\]

where \(E\) is the analytic vector bundle on \(S\) whose Module of holomorphic sections is \(\underline E\). This homomorphism is defined, in the neighborhood of a point \(s\in S\), first in the neighborhood of the zero section of \(E\) by the methods well known in the theory of formal groups in char. zero, and then extends in an obvious way by multiplicativity to all of \(E\). It is a homomorphism of relative analytic groups over \(S\), which is étale and has as image the open subspace \(A^o\) of \(A\) the union of the identity components of the fibers of \(A\) over \(S\). If \(R\) is the analytic subspace kernel of \(\exp\), we thus have an exact sequence of relative analytic groups:
\[(13.1.3)\qquad 0\longrightarrow R\longrightarrow E\longrightarrow A^o \longrightarrow0,\]

where the morphism \(E\longrightarrow A^o\) is surjective and étale, hence \(R\) is a relative analytic group étale over \(S\). It is an analytic subspace of \(E\), which is closed in \(E\) if and only if \(A^o\) is separated over \(S\), i.e. the zero section of \(A^o\) is closed in \(A^o\).

13.1.4. Of course, the exact sequence (13.1.3) is functorial in \(A\), and commutes with every base change \(S'\longrightarrow S\). In this way one finds an equivalence of categories, compatible with base changes, between the category of relative analytic groups over \(S\) smooth and with connected fibers, and the category of pairs \((E,R)\) of a locally free vector bundle \(E\) on \(S\) and an analytic subgroup \(R\) of \(E\) étale over \(S\).

13.1.5. When \(A\) is an "abeloid" group over \(S\), by which we mean that it is smooth, proper and separated over \(S\) and with connected fibers, so that we have in particular \(A=A^o\), then \(R\) is a locally constant group on \(S\), whose rank at each point \(s\) (as a \(\mathbb Z\)-module) is equal to \(2\operatorname{rang}_s(E)\), and conversely. In this way one finds the well-known interpretation of an abeloid space over \(S\) in terms of a "local system" \(R\) on \(S\) and of a structure of complex analytic vector bundle on the real analytic bundle \(R\otimes_{\mathbb Z}\mathbb R\) defined by \(R\).
13.2. Formal completion along a fiber.

We henceforth suppose given a point \(s\in S\), and shall work locally in the neighborhood of \(s\). For every integer \(n\geq0\), let us denote by \(S_n\) the \(n\)-th infinitesimal neighborhood of \(s\) in \(S\), and for every analytic space \(X\) over \(S\), let similarly \(X_n=X\times_S S_n\); for variable \(n\), the system of the \(X_n\) then forms an inductive system of analytic spaces over \(S\), which will be denoted by \(\widehat X\) and called the formal completion of \(X\) along \(X_s\). It obviously depends functorially on \(X\), and its formation commutes with finite projective limits; hence \(X\longmapsto\widehat X\) transforms groups into groups. Let us note that \(\widehat X\) in fact depends only on the restriction of \(X\) to an arbitrary open neighborhood \(U\) of \(s\).

blocks.title: Lemma 13.2.1.
blocks.html:
Lemma 13.2.1.

The data are those of 13.1, and we assume that the following conditions are satisfied: a) \(A=A^o\), b) \(R\) is constant in the neighborhood of \(s\), and c) \(R_s\) generates the complex vector space \(E_s\). Let \(A'\) be a second smooth analytic group over \(S\), and consider the map

\[u\longmapsto\widehat u:\operatorname{Hom}_{S\text{-gr}}(A,A') \longrightarrow\operatorname{Hom}_{S\text{-gr}}(\widehat A,\widehat {A'}),\]

where the notations are those of 13.2, and the analogous map obtained by restricting above a variable open neighborhood \(U\) of \(s\), and passing to the inductive limit:

\[(13.2.1.1)\qquad u\longmapsto\widehat u: \operatorname{Hom}_{(S,s)\text{-gr}}(A,A')\longrightarrow \operatorname{Hom}_{\widehat S\text{-gr}}(\widehat A,\widehat {A'}),\]

(where \((S,s)\) denotes the germ of analytic space of \(S\) at \(s\)). Then the preceding map is bijective.
Indeed, consider the exact sequence
\[0\longrightarrow R'\longrightarrow E'\longrightarrow {A'}^o\longrightarrow0\]

analogous to (13.1.3) defined by \(A'\). Taking into account the fact that the homomorphisms \(A\longrightarrow A'\) necessarily factor through \({A'}^o\), and likewise after the base changes \(U\longrightarrow S\) and \(S_n\longrightarrow S\), we may assume \(A'={A'}^o\). Taking into account the dictionary 13.1.4, we must prove that the datum of a germ of homomorphism of vector bundles \(E\longrightarrow E'\), mapping \(R\) into \(R'\), amounts to the datum of a coherent system of homomorphisms \(E_n\longrightarrow E'_n\) mapping \(R_n\) into \(R'_n\). Now if \(\mathcal O_s\) denotes the local ring of \(S\) at \(s\), the datum of a coherent system of homomorphisms \(E_n\longrightarrow E'_n\) amounts to the datum of a homomorphism of \(\widehat{\mathcal O}_s\)-modules
\[\widehat F:\widehat E_s\longrightarrow\widehat E'_s\]

(where \(E_s\) and \(E'_s\) are the free \(\mathcal O_s\)-modules fibers at \(s\) of the \(\mathcal O_S\)-Modules \(\underline E,\underline E'\) corresponding to \(E,E'\)), while a germ of homomorphism of vector bundles \(E\longrightarrow E'\) amounts to the datum of a homomorphism of \(\mathcal O_s\)-modules
\[F:E_s\longrightarrow E'_s.\]

This already shows that the map (13.2.1.1) is injective, and for the surjectivity, it suffices to prove that if we have a homomorphism \(\widehat F\) as above, such that for every \(n\) the induced homomorphism \(F_n:E_n\longrightarrow E'_n\) maps \(R_n\) into \(R'_n\), then \(\widehat F\) comes from an \(F\), i.e. maps \(E_s\) into \(E'_s\), and the corresponding germ of homomorphisms \(E\longrightarrow E'\) maps \(R\) into \(R'\).

Now, as by hypothesis c), \(R_s=R_o\) generates \(E_s\), it follows by Nakayama that the group \(R_o\) (identified with the group of sections of \(\widehat R\) over \(\widehat S\), or, as one prefers, with the group of germs of sections of \(R\) over \(S\)) generates \(\widehat E_s\); hence, to prove that \(\widehat F(E_s)\subset E'_s\), it suffices to prove that \(\widehat F(R_o)\subset E'_s\). Now we have \(\widehat F(R_o)\subset R'_o\), where \(R'_o\) is itself interpreted as the group of germs of sections of \(R'\subset E'\) in the neighborhood of \(s\), whence the announced inclusion, and consequently the existence of \(F\). Moreover we see that \(F(R_o)\subset R'_o\), where \(R_o\) and \(R'_o\) are considered as groups of germs of sections of \(E\) resp. \(E'\). By virtue of b), \(R_o\) generates \(R\) in the neighborhood of \(s\), whence it results that \(F(R)\subset R'\) in the neighborhood of \(s\), which completes the proof.

blocks.title: Corollary 13.2.2.
blocks.html:
Corollary 13.2.2.

If \(A\) satisfies conditions a), b), c) of 13.2.2, it is determined up to unique isomorphism in the neighborhood of \(s\) by the knowledge of the \(\widehat S\)-group \(\widehat A\).
13.3. The analytic RAYNAUD extension.

We are now in a position to paraphrase, in Analytic Geometry, the construction of the Raynaud extension given in 7. Suppose given \(A\) as in 13.1, with \(A=A_o\), and consider \(R_o=R_s\), which is a free \(\mathbb Z\)-module of finite type. After possibly restricting \(S\) to a small enough open neighborhood of \(s\), interpreting \(R_o\) as the group of germs of sections of \(R\) in the neighborhood of \(s\), we find a homomorphism
\[(13.3.1)\qquad R_{oS}\longrightarrow R\subset E\]

where \(R_{oS}\) denotes the constant analytic group on \(S\) with value \(R_o\). Choosing the neighborhood of \(s\) small enough so that every section of \(\mathcal O_S\) whose germ at \(s\) is zero is itself zero, we see that the preceding homomorphism is injective. Its image is thus a constant analytic subgroup of \(R\), which is obviously the largest constant subgroup of \(R\), and which is called the fixed part of \(R\), and is denoted \(R^f\):
\[(13.3.2)\qquad R_{oS}\mathrel{\sim}R^f\lhook\joinrel\longrightarrow R, \quad\text{induces an isomorphism }R^f_s\mathrel{\sim}R_s.\]
We may then consider the quotient group
\[(13.3.3)\qquad A^{\natural}=E/R^f\]
and we find a canonical exact sequence, taking (13.1.3) into account:
\[(13.3.4)\qquad0\longrightarrow R/R^f\mathop{\longrightarrow}^{i}A^{\natural} \longrightarrow A\longrightarrow0.\]

One will note that \(R^f\) is always closed in \(E\), provided that we restrict \(S\) to a neighborhood of \(s\), and that consequently \(A^{\natural}\) is separated over \(S\). One will note also that the homomorphism \(A^{\natural}\longrightarrow A\) is étale, hence induces an isomorphism
\[(13.3.5)\qquad \operatorname{Lie}(A^{\natural})\mathrel{\sim}\operatorname{Lie}(A)=E.\]

On the other hand, for every \(n\geq0\), the canonical homomorphism \(A^{\natural}\longrightarrow A\) induces an isomorphism \(A^{\natural}_n\mathrel{\sim}A_n\) (since \(R^f_n\mathrel{\sim}R_n\)), hence it induces an isomorphism
\[(13.3.6)\qquad \widehat{A^{\natural}}\mathrel{\sim}\widehat A.\]

This last fact shows that \(A^{\natural}\) indeed plays the role of the group, again denoted \(A^{\natural}\), that we had considered in § 7. It has been constructed here without making any hypothesis on the fiber \(A_s=A_o\), of the type "extension of an abeloid group by a torus", which will however be necessary if we want to recover the important extension structure (7.2.3) on \(A^{\natural}\). On the other hand, the homomorphism \(A^{\natural}\longrightarrow A\) of (13.3.4) is of an essentially transcendental nature and has no counterpart in the purely algebraic theory of § 7. We shall moreover return in § 14 to the question of a purely algebraic interpretation of the homomorphism \(i:R/R^f\longrightarrow A^{\natural}\) of (13.3.4).
Suppose first that we are given a complex subtorus
\[(13.3.7)\qquad T_o\subset A_o,\]
where by "complex torus" we mean an analytic group isomorphic to a cartesian power of \(\mathbb C^*\). Let
\[(13.3.8)\qquad T=T_o\times S\]
be the constant torus on \(S\) with value \(T_o\). Proceeding as in 5.1, we find a unique homomorphism (and even an immersion) of inductive systems of analytic groups
\[\widehat T\longrightarrow\widehat{A^{\natural}},\]
which above \(S_o\) reduces to (13.3.7). By virtue of 13.2.2, this homomorphism comes from a unique germ of homomorphisms
\[(13.3.9)\qquad T\longrightarrow A^{\natural},\]
and taking \(S\) small enough around \(s\), we may assume this homomorphism defined above all of \(S\). The corresponding homomorphism
\[\operatorname{Lie}(T)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Lie}(A^{\natural})\mathrel{\sim}E\]

is injective on the \(S_n\) hence on \(\widehat S\), hence on \(\mathcal O_s\) by flat descent, hence there exists a neighborhood of \(s\), which we may assume equal to \(S\), on which \(\operatorname{Lie}(T)\) is identified with a locally direct factor sub-Module of \(E\), which we shall denote \(E^t\); the corresponding subbundle of \(E\) is denoted \(E^t\):
\[(13.3.10)\qquad \operatorname{Lie}(T)\mathrel{\sim}E^t\subset E \mathrel{\sim}\operatorname{Lie}(A^{\natural})\mathrel{\sim}\operatorname{Lie}(A), \quad E^t\subset E.\]
Similarly, let us denote by
\[(13.3.11)\qquad R_o^t\subset R_o\quad\text{and}\quad R^t=R_{oS}^t\subset R_{oS}\mathrel{\sim}R^f\]
the sublattice of \(R_o\) defined by the subtorus \(T_o\) of \(A_o\), and the corresponding constant analytic group on \(S\), so that the homomorphism (13.3.9) corresponds simply to the homomorphism
\[E^t/R_{oS}^t=E^t/R^t\longrightarrow E/R_{oS}=E/R^f\]
induced by the inclusions
\[E^t\lhook\joinrel\longrightarrow E,\qquad R^t\lhook\joinrel\longrightarrow R^f.\]
Using the fact that (13.3.9) induces an immersion on the fibers at \(s\), we conclude that the homomorphism
\[R_o/R_o^t\longrightarrow E_o/E_o^t=(E/E^t)_o\]
is injective, whence it follows at once that
\[R'_o/{R'_o}^t\longrightarrow(E/E^t)_{s'}\]
is again injective for \(s'\) close to \(s\), which implies that, after possibly shrinking \(S\) around \(s\), we may assume that (13.3.9) is a closed immersion.
It is then natural to introduce
\[(13.3.12)\qquad B=A^{\natural}/T\mathrel{\sim}(E/E^t)/(R^f/R^t),\]
so that we obtain on \(A^{\natural}\) a structure of extension
\[(13.3.13)\qquad0\longrightarrow T\longrightarrow A^{\natural}\longrightarrow B\longrightarrow0,\]
\(B\) being a relative analytic group over \(S\), smooth and separated over \(S\) and with connected fibers. By the very construction, we thus have
\[(13.3.14)\qquad\operatorname{Lie}(B)\mathrel{\sim}E/E^t,\]
and the exponential exact sequence for \(B\)
\[(13.3.15)\qquad0\longrightarrow R^f/R^t\longrightarrow E/E^t\longrightarrow B\longrightarrow0.\]
One will note that
is a constant analytic group on \(S\). It therefore follows from 13.1.5 that \(B\) is an abeloid group over \(S\) in the neighborhood of \(s\) if and only if its fiber at \(s\)

is an abeloid analytic group, so that \(A_o\) then appears as an extension of such a group \(B_o\) by a torus \(T_o\). Every way of writing \(A_o\) in the form of an extension of an abeloid group by a complex torus (*) thus gives rise on \(A^{\natural}\), in the neighborhood of \(s\), to a unique structure of extension (13.3.13) of an abeloid group \(B\) by a torus \(T\), inducing the given extension structure at \(s\).
13.4. Interpretation in terms of DELIGNE's "mixed Hodge structures".

Let us first take up again the dictionary 13.1.4, which we are going to reformulate in a slightly different way, better adapted to the introduction of the "Hodge structures" pertinent to the situation. For this, let \(R\) be the sheaf of sections of \(R\) on \(S\), and let us introduce the \(\mathcal O_S\)-Module
\[(13.4.1)\qquad \mathcal E(A)=E=R\otimes_{\mathbb Z}\mathcal O_S,\]

which is locally free of finite type when \(R\) is locally constant. (NB The sheaf \(\mathcal E\) is not necessarily coherent if \(R\) is not locally constant.) The homomorphism (13.1.3) \(R\longrightarrow E\) then defines a canonical homomorphism
\[(13.4.2)\qquad \mathcal E=R\otimes_{\mathbb Z}\mathcal O_S\longrightarrow E,\]
whose kernel will be denoted \(F(A)\) or simply \(F\), so that we have an exact sequence
\[(13.4.3)\qquad0\longrightarrow F\longrightarrow\mathcal E\longrightarrow E.\]

The most interesting case is that where the homomorphism (13.4.2) is an epimorphism, i.e. where for every \(s\in S\), \(R_s\) generates the complex vector space \(E_s\). In this case we thus find an exact sequence
\[(13.4.4)\qquad0\longrightarrow F\longrightarrow\mathcal E\longrightarrow E\longrightarrow0,\]

and if \(R\) is locally constant, this is a short exact sequence of locally free Modules on \(S\). The knowledge of the pair \((E,R)\) is then equivalent to that of the pair \((R,F)\), where \(R\) is a locally constant analytic group on \(S\) whose fibers are free \(\mathbb Z\)-Modules of finite type, and \(F\) is a locally direct factor sub-Module of \(\mathcal E=R\otimes_{\mathbb Z}\mathcal O_S\), such that we have moreover the relation
From the point of view of Hodge structures, it is appropriate to regard \(\mathcal E\) as filtered by the "Hodge filtration"
\[(13.4.6)\qquad \operatorname{Fil}^1(\mathcal E)=\mathcal E,\quad \operatorname{Fil}^0(\mathcal E)=F,\quad \operatorname{Fil}^{-1}(\mathcal E)=0.\]
Let us note that the exact sequence (13.4.3) again depends functorially on the analytic group \(A\) over \(S\), and that it commutes with arbitrary base changes.
Returning then to the conditions of 13.3, we set
\[(13.4.7)\qquad \mathcal E^f=R^f\otimes_{\mathbb Z}\mathcal O_S,\qquad \mathcal E^t=R^t\otimes_{\mathbb Z}\mathcal O_S,\]
these Modules being defined in a suitable neighborhood of \(s\) (which we may assume equal to \(S\)), and thus define a filtration of \(\mathcal E\) (called the weight filtration)
\[(13.4.8)\qquad \mathcal E\supset\mathcal E^f\supset\mathcal E^t\supset0,\]

where it is appropriate to regard \(\mathcal E^t\), \(\mathcal E^f\) and \(\mathcal E\) respectively as of weights \(-2,-1\) and \(0\) (it is thus an increasing filtration). One will note that as \(R^t,\mathcal E^t\) depends on the choice of a subtorus \(T_o\) of \(A_s=A_o\), one will note that as \(R^f\) and \(R^t\) are locally constant, \(\mathcal E^f\) and \(\mathcal E^t\) are locally free, and that one can also interpret them as
\[(13.4.9)\qquad \mathcal E^f=\mathcal E(A^{\natural}),\qquad \mathcal E^t=\mathcal E(T).\]
The functoriality of the exact sequence (13.4.3) in variable \(A\), applied to the homomorphisms
\[T\longrightarrow A^{\natural}\longrightarrow A,\]
then shows us at once that \(F(A^{\natural})\) and \(F(T)\) are identified respectively with
\[(13.4.10)\qquad F(A^{\natural})\simeq F^f\overset{\mathrm{dfn}}=F\cap\mathcal E^f, \qquad F(T)=F^t=F\cap\mathcal E^t=0,\]

the \(=0\) coming from the immediate fact that for a torus \(T\), we have \(F(T)=0\), the exact sequence of type (13.4.3) becoming here \(0\to0\to\mathcal E(T)\to\operatorname{Lie}(T)\to0\). We thus see at the same time that the composite homomorphism
\[\mathcal E^t\longrightarrow\mathcal E\longrightarrow E\]
induces an isomorphism
\[(13.4.11)\qquad \mathcal E^t\xrightarrow{\sim}E^t\;(\simeq\operatorname{Lie}(T)).\]

Suppose now that \(R_s\) generates the complex vector space \(E_s\), which is the case in particular in the favorable case 13.3.16 where \(A_s\) is an extension of an abeloid group by a complex torus \(T_o\). Then, after possibly restricting \(S\) to a suitable neighborhood of \(s\), we find that the canonical homomorphism of type (13.4.2) (with \(A\) replaced by \(A^{\natural}\))
\[\mathcal E^f=\mathcal E(A^{\natural})\longrightarrow\operatorname{Lie}(A^{\natural})\simeq E\]
is an epimorphism, and a fortiori (13.4.2) is an epimorphism. We thus have exact sequences (13.4.4) and
\[(13.4.12)\qquad0\longrightarrow F^f\longrightarrow\mathcal E^f\longrightarrow E\longrightarrow0,\]
which shows in particular that we have
\[(13.4.13)\qquad F+\mathcal E^f=\mathcal E,\]

since \(F\cap\mathcal E^f\simeq F^f\). Moreover, as \(B=A^{\natural}/T\) is a quotient of \(A\), it satisfies the analogous surjectivity condition, i.e. gives rise to an exact sequence of type (13.4.4):
\[(13.4.14)\qquad0\longrightarrow F(B)\longrightarrow\mathcal E(B)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Lie}(B)\longrightarrow0,\]
whose interpretation is immediate in terms of the Modules already introduced. Indeed, we have by (13.3.12)
\[(13.4.15)\qquad \begin{cases} \mathcal E(B)\simeq(R^f/R^t)\otimes_{\mathbb Z}\mathcal O_S\simeq\mathcal E^f/\mathcal E^t,\\ \operatorname{Lie}(B)\simeq E/E^t, \end{cases}\]

whence we conclude, thanks to (13.4.11) and to the relation \(F\cap\mathcal E^t=0\) (13.4.10), that \(F(B)=\operatorname{Ker}(\mathcal E^f/\mathcal E^t\longrightarrow E/E^t)\) is isomorphic to \(F^f\) by the homomorphism
\[(13.4.16)\qquad F^f\xrightarrow{\sim}F(A^{\natural})\xrightarrow{\sim}F(B).\]

Let us then recapitulate the properties obtained for the "weight" filtration (13.4.8), and its relations with the "Hodge filtration" (13.4.6) of \(\mathcal E\) defined by the sub-Module \(F\) of \(\mathcal E\), in the favorable case 13.3.16 where \(A_s\) is an extension of an abeloid analytic group \(B_o\) by a torus \(T_o\) (which will be used to define \(R^t\), so that \(B\) is an abeloid analytic group over \(S\)):

Proposition 13.4.17. Under the preceding conditions, the "Hodge" and "weight" filtrations on \(\mathcal E=R\otimes_{\mathbb Z}\mathcal O_S\):
\[(13.4.17.1)\qquad \mathcal E\supset F,\qquad \mathcal E\supset\mathcal E^f\supset\mathcal E^t\supset0\]

satisfy the following conditions:

a) The weight filtration comes, by tensoring \(\otimes_{\mathbb Z}\mathcal O_S\), from an analogous filtration on \(R\)

\[R\supset R^f\supset R^t\supset0,\]

where \(R^f\) and \(R^t\) are locally constant analytic groups on \(S\), with values free \(\mathbb Z\)-modules of finite type (namely \(R_o^f=R_s=\pi_1(A_s)\) and \(R_o^t=\pi_1(T_o)\)).

b) We have the relations

\[(*)\qquad F\cap\mathcal E^t=0,\qquad F+\mathcal E^f=\mathcal E.\]

In other words, the filtration of \(\mathcal E^t\) induced by the Hodge filtration (with the degree conventions (13.4.6)) is concentrated in degree \(-1\), that of \(\mathcal E/\mathcal E^f\) induced by the Hodge filtration is concentrated in degree \(0\).

c) Consider the filtration of

\[\mathcal E^{ab}\overset{\mathrm{dfn}}=\mathcal E^f/\mathcal E^t\simeq R^{ab}\otimes_{\mathbb Z}\mathcal O_S, \qquad\text{where }R^{ab}=R^f/R^t\quad\text{(cf. a)),}\]

induced by the Hodge filtration of \(\mathcal E\), hence defined by the sub-Module \(F^{ab}\) of \(\mathcal E^{ab}\) the image of

\[F^f\overset{\mathrm{dfn}}=F\cap\mathcal E^f\longrightarrow\mathcal E^{ab},\]

(the arrow being a monomorphism thanks to the first relation (*)). Then the pair \((R^{ab},F^{ab}\subset\mathcal E^{ab}\simeq R^{ab}\otimes_{\mathbb Z}\mathcal O_S)\) is isomorphic to the pair \((R(B),F(B))\) associated to an abeloid group over \(S\), as explained after (13.4.4) (where thus \(B\simeq\mathcal E^{ab}/(F^{ab}+R^{ab})\), the non-underlined symbols denoting the corresponding analytic groups over \(S\)).

Remark 13.4.18. In the terminology of DELIGNE [4], the conclusion of the preceding proposition, when we restrict above an open set \(U\) of \(S\) on which \(R\) is locally constant, expresses that \(R\), equipped with the two filtrations (13.4.17.1) of \(\mathcal E=R\otimes_{\mathbb Z}\mathcal O_S\), is an "analytic family of mixed Hodge structures on \(U\)". The situation just made explicit served as a model for DELIGNE's conjectures on Néron theory in higher dimensions, stated in [9, 9.8 to 9.13]. There is however an important feature of the algebraic theory studied in § 7 which is missing in the description 13.4.17, when we assume that \(A\) is an abeloid group above an open set \(U\) complementary to a nowhere dense analytic subset \(Z\) of \(S\) (say \(Z=\{s\}\), when \(\dim S=1\)), so that (13.1.5) \(R|U\) is a local system on \(U\): namely that the local system \(R/R^t\) on \(U\) should be constant, and more precisely, that it should be dual to the local system \({R'}^f\), where \(R'\) is the analytic group étale over \(S\) which would correspond (as \(R\) to \(A\)) to a suitable analytic group \(A'\) over \(S\), such that \(A'|U\) is the abeloid group "dual" to \(A|U\). (The algebraic analogue of this statement, with the notations of § 5, is the duality of \(T_\ell(A_K)/T_\ell(A_K)^t\) with \(T_\ell(A'_K)^f\) (5.5.9) resulting from the duality theorem 5.2.) I do not know whether such a statement is valid, under the sole hypotheses of 13.4.17, nor even whether the action of \(\pi_1(U,t)\) on \(R_t\) is unipotent (\(t\in U\)); and it does not seem that the naive methods used in the present section make it possible to prove such a result, even under the supplementary hypothesis where we assume that \(A|U\) is equipped with a "polarization", i.e. an ample invertible sheaf on each fiber. This is however what the "semi-simple group people" know how to prove, it seems, at least for \(S\) non-singular of dimension 1, cf. the report [9]. In the following subsection, we are going to show that everything works well by making hypotheses of relative algebraicity on \(A\), and at the same time make the link with the construction of § 7.
13.5. Case of a relatively algebraic situation.

We now assume that \(A\) comes from a relative group scheme over \(S\), smooth, separated and whose fibers are extensions of abelian schemes by tori. We shall denote this relative scheme by the same letter \(A\). (We allow ourselves this abuse of notation thanks to the fact that the functor \(G\mapsto G^{\mathrm{an}}\), which goes from the category of relative group schemes of the type considered to the category of analytic groups over \(S\), is fully faithful, as would be easy to establish thanks to the results of relative GAGA type [10].) For everything concerning the notion of relative scheme, and the relations between relative schemes over \(S\) and analytic spaces over \(S\), we refer to [10] (cf. also [2, Exp. 15]). If \(O\) denotes the local ring of \(S\) at \(s\), the relative scheme \(A\) is known in the neighborhood of \(s\) by the knowledge of the ordinary scheme \(A_O\) over \(\operatorname{Spec}(O)\), deduced from \(A\) by localization at \(s\). It is then clear that the formal completion of \(A_O\) along its special fiber (isomorphic to \(A_s=A_o\)) is isomorphic to the formal completion of the analytic group \(A\) along \(A_s\) (13.2), identifying the \(A_n=A\times_S S_n\) with algebraic schemes over \(S\), by the abuse of notation introduced above. In the favorable case where \(\widehat A\), regarded as a formal group scheme over \(\widehat O\), is algebraizable (7.2.1), which is the case in particular if \(S\) is normal at \(s\) and \(A\) quasi-projective over \(S\) (7.1.5), we thus obtain a group scheme \(\widehat A_O^{\natural}\) over \(\operatorname{Spec}(\widehat O)\), an extension of an abelian scheme \(\widehat B_O\) by a torus. To simplify, we assume that \(\widehat B_O\) is projective over \(\widehat O\) (which is indeed the case, as is known, if \(O\) hence \(\widehat O\) is normal). We are then going to prove:

Proposition 13.5.1. Under the preceding conditions, the analytic group \(A^{\natural}\) of (13.3.3) comes, in the neighborhood of \(s\), from a relative group scheme determined up to unique isomorphism, an extension of a relative abelian scheme projective over \(S\) by a torus, and if \(A_O^{\natural}\) is the group scheme it defines over \(O=O_{S,s}\), then the group scheme \(A_O^{\natural}\otimes_O\widehat O\) is canonically isomorphic, with its extension structure, to the Raynaud extension \(\widehat A_O^{\natural}\) introduced previously.

This last fact will follow immediately from the first assertion, taking 7.2.1 into account, since \(A_O^{\natural}\otimes_O\widehat O\) has as formal completion along the special fiber \(\widehat A_O^{\natural}\), by virtue of (13.3.6). To prove the algebraicity of \(A^{\natural}\) in the neighborhood of \(s\), proceeding as in 7.2.1, we are reduced at once to proving that of the abelian quotient \(B\) of \(A^{\natural}\), which will follow from the

Lemma 13.5.2. Let \(B\) be an abeloid analytic group over the analytic space \(S\), \(s\) a point of \(S\) such that the completion \(\widehat B\) along the fiber \(B_s\) is algebraizable, i.e. such that the \(B_n=B\times_S S_n\) are algebraizable, and that \(\widehat B\), now regarded as a formal scheme over \(\widehat O_{S,s}\), is algebraizable, i.e. comes from an abelian scheme \(\widehat B_O\) over \(\widehat O\). Assume moreover that \(\widehat B_O\) is projective over \(\widehat O\). Then \(B\) is projective over \(S\) in the neighborhood of \(s\).

The hypothesis means that there exists a polarization of the formal analytic space \(\widehat B\), i.e. a sequence of polarizations \(p_n\) of the \(B_n\) which "glue". The conclusion means that there exists a polarization \(p\) of \(B\) above a neighborhood of \(s\). Now let \(P\) be the analytic space over \(S\) of relative polarizations of \(B\) over \(S\), which represents the functor
\[S'\longmapsto\text{set of polarizations of }B_{S'}\text{ over }S'\]

on the category of analytic spaces \(S'\) over \(S\), which is an open subfunctor of the Néron-Severi functor \(\operatorname{NS}_{B/S}\) of \(S\). Standard arguments of the simplest kind, which we dispense with making explicit here, show that \(\operatorname{NS}_{B/S}\), and consequently also \(P\), is representable by an analytic space net (i.e. unramified) over \(S\). The hypothesis is then expressed by saying that there exists a formal section \(p\) of \(P\) over \(S\) at \(s\). As \(P\) is unramified hence locally quasi-finite over \(S\), it follows [2, Exp. 18, n°1] that every formal section of \(P\) over \(S\) at \(s\) comes from an analytic section in the neighborhood of \(s\), i.e. from a polarization of \(B\) above a neighborhood of \(s\), whence the announced conclusion.

Remarks 13.5.3. a) If we omit in 13.5.2 the hypothesis that \(\widehat B_O\) is projective over \(S\), we can still conclude that \(B\) is algebraizable in the neighborhood of \(s\). For this one uses an unpublished result (due independently to M. ARTIN and P. DELIGNE, using the theory of the sheaves \(\underline T^i\) of VI 2), asserting that if \(X\) proper over \(S\) is such that \(X_{\mathrm{réd}}\) is algebraizable, the same is true of \(X\). This reduces us to the case where \(S\) is reduced. On the other hand, when \(S\) is normal, hence \(\widehat O\) is normal, it is known that every abelian scheme over \(\widehat O\) is projective over \(\widehat O\) (cf. for example [23]), and 13.5.2 applies. To obtain the case of an arbitrary reduced \(S\), one proceeds by non-flat descent from the normalization \(S'\) of \(S\), thanks to the following (delicate) result, which we shall not prove here: a mor-

Unpublished results of M. RAYNAUD imply moreover that this condition is always satisfied.
i.e. which are subobjects as projective systems, i.e. term by term.
The case \(\ell\ne p\) will be studied in §§ 5 and 6 below.
This is essentially the case \(i=1\) of the said conjectures for the \(H^i\).
It would in fact suffice that \(k\) be <u>perfect</u>, but by passage to the strict henselization of \(S\), we reduce at once to the case considered.

It is also appropriate to define the <u>tame conductor exponent</u> of \(A_K\) with respect to \(V\) as the integer difference of the lengths over \(\mathbb Z/\ell\mathbb Z\) of the generic and special geometric fibers of \({}_\ell A^o\), which is independent of \(\ell\) and equal to \(2\lambda+\mu\) with the notations of (1.1.11); the <u>(total) conductor exponent</u> of \(A_K\) is defined as the sum of the two preceding integers; it is the local term which appears in the Euler-Poincaré formula for \({}_\ell A^o\) of SGA 5 X 6.2, [20].

As follows from an example communicated to me by J. TATE, 4.7 does not remain valid when \(n\) is equal to the residue characteristic \(p\), even if \(K\) is of characteristic zero and \(A_K\) is an elliptic curve with complex multiplication.
This hypothesis is moreover not essential, as is shown in 6.1 below.
This hypothesis is doubtless unnecessary, cf. 5.11.
